Key Themes for FCPS/MRCP

  • Mnemonics: SIG E CAPS (depression), DIG FAST (mania), 4 A's (capacity), SAD PERSONS (suicide risk)
  • Investigations: MSE, capacity assessment, risk assessment
  • Pharmacology: SSRIs/SNRIs first-line; lithium for bipolar; clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia
  • Emergencies: NMS, serotonin syndrome, rapid tranquilisation
  • Legal: MHA, MCA, capacity, consent
  • Special populations: perinatal, elderly, adolescent, medically ill

▸ 29.0.900 Chapter Moc
Document Type: MOC (Map of Content) (Chapter-Level Overview)
Davidson Alignment: Chapter 29: Medical Psychiatry
Chapter Number: 29
Target Exams: FCPS, MRCP Part 1, MRCP Part 2, PACES
Style: Sea Knowledge comprehensive integrated note with MCQs/SBAs/Flashcards/Tracker
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep
This is the Master Overview for Chapter 29: Medical Psychiatry. It provides the big-picture roadmap — study priorities, key algorithms, viva topics, cross-references to all atomic topics, and progress tracking. Read this FIRST, then drill into atomic topics using the hierarchy file.

1. Study Plan & Prioritisation

This chapter contains 78 atomic topics organised into hubs. Below is the recommended study priority for Medical Psychiatry.

Tier 1 — Core High-Yield (Master first — covers ~80% of exam marks)

  • Definitions, classifications, and severity criteria
  • First-line investigation algorithms
  • First-line management and emergency protocols
  • Common clinical syndromes and bedside recognition

Tier 2 — High-Probability (Solidify second — ~15% of exam marks)

  • Scoring systems and risk stratification
  • Differential diagnoses and mimics
  • Pharmacology: indications, contraindications, doses
  • Device and procedural indications

Tier 3 — Differentiators (Polishing — ~5% of exam marks)

  • Rare conditions and eponymous syndromes
  • Pathophysiology deep-dive
  • Latest trial evidence and guidelines
  • Genetic and molecular mechanisms

Method: Master Tier 1 → Read all atomic topics in order → Drill MCQs/SBAs → Use spaced repetition tracker below.

2. Original Medicine Note Content (Preserved)

The following is preserved from the Medicine source Medical Psychiatry MOC.md. It contains the author-curated study plans, scoring systems, viva topics, and progress trackers from the original source.

Chapter Scope Note

  • This folder is organized as chapter > heading hub > disease-level file.
  • Only topics belonging to Davidson Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry are scaffolded here.
  • Covers liaison psychiatry, common psychiatric disorders, substance use, psychological therapies, psychopharmacology, legal aspects, and special populations.

  • Heading hubs: 19
  • Topic-group hubs: 19 (one per heading hub in this chapter)

  • Full-note completion: 78 / 78 first-pass full notes completed

  • Investigations: MSE, capacity assessment, risk assessment
  • Pharmacology: SSRIs/SNRIs first-line; lithium for bipolar; clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia
  • Emergencies: NMS, serotonin syndrome, rapid tranquilisation

7. Flashcards — Active Recall (Chapter-Specific)

Cover the answer, try to recall, then reveal. These flashcards cover the most frequently tested Medical Psychiatry facts.

8. Spaced Repetition Tracker

▸ 29.0.901 Chapter Hierarchy
Document Type: Topic Hierarchy (Davidson-aligned) (Chapter-Level Overview)
Davidson Alignment: Chapter 29: Medical Psychiatry
Chapter Number: 29
Target Exams: FCPS, MRCP Part 1, MRCP Part 2, PACES
Style: Sea Knowledge comprehensive integrated note with MCQs/SBAs/Flashcards/Tracker
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep
This is the Topic Hierarchy for Chapter 29: Medical Psychiatry. It maps every atomic topic to its parent hub and Davidson section. Use this as your navigation index — every link points to the corresponding topic.md file in the MedSea repository.

12. Metadata & Tracking
▸ 29.1 Anxiety and Stress-Related Disorders 7
▸ 29.1.1 Adjustment Disorder And Acute Stress Reaction

Definition

Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive reaction to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that do not meet criteria for another mental disorder and resolve within 6 months. Acute stress reaction (formerly acute stress disorder in DSM-IV) is a transient disturbance following trauma, lasting hours to days.

Core Clinical Content

Adjustment disorder criteria

Symptoms within 3 months of stressor; not meeting criteria for another disorder; resolves within 6 months

Adjustment disorder subtypes

Depressed mood, anxiety, mixed, disturbance of conduct, mixed disturbance

Adjustment disorder prevalence

Common; associated with life events (bereavement, divorce, illness, job loss)

Adjustment disorder treatment

Brief psychotherapy (CBT, supportive); usually self-limiting

Adjustment disorder vs bereavement

Bereavement: normal grief, not disorder; AD: symptoms disproportionate, impairs function

Adjustment disorder vs MDD

MDD: meets full criteria; AD: subsyndromal, tied to stressor

Acute stress reaction (ICD-11)

Transient; days; mixed anxiety-depression; dissociative symptoms

Acute stress disorder (DSM-5)

≥3 days to 1 month; trauma-related; similar to PTSD but shorter

Risk of PTSD

Acute stress disorder 50% progress to PTSD

Treatment

Brief CBT; watchful waiting; trauma-focused therapy if persistent

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Adjustment disorder criteriaSymptoms within 3 months of stressor; not meeting criteria for another disorder;
Adjustment disorder subtypesDepressed mood, anxiety, mixed, disturbance of conduct, mixed disturbance
Adjustment disorder prevalenceCommon; associated with life events (bereavement, divorce, illness, job loss)
Adjustment disorder treatmentBrief psychotherapy (CBT, supportive); usually self-limiting
Adjustment disorder vs bereavementBereavement: normal grief, not disorder; AD: symptoms disproportionate, impairs
Adjustment disorder vs MDDMDD: meets full criteria; AD: subsyndromal, tied to stressor
Acute stress reaction (ICD-11)Transient; days; mixed anxiety-depression; dissociative symptoms
Acute stress disorder (DSM-5)≥3 days to 1 month; trauma-related; similar to PTSD but shorter
Risk of PTSDAcute stress disorder 50% progress to PTSD
TreatmentBrief CBT; watchful waiting; trauma-focused therapy if persistent

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive reaction to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that do not meet criteria for another mental disorder and resolve within 6 months. Acute stress reaction (formerly acute stress disorder in DSM-IV) is a transient disturbance following trauma, lasting hours to days.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Key features include: adjustment disorder criteria, adjustment disorder subtypes, adjustment disorder prevalence.

Q3. How is Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Mind Map

Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Features
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute
│   └── Long-term
└── Complications

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentStandard workup
3. AcuteSpecific intervention
4. Long-termMaintenance plan
5. MonitorFollow-up and review

D) Brief psychotherapy (CBT, supportive); usually self-limiting

  1. Patient presents with features of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction. Which is most appropriate? | ≥3 days to 1 month; trauma-related; similar to PTSD but shorter
  1. Patient presents with features of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction. Which is most appropriate? | Acute stress disorder 50% progress to PTSD
  1. Patient presents with features of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction. Which is most appropriate? | Brief CBT; watchful waiting; trauma-focused therapy if persistent

Flashcards

Q: Adjustment disorder criteria?

A: Symptoms within 3 months of stressor; not meeting criteria for another disorder; resolves within 6 m

Q: Adjustment disorder subtypes?

A: Depressed mood, anxiety, mixed, disturbance of conduct, mixed disturbance

Q: Adjustment disorder prevalence?

A: Common; associated with life events (bereavement, divorce, illness, job loss)

Q: Adjustment disorder treatment?

A: Brief psychotherapy (CBT, supportive); usually self-limiting

Q: Adjustment disorder vs bereavement?

A: Bereavement: normal grief, not disorder; AD: symptoms disproportionate, impairs function

Q: Adjustment disorder vs MDD?

A: MDD: meets full criteria; AD: subsyndromal, tied to stressor

Q: Acute stress reaction (ICD-11)?

A: Transient; days; mixed anxiety-depression; dissociative symptoms

Q: Acute stress disorder (DSM-5)?

A: ≥3 days to 1 month; trauma-related; similar to PTSD but shorter

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.1.2 Generalised Anxiety Disorder Gad

Definition

Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterised by excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple events/activities for at least 6 months, accompanied by physical symptoms of anxiety (restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance).

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Excessive anxiety/worry ≥6 months, ≥3 of 6 associated symptoms

Associated symptoms

Restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance

Prevalence

Lifetime ~5-6%; 2x more common in women; median onset 30 years

Comorbidity

Depression (60%), other anxiety disorders, substance use

First-line treatment

SSRI/SNRI + CBT

Short-term anxiolytic

Benzodiazepine (max 2-4 weeks) for severe acute anxiety

Pregabalin

Alternative to benzodiazepine; reduced abuse potential

Buspirone

5-HT1A partial agonist; non-sedating, no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks

Cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT)

As effective as medication; lasting benefits

Course

Chronic, fluctuating; tends to improve with age

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteriaExcessive anxiety/worry ≥6 months, ≥3 of 6 associated symptoms
Associated symptomsRestlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, s
PrevalenceLifetime ~5-6%; 2x more common in women; median onset 30 years
ComorbidityDepression (60%), other anxiety disorders, substance use
First-line treatmentSSRI/SNRI + CBT
Short-term anxiolyticBenzodiazepine (max 2-4 weeks) for severe acute anxiety
PregabalinAlternative to benzodiazepine; reduced abuse potential
Buspirone5-HT1A partial agonist; non-sedating, no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks
Cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT)As effective as medication; lasting benefits
CourseChronic, fluctuating; tends to improve with age

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterised by excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple events/activities for at least 6 months, accompanied by physical symptoms of anxiety (restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, associated symptoms, prevalence. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

D) Alternative to benzodiazepine; reduced abuse potential

Answer: A

A) Restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, s

C) Benzodiazepine (max 2-4 weeks) for severe acute anxiety

A) Lifetime ~5-6%; 2x more common in women; median onset 30 years

B) SSRI/SNRI + CBT

C) Alternative to benzodiazepine; reduced abuse potential

D) As effective as medication; lasting benefits

▸ 29.1.3 Hoarding Disorder And Body Dysmorphic Disorder

Definition

Hoarding disorder is characterised by persistent difficulty discarding or parting with possessions regardless of value, due to perceived need to save and distress associated with discarding. Body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) is preoccupation with perceived defects in physical appearance that are not observable or appear slight, causing significant distress.

Core Clinical Content

Hoarding disorder DSM-5

Persistent difficulty discarding possessions; cluttered living space; distress/impairment

Hoarding prevalence

~2-6%; increases with age; more common in older adults

Hoarding vs OCD

Hoarding: ego-syntonic, no obsessions; OCD: ego-dystonic, obsessions present

Hoarding vs collecting

Collectors: organised, valued, displayed; hoarders: cluttered, distress, impairment

Hoarding treatment

CBT (most evidence-based); decluttering; motivational interviewing; SSRIs sometimes

BDD definition

Preoccupation with perceived physical defects not visible to others; significant distress

BDD prevalence

~2%; often begins in adolescence; equal M:F

BDD common concerns

Skin, hair, nose, body shape, symmetry; mirror checking, camouflaging

BDD comorbidity

Depression (60-80%), OCD (30%), social anxiety, eating disorders

BDD treatment

SSRI (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine); CBT (ERP + cognitive restructuring); clomipramine

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Hoarding disorder DSM-5Persistent difficulty discarding possessions; cluttered living space; distress/i
Hoarding prevalence~2-6%; increases with age; more common in older adults
Hoarding vs OCDHoarding: ego-syntonic, no obsessions; OCD: ego-dystonic, obsessions present
Hoarding vs collectingCollectors: organised, valued, displayed; hoarders: cluttered, distress, impairm
Hoarding treatmentCBT (most evidence-based); decluttering; motivational interviewing; SSRIs someti
BDD definitionPreoccupation with perceived physical defects not visible to others; significant
BDD prevalence~2%; often begins in adolescence; equal M:F
BDD common concernsSkin, hair, nose, body shape, symmetry; mirror checking, camouflaging
BDD comorbidityDepression (60-80%), OCD (30%), social anxiety, eating disorders
BDD treatmentSSRI (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine); CBT (ERP + cognitive restructuring); clomipramin

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Hoarding disorder is characterised by persistent difficulty discarding or parting with possessions regardless of value, due to perceived need to save and distress associated with discarding. Body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) is preoccupation with perceived defects in physical appearance that are not observable or appear slight, causing significant distress.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Key features include: hoarding disorder dsm-5, hoarding prevalence, hoarding vs ocd.

Q3. How is Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.1.4 Obsessive Compulsive Disorder Ocd

Definition

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is characterised by obsessions (recurrent, intrusive, distressing thoughts/images) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviours or mental acts performed to reduce anxiety), causing significant impairment.

Core Clinical Content

Obsessions

Recurrent, intrusive, unwanted thoughts/images; recognised as own (ego-dystonic)

Compulsions

Repetitive behaviours or mental acts; aimed at reducing anxiety

Common obsessions

Contamination, harm, symmetry, sexual, religious, somatic

Common compulsions

Washing, checking, counting, ordering, repeating, mental rituals

Insight

Usually preserved (ego-dystonic)

Prevalence

Lifetime ~2-3%; equal M:F; onset usually in adolescence/young adulthood

Comorbidity

Depression (60%), tic disorders, anxiety, OCPD (different condition)

First-line treatment

SSRI (higher dose than for depression) + CBT (ERP — exposure and response prevention)

SSRI dose

Often need higher doses (e.g., fluoxetine 60-80 mg) for OCD

Onset of action

Slower than for depression (8-12 weeks for full effect)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ObsessionsRecurrent, intrusive, unwanted thoughts/images; recognised as own (ego-dystonic)
CompulsionsRepetitive behaviours or mental acts; aimed at reducing anxiety
Common obsessionsContamination, harm, symmetry, sexual, religious, somatic
Common compulsionsWashing, checking, counting, ordering, repeating, mental rituals
InsightUsually preserved (ego-dystonic)
PrevalenceLifetime ~2-3%; equal M:F; onset usually in adolescence/young adulthood
ComorbidityDepression (60%), tic disorders, anxiety, OCPD (different condition)
First-line treatmentSSRI (higher dose than for depression) + CBT (ERP — exposure and response preven
SSRI doseOften need higher doses (e.g., fluoxetine 60-80 mg) for OCD
Onset of actionSlower than for depression (8-12 weeks for full effect)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is characterised by obsessions (recurrent, intrusive, distressing thoughts/images) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviours or mental acts performed to reduce anxiety), causing significant impairment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

Key features include: obsessions, compulsions, common obsessions. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)

├── Clinical Features

▸ 29.1.5 Panic Disorder And Panic Attacks

Definition

Panic disorder is characterised by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks (discrete episodes of intense fear with physical/cognitive symptoms) followed by persistent concern about further attacks, leading to behavioural change (agoraphobia).

Core Clinical Content

Panic attack

Discrete period of intense fear, ≥4 of 13 symptoms, peaks within minutes

Panic attack symptoms

Palpitations, sweating, trembling, dyspnoea, choking, chest pain, nausea, dizziness, derealisation, paraesthesia, chills, fear of dying/losing control

Duration

Typically 5-30 min, peaks within 10 min

Panic disorder

Recurrent unexpected attacks + persistent concern (≥1 month)

Agoraphobia

Fear of places where escape might be difficult; often develops with panic

Differential

MI, PE, hyperthyroidism, arrhythmias, phaeochromocytoma

First-line treatment

SSRI + CBT (exposure therapy)

Acute treatment

Benzodiazepine (short-acting) for acute attack

Hyperventilation

Often present; breathing exercises can help

Prognosis

Chronic, fluctuating; good response to treatment; 50-70% recover with treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Panic attackDiscrete period of intense fear, ≥4 of 13 symptoms, peaks within minutes
Panic attack symptomsPalpitations, sweating, trembling, dyspnoea, choking, chest pain, nausea, dizzin
DurationTypically 5-30 min, peaks within 10 min
Panic disorderRecurrent unexpected attacks + persistent concern (≥1 month)
AgoraphobiaFear of places where escape might be difficult; often develops with panic
DifferentialMI, PE, hyperthyroidism, arrhythmias, phaeochromocytoma
First-line treatmentSSRI + CBT (exposure therapy)
Acute treatmentBenzodiazepine (short-acting) for acute attack
HyperventilationOften present; breathing exercises can help
PrognosisChronic, fluctuating; good response to treatment; 50-70% recover with treatment

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Panic disorder and panic attacks?

Panic disorder is characterised by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks (discrete episodes of intense fear with physical/cognitive symptoms) followed by persistent concern about further attacks, leading to behavioural change (agoraphobia).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Panic disorder and panic attacks?

Key features include: panic attack, panic attack symptoms, duration. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Panic disorder and panic attacks diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Panic disorder and panic attacks?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Panic disorder and panic attacks?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Panic disorder and panic attacks — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.1.6 Phobias Specific Social Agoraphobia

Definition

Phobias are anxiety disorders characterised by excessive, persistent, irrational fear of a specific object, situation, or activity, leading to avoidance and significant functional impairment.

Core Clinical Content

Specific phobia

Fear of specific object/animal/situation (spiders, heights, blood, flying)

Types of specific phobia

Animal, natural environment, blood-injection-injury, situational, other

Social anxiety disorder

Fear of social situations where scrutiny is possible; performance vs generalised

Agoraphobia

Fear of ≥2 of: public transport, open spaces, enclosed places, queues, crowds, going out alone

Prevalence

Specific phobia 7-9%, social anxiety 7%, agoraphobia 1.7%

Treatment of specific phobia

Exposure therapy (gold standard); CBT; SSRI/SNRI for severe

Treatment of social anxiety

CBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)

Beta-blockers

For performance-only social anxiety (propranolol before event)

Course

Usually chronic if untreated; specific phobias often start in childhood

Insight

Usually preserved; patient recognises fear as excessive

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Specific phobiaFear of specific object/animal/situation (spiders, heights, blood, flying)
Types of specific phobiaAnimal, natural environment, blood-injection-injury, situational, other
Social anxiety disorderFear of social situations where scrutiny is possible; performance vs generalised
AgoraphobiaFear of ≥2 of: public transport, open spaces, enclosed places, queues, crowds, g
PrevalenceSpecific phobia 7-9%, social anxiety 7%, agoraphobia 1.7%
Treatment of specific phobiaExposure therapy (gold standard); CBT; SSRI/SNRI for severe
Treatment of social anxietyCBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)
Beta-blockersFor performance-only social anxiety (propranolol before event)
CourseUsually chronic if untreated; specific phobias often start in childhood
InsightUsually preserved; patient recognises fear as excessive

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

Phobias are anxiety disorders characterised by excessive, persistent, irrational fear of a specific object, situation, or activity, leading to avoidance and significant functional impairment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

Key features include: specific phobia, types of specific phobia, social anxiety disorder. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

A) Specific phobia 7-9%, social anxiety 7%, agoraphobia 1.7%

B) Exposure therapy (gold standard); CBT; SSRI/SNRI for severe

C) CBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)

D) For performance-only social anxiety (propranolol before event)

Answer: A

  1. Prognosis of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

A) Exposure therapy (gold standard); CBT; SSRI/SNRI for severe

B) CBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)

C) For performance-only social anxiety (propranolol before event)

D) Usually chronic if untreated; specific phobias often start in childhood

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

A) CBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)

B) For performance-only social anxiety (propranolol before event)

C) Usually chronic if untreated; specific phobias often start in childhood

D) Usually preserved; patient recognises fear as excessive

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

A) Fear of specific object/animal/situation (spiders, heights, blood, flying)

B) Fear of social situations where scrutiny is possible; performance vs generalised

C) Specific phobia 7-9%, social anxiety 7%, agoraphobia 1.7%

D) CBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

A) Animal, natural environment, blood-injection-injury, situational, other

B) Fear of ≥2 of: public transport, open spaces, enclosed places, queues, crowds, g

C) Exposure therapy (gold standard); CBT; SSRI/SNRI for severe

D) For performance-only social anxiety (propranolol before event)

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)?

A) Fear of social situations where scrutiny is possible; performance vs generalised

B) Specific phobia 7-9%, social anxiety 7%, agoraphobia 1.7%

C) CBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)

D) Usually chronic if untreated; specific phobias often start in childhood

Answer: C

SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient presents with features of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia). Most appropriate first-line? | Fear of specific object/animal/situation (spiders, heights, blood, flying)
  1. Which investigation is most useful in Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  1. What is the prevalence of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Animal, natural environment, blood-injection-injury, situational, other
  1. First-line management of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Fear of specific object/animal/situation (spiders, heights, blood, flying)
  1. Which is a recognised risk factor for Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Fear of social situations where scrutiny is possible; performance vs generalised
  1. What is the typical course of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  1. Which complication should be screened for in Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  1. Duration criterion for Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Fear of ≥2 of: public transport, open spaces, enclosed places, queues, crowds, g
  1. Most appropriate referral for Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia)? | Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  1. Differential diagnosis of Phobias (specific, social, agoraphobia) includes? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

Flashcards

Q: Specific phobia?

A: Fear of specific object/animal/situation (spiders, heights, blood, flying)

Q: Types of specific phobia?

A: Animal, natural environment, blood-injection-injury, situational, other

Q: Social anxiety disorder?

A: Fear of social situations where scrutiny is possible; performance vs generalised

Q: Agoraphobia?

A: Fear of ≥2 of: public transport, open spaces, enclosed places, queues, crowds, going out alone

Q: Prevalence?

A: Specific phobia 7-9%, social anxiety 7%, agoraphobia 1.7%

Q: Treatment of specific phobia?

A: Exposure therapy (gold standard); CBT; SSRI/SNRI for severe

Q: Treatment of social anxiety?

A: CBT (exposure), SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine)

Q: Beta-blockers?

A: For performance-only social anxiety (propranolol before event)

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.1.7 Post Traumatic Stress Disorder Ptsd

Definition

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) develops after exposure to a traumatic event, characterised by re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, and hyperarousal, lasting >1 month.

Core Clinical Content

Trauma criteria

Exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence

DSM-5 symptom clusters

Intrusion (re-experiencing), Avoidance, Negative alterations in cognition/mood, Hyperarousal

Duration

≥1 month (if <3 months = acute; >3 months = chronic)

Complex PTSD

PTSD + affect dysregulation, negative self-concept, relationship difficulties

Risk factors

Severity of trauma, prior trauma, lack of support, female sex

First-line trauma-focused therapy

TF-CBT, EMDR (eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing)

First-line medication

SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine) — FDA approved

Prazosin

For trauma-related nightmares (off-label)

Acute stress disorder

Lasts 3 days to 1 month; same symptoms as PTSD; if >1 month → PTSD

Adjustment disorder

Milder, more common, develops within 3 months of stressor

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Trauma criteriaExposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence
DSM-5 symptom clustersIntrusion (re-experiencing), Avoidance, Negative alterations in cognition/mood,
Duration≥1 month (if <3 months = acute; >3 months = chronic)
Complex PTSDPTSD + affect dysregulation, negative self-concept, relationship difficulties
Risk factorsSeverity of trauma, prior trauma, lack of support, female sex
First-line trauma-focused therapyTF-CBT, EMDR (eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing)
First-line medicationSSRI (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine) — FDA approved
PrazosinFor trauma-related nightmares (off-label)
Acute stress disorderLasts 3 days to 1 month; same symptoms as PTSD; if >1 month → PTSD
Adjustment disorderMilder, more common, develops within 3 months of stressor

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) develops after exposure to a traumatic event, characterised by re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, and hyperarousal, lasting >1 month.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?

Key features include: trauma criteria, dsm-5 symptom clusters, duration. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

├── Clinical Features

├── Management

▸ 29.10 Psychiatric Assessment and Communication 6
▸ 29.10.1 Capacity Assessment

Definition

Capacity (or competence) is the ability to understand, retain, weigh, and communicate a decision about a specific medical intervention at a particular time. It is decision-specific and time-specific.

Core Clinical Content

Four criteria of capacity (UK MCA 2005)

Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Capacity is decision-specific

Can have capacity for one decision but not another

Capacity is time-specific

Can fluctuate; review when condition changes

Presumption of capacity

Adults presumed to have capacity unless assessed otherwise

Who assesses capacity?

Treating clinician (not a psychiatrist necessarily)

Two-stage test

(1) Diagnostic — does the person have impairment of mind/brain? (2) Functional — does the impairment affect capacity for this decision?

Common scenarios

Refusal of treatment, advance decisions, research participation, financial decisions

If lacks capacity

Best interests decision, advance decision, IMCA, court of protection

Communication aids

Use interpreters, sign language, simple language, visual aids

Adults with capacity can make unwise decisions

Capacity ≠ 'right' decision

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Four criteria of capacity (UK MCA 2005)Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate
Capacity is decision-specificCan have capacity for one decision but not another
Capacity is time-specificCan fluctuate; review when condition changes
Presumption of capacityAdults presumed to have capacity unless assessed otherwise
Who assesses capacity?Treating clinician (not a psychiatrist necessarily)
Two-stage test(1) Diagnostic — does the person have impairment of mind/brain? (2) Functional —
Common scenariosRefusal of treatment, advance decisions, research participation, financial decis
If lacks capacityBest interests decision, advance decision, IMCA, court of protection
Communication aidsUse interpreters, sign language, simple language, visual aids
Adults with capacity can make unwise decisionsCapacity ≠ 'right' decision

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Capacity assessment?

Capacity (or competence) is the ability to understand, retain, weigh, and communicate a decision about a specific medical intervention at a particular time. It is decision-specific and time-specific.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Capacity assessment?

Key features include: four criteria of capacity (uk mca 2005), capacity is decision-specific, capacity is time-specific. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Capacity assessment diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Capacity assessment?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Capacity assessment?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Capacity assessment — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

  6. Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  7. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  8. Adults presumed to have capacity unless assessed otherwise
  9. Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  10. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

▸ 29.10.2 Mental State Examination Mse

Definition

The mental state examination (MSE) is a structured assessment of a patient's current mental state, organised by Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought, Perception, Cognition, Insight, and Risk.

Core Clinical Content

MSE components

Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form/content, Perception, Cognition, Insight, Risk

Appearance

Dress, grooming, hygiene, apparent age, distinguishing features

Behaviour

Eye contact, rapport, psychomotor activity (retardation, agitation), abnormal movements

Speech

Rate, rhythm, volume, quantity, spontaneity, latency, abnormalities (pressure, mute)

Mood

Subjective emotional state (euthymic, depressed, elevated, anxious, irritable)

Affect

Observed emotional expression (broad, restricted, blunted, flat, labile, congruent)

Thought form

Linear, circumstantial, tangential, flight of ideas, loosening of associations, neologisms

Thought content

Preoccupations, obsessions, phobias, delusions, suicidal/homicidal ideation

Perception

Illusions, hallucinations (auditory, visual, olfactory, gustatory, tactile)

Cognition

Orientation, attention, concentration, memory (immediate, recent, remote)

Insight

Patient's understanding of their condition; levels 0-3 (none, partial, full)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
MSE componentsAppearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form/content, Perception, C
AppearanceDress, grooming, hygiene, apparent age, distinguishing features
BehaviourEye contact, rapport, psychomotor activity (retardation, agitation), abnormal mo
SpeechRate, rhythm, volume, quantity, spontaneity, latency, abnormalities (pressure, m
MoodSubjective emotional state (euthymic, depressed, elevated, anxious, irritable)
AffectObserved emotional expression (broad, restricted, blunted, flat, labile, congrue
Thought formLinear, circumstantial, tangential, flight of ideas, loosening of associations,
Thought contentPreoccupations, obsessions, phobias, delusions, suicidal/homicidal ideation
PerceptionIllusions, hallucinations (auditory, visual, olfactory, gustatory, tactile)
CognitionOrientation, attention, concentration, memory (immediate, recent, remote)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Mental state examination (MSE)?

The mental state examination (MSE) is a structured assessment of a patient's current mental state, organised by Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought, Perception, Cognition, Insight, and Risk.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mental state examination (MSE)?

Key features include: mse components, appearance, behaviour. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Mental state examination (MSE) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Mental state examination (MSE)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Mental state examination (MSE)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Mental state examination (MSE) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.10.3 Psychiatric History Taking

Definition

Psychiatric history taking is a comprehensive assessment of the patient's presenting complaint, history of present illness, past psychiatric and medical history, medication, family history, personal/social history, premorbid personality, and substance use.

Core Clinical Content

Presenting complaint (PC)

Patient's own words for why they are seeking help

History of present illness (HPI)

Chronological narrative of current problem

Past psychiatric history

Previous episodes, diagnoses, treatments, hospitalisations

Past medical history

Medical conditions, especially relevant (e.g., thyroid, neurological)

Medication history

Current and past medications; OTC, supplements, recreational

Family history

Psychiatric disorders, suicide, substance use in first-degree relatives

Personal history

Childhood, education, occupation, relationships, living situation

Premorbid personality

Patient's personality before the illness

Substance use history

Tobacco, alcohol, illicit drugs — type, amount, duration, route

Forensic history

Criminal record, violence, legal issues

Sexual history

When relevant (relationship, orientation, STIs, dysfunction)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Presenting complaint (PC)Patient's own words for why they are seeking help
History of present illness (HPI)Chronological narrative of current problem
Past psychiatric historyPrevious episodes, diagnoses, treatments, hospitalisations
Past medical historyMedical conditions, especially relevant (e.g., thyroid, neurological)
Medication historyCurrent and past medications; OTC, supplements, recreational
Family historyPsychiatric disorders, suicide, substance use in first-degree relatives
Personal historyChildhood, education, occupation, relationships, living situation
Premorbid personalityPatient's personality before the illness
Substance use historyTobacco, alcohol, illicit drugs — type, amount, duration, route
Forensic historyCriminal record, violence, legal issues

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Psychiatric history taking?

Psychiatric history taking is a comprehensive assessment of the patient's presenting complaint, history of present illness, past psychiatric and medical history, medication, family history, personal/social history, premorbid personality, and substance use.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Psychiatric history taking?

Key features include: presenting complaint (pc), history of present illness (hpi), past psychiatric history. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Psychiatric history taking diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Psychiatric history taking?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Psychiatric history taking?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Psychiatric history taking — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.10.4 Risk Assessment In Psychiatry Suicide Violence Sel

Definition

Psychiatric risk assessment is the systematic evaluation of a patient's risk of suicide, self-harm, violence to others, self-neglect, and vulnerability, used to guide management decisions including hospitalisation and supervision.

Core Clinical Content

Suicide risk factors

Previous attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, social isolation, chronic pain, terminal illness, access to means

Suicide protective factors

Social support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagement

SAD PERSONS scale

Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social support lacking, Organised plan, No spouse, Sickness

High-risk features

Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

Ask directly about suicide

Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

Violence risk factors

Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, personality disorder

HCR-20

Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

Self-harm

Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

Risk management

Remove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan

Documentation

Document risk assessment, plan, discussion with patient and family

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Suicide risk factorsPrevious attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, soc
Suicide protective factorsSocial support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagem
SAD PERSONS scaleSex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor
High-risk featuresActive suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent
Ask directly about suicideDoes NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask
Violence risk factorsPast violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, persona
HCR-20Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool
Self-harmDistinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die
Risk managementRemove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan
DocumentationDocument risk assessment, plan, discussion with patient and family

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Psychiatric risk assessment is the systematic evaluation of a patient's risk of suicide, self-harm, violence to others, self-neglect, and vulnerability, used to guide management decisions including hospitalisation and supervision.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Key features include: suicide risk factors, suicide protective factors, sad persons scale. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

MCQs (10)

  1. Key feature of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Previous attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, soc

B) Social support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagem

C) Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor

D) Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

Answer: A

  1. Regarding Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Social support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagem

B) Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor

C) Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

D) Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true about Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor

B) Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

C) Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

D) Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, persona

Answer: C

  1. Diagnostic criterion for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

B) Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

C) Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, persona

D) Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

Answer: D

  1. Management of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm) includes?

A) Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

B) Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, persona

C) Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

D) Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

Answer: A

  1. Prognosis of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, persona

B) Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

C) Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

D) Remove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

B) Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

C) Remove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan

D) Document risk assessment, plan, discussion with patient and family

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Previous attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, soc

B) Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor

C) Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

D) Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Social support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagem

B) Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

C) Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, persona

D) Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

A) Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor

B) Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

C) Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

Flashcards

Q: Suicide risk factors?

A: Previous attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, social isolation, chronic pain, terminal illness, access to means

Q: Suicide protective factors?

A: Social support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagement

Q: SAD PERSONS scale?

A: Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social support lacking, Organised plan, No spouse, Sickness

Q: High-risk features?

A: Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

Q: Ask directly about suicide?

A: Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

Q: Violence risk factors?

A: Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, personality disorder

Q: HCR-20?

A: Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

Q: Self-harm?

A: Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.10.4 Risk Assessment In Psychiatry Suicide Violence Self Harm

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Psychiatric Assessment and Communication

topic: Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)

Definition

Psychiatric risk assessment is the systematic evaluation of a patient's risk of suicide, self-harm, violence to others, self-neglect, and vulnerability, used to guide management decisions including hospitalisation and supervision.

Core Clinical Content

Suicide risk factors

Previous attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, social isolation, chronic pain, terminal illness, access to means

Suicide protective factors

Social support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagement

SAD PERSONS scale

Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social support lacking, Organised plan, No spouse, Sickness

High-risk features

Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

Ask directly about suicide

Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

Violence risk factors

Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, personality disorder

HCR-20

Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

Self-harm

Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

Risk management

Remove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan

Documentation

Document risk assessment, plan, discussion with patient and family

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Suicide risk factorsPrevious attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, soc
Suicide protective factorsSocial support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagem
SAD PERSONS scaleSex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor
High-risk featuresActive suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent
Ask directly about suicideDoes NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask
Violence risk factorsPast violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, persona
HCR-20Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool
Self-harmDistinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die
Risk managementRemove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan
DocumentationDocument risk assessment, plan, discussion with patient and family

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Psychiatric risk assessment is the systematic evaluation of a patient's risk of suicide, self-harm, violence to others, self-neglect, and vulnerability, used to guide management decisions including hospitalisation and supervision.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Key features include: suicide risk factors, suicide protective factors, sad persons scale. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

Mind Map

Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Features
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute
│   └── Long-term
└── Complications

2. AssessmentStandard workup

5. MonitorFollow-up and review

▸ 29.10.5 The Doctor Patient Relationship And Communication

Definition

The doctor-patient relationship is the foundation of medical psychiatry, encompassing the professional bond, communication, and trust that enables therapeutic engagement. Effective communication in psychiatric assessment includes active listening, empathy, attending to nonverbal cues, and managing the emotional content of the interview.

Core Clinical Content

Calgary-Cambridge model

Framework for medical communication: initiating, gathering info, providing structure, building relationship, explaining, closing

Patient-centred interview

Focus on patient's perspective, ideas, concerns, expectations (ICE)

Open vs closed questions

Open for narrative; closed for clarification

Active listening

Verbal (reflection, paraphrasing) + nonverbal (eye contact, posture)

Empathy

Ability to understand patient's feelings; core therapeutic skill

Rapport

Foundation for trust and disclosure

Silence

Allows patient to reflect and elaborate

Transference

Patient's feelings about clinician (from past relationships)

Countertransference

Clinician's emotional response to patient

Breaking bad news

SPIKES protocol (Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Calgary-Cambridge modelFramework for medical communication: initiating, gathering info, providing struc
Patient-centred interviewFocus on patient's perspective, ideas, concerns, expectations (ICE)
Open vs closed questionsOpen for narrative; closed for clarification
Active listeningVerbal (reflection, paraphrasing) + nonverbal (eye contact, posture)
EmpathyAbility to understand patient's feelings; core therapeutic skill
RapportFoundation for trust and disclosure
SilenceAllows patient to reflect and elaborate
TransferencePatient's feelings about clinician (from past relationships)
CountertransferenceClinician's emotional response to patient
Breaking bad newsSPIKES protocol (Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of The doctor-patient relationship and communication?

The doctor-patient relationship is the foundation of medical psychiatry, encompassing the professional bond, communication, and trust that enables therapeutic engagement. Effective communication in psychiatric assessment includes active listening, empathy, attending to nonverbal cues, and managing the emotional content of the interview.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of The doctor-patient relationship and communication?

Key features include: calgary-cambridge model, patient-centred interview, open vs closed questions. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is The doctor-patient relationship and communication diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for The doctor-patient relationship and communication?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of The doctor-patient relationship and communication?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for The doctor-patient relationship and communication — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of The doctor-patient relationship and communication?

A) Framework for medical communication: initiating, gathering info, providing struc

B) Open for narrative; closed for clarification

C) Ability to understand patient's feelings; core therapeutic skill

D) Allows patient to reflect and elaborate

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for The doctor-patient relationship and communication?

A) Focus on patient's perspective, ideas, concerns, expectations (ICE)

B) Verbal (reflection, paraphrasing) + nonverbal (eye contact, posture)

C) Foundation for trust and disclosure

D) Patient's feelings about clinician (from past relationships)

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for The doctor-patient relationship and communication?

A) Open for narrative; closed for clarification

B) Ability to understand patient's feelings; core therapeutic skill

C) Allows patient to reflect and elaborate

D) Clinician's emotional response to patient

Answer: C

SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient presents with features of The doctor-patient relationship and communication. Most appropriate first-line? | Framework for medical communication: initiating, gathering info, providing struc
  1. Which investigation is most useful in The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  1. What is the prevalence of The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Focus on patient's perspective, ideas, concerns, expectations (ICE)
  1. First-line management of The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Framework for medical communication: initiating, gathering info, providing struc
  1. Which is a recognised risk factor for The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Open for narrative; closed for clarification
  1. What is the typical course of The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  1. Which complication should be screened for in The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  1. Duration criterion for The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Verbal (reflection, paraphrasing) + nonverbal (eye contact, posture)
  1. Most appropriate referral for The doctor-patient relationship and communication? | Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  1. Differential diagnosis of The doctor-patient relationship and communication includes? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

Flashcards

Q: Calgary-Cambridge model?

A: Framework for medical communication: initiating, gathering info, providing structure, building relationship, explaining, closing

Q: Patient-centred interview?

A: Focus on patient's perspective, ideas, concerns, expectations (ICE)

Q: Open vs closed questions?

A: Open for narrative; closed for clarification

Q: Active listening?

A: Verbal (reflection, paraphrasing) + nonverbal (eye contact, posture)

Q: Empathy?

A: Ability to understand patient's feelings; core therapeutic skill

Q: Rapport?

A: Foundation for trust and disclosure

Q: Silence?

A: Allows patient to reflect and elaborate

Q: Transference?

A: Patient's feelings about clinician (from past relationships)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.11 Psychiatric Emergencies 4
▸ 29.11.1 Acute Behavioural Disturbance And Rapid Tranquilis

Definition

Acute behavioural disturbance is a clinical state of severe agitation, aggression, or violence posing immediate risk to the patient or others, often requiring rapid tranquilisation with antipsychotics and/or benzodiazepines after verbal de-escalation fails.

Core Clinical Content

Stepwise management

Verbal de-escalation → oral medication → IM medication → physical restraint (last resort)

Verbal de-escalation

Calm tone, clear language, offer choices, time, place of safety

Rapid tranquilisation (RT)

Use of medication to rapidly calm agitated patient; lowest effective dose

RT medications

IM lorazepam + haloperidol; olanzapine IM; aripiprazole IM; midazolam IM

Avoid benzodiazepines in

Respiratory depression risk; substance use, frailty

Avoid haloperidol in

Parkinsonism, Lewy body dementia (severe sensitivity), prolonged QT

Antipsychotic + benzodiazepine

Often combined; haloperidol 5 mg + lorazepam 2 mg IM is common RT combination

Dosing interval

Repeat every 30-60 min as needed; do not exceed BNF max

Monitoring post-RT

BP, pulse, SpO2, temperature, GCS, hydration; ECG if possible

Physical restraint

Last resort; rapid tranquilisation preferred; face-to-face review within 1 h

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Stepwise managementVerbal de-escalation → oral medication → IM medication → physical restraint (las
Verbal de-escalationCalm tone, clear language, offer choices, time, place of safety
Rapid tranquilisation (RT)Use of medication to rapidly calm agitated patient; lowest effective dose
RT medicationsIM lorazepam + haloperidol; olanzapine IM; aripiprazole IM; midazolam IM
Avoid benzodiazepines inRespiratory depression risk; substance use, frailty
Avoid haloperidol inParkinsonism, Lewy body dementia (severe sensitivity), prolonged QT
Antipsychotic + benzodiazepineOften combined; haloperidol 5 mg + lorazepam 2 mg IM is common RT combination
Dosing intervalRepeat every 30-60 min as needed; do not exceed BNF max
Monitoring post-RTBP, pulse, SpO2, temperature, GCS, hydration; ECG if possible
Physical restraintLast resort; rapid tranquilisation preferred; face-to-face review within 1 h

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Acute behavioural disturbance is a clinical state of severe agitation, aggression, or violence posing immediate risk to the patient or others, often requiring rapid tranquilisation with antipsychotics and/or benzodiazepines after verbal de-escalation fails.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Key features include: stepwise management, verbal de-escalation, rapid tranquilisation (rt).

Q3. How is Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation

Definition

Acute behavioural disturbance is a clinical state of severe agitation, aggression, or violence posing immediate risk to the patient or others, often requiring rapid tranquilisation with antipsychotics and/or benzodiazepines after verbal de-escalation fails.

Core Clinical Content

Stepwise management

Verbal de-escalation → oral medication → IM medication → physical restraint (last resort)

Verbal de-escalation

Calm tone, clear language, offer choices, time, place of safety

Rapid tranquilisation (RT)

Use of medication to rapidly calm agitated patient; lowest effective dose

RT medications

IM lorazepam + haloperidol; olanzapine IM; aripiprazole IM; midazolam IM

Avoid benzodiazepines in

Respiratory depression risk; substance use, frailty

Avoid haloperidol in

Parkinsonism, Lewy body dementia (severe sensitivity), prolonged QT

Antipsychotic + benzodiazepine

Often combined; haloperidol 5 mg + lorazepam 2 mg IM is common RT combination

Dosing interval

Repeat every 30-60 min as needed; do not exceed BNF max

Monitoring post-RT

BP, pulse, SpO2, temperature, GCS, hydration; ECG if possible

Physical restraint

Last resort; rapid tranquilisation preferred; face-to-face review within 1 h

Q1. What is the definition of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Acute behavioural disturbance is a clinical state of severe agitation, aggression, or violence posing immediate risk to the patient or others, often requiring rapid tranquilisation with antipsychotics and/or benzodiazepines after verbal de-escalation fails.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Key features include: stepwise management, verbal de-escalation, rapid tranquilisation (rt).

Q3. How is Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

▸ 29.11.2 Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome Nms

Definition

Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to antipsychotics (dopamine antagonists) characterised by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, autonomic instability, and altered mental status.

Core Clinical Content

NMS tetrad

Hyperthermia, muscle rigidity ('lead pipe'), autonomic instability, altered consciousness

Onset

Hours to days after antipsychotic change (rapid dose increase, parenteral)

Risk factors

High-potency typical antipsychotics, rapid dose escalation, dehydration, NMS history

Investigations

↑ CK (often >1000), ↑ WCC, ↓ Na, ↑ K (rhabdomyolysis), abnormal LFTs, myoglobinuria

Differential

Serotonin syndrome, malignant hyperthermia, anticholinergic toxicity, catatonia

Serotonin vs NMS

Serotonin: hyperreflexia, clonus, on serotonergic drugs; NMS: rigidity, bradyreflexia, on antipsychotics

Treatment

STOP antipsychotic; supportive care (ICU); dantrolene, bromocriptine, ECT in severe

Dantrolene

Muscle relaxant; reduces hyperthermia/rigidity

Bromocriptine

Dopamine agonist; reverses NMS pathophysiology

Prognosis

5-20% mortality if untreated; 80% recover with prompt recognition and treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
NMS tetradHyperthermia, muscle rigidity ('lead pipe'), autonomic instability, altered cons

Risk factorsHigh-potency typical antipsychotics, rapid dose escalation, dehydration, NMS his
