Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)?

Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to antipsychotics (dopamine antagonists) characterised by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, autonomic instability, and altered mental status.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)?

Key features include: nms tetrad, onset, risk factors.

Q3. How is Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.11.3 Serotonin Syndrome And Catatonia

Definition

Serotonin syndrome is a potentially life-threatening condition caused by excess serotonergic activity, characterised by cognitive, autonomic, and somatic features. Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome of psychomotor dysregulation with multiple subtypes.

Core Clinical Content

Serotonin syndrome triad

Cognitive (agitation, confusion), autonomic (fever, tachycardia, hypertension), somatic (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor)

Cause

Serotonergic drugs (SSRIs + MAOIs, tramadol, linezolid, St John's Wort, triptans)

Onset

Within hours of serotonergic drug change

Clonus

Inducible, spontaneous, ocular — hallmark of serotonin syndrome

Treatment

STOP serotonergic drugs; supportive care; cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist)

Cyproheptadine

5-HT2A antagonist; for moderate-severe cases

Catatonia features

Mutism, stupor, negativism, waxy flexibility, echolalia, echopraxia, posturing

Catatonia subtypes

Retarded (withdrawn), excited, malignant (with autonomic instability)

Catatonia causes

Mood disorders (esp. bipolar), schizophrenia, medical, drug-induced

Lorazepam challenge

1-2 mg IV/IM lorazepam; rapid response confirms catatonia; ECT for refractory

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Serotonin syndrome triadCognitive (agitation, confusion), autonomic (fever, tachycardia, hypertension),

OnsetWithin hours of serotonergic drug change
ClonusInducible, spontaneous, ocular — hallmark of serotonin syndrome
TreatmentSTOP serotonergic drugs; supportive care; cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist)
Cyproheptadine5-HT2A antagonist; for moderate-severe cases
Catatonia featuresMutism, stupor, negativism, waxy flexibility, echolalia, echopraxia, posturing
Catatonia subtypesRetarded (withdrawn), excited, malignant (with autonomic instability)
Catatonia causesMood disorders (esp. bipolar), schizophrenia, medical, drug-induced
Lorazepam challenge1-2 mg IV/IM lorazepam; rapid response confirms catatonia; ECT for refractory

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Serotonin syndrome and catatonia?

Serotonin syndrome is a potentially life-threatening condition caused by excess serotonergic activity, characterised by cognitive, autonomic, and somatic features. Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome of psychomotor dysregulation with multiple subtypes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Serotonin syndrome and catatonia?

Key features include: serotonin syndrome triad, cause, onset.

Q3. How is Serotonin syndrome and catatonia diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Serotonin syndrome and catatonia?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Serotonin syndrome and catatonia?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

▸ 29.12 Psychological Therapies 4
▸ 29.12.1 Cognitive Behavioural Therapy Cbt And Related Ther

Definition

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a structured, time-limited, present-focused psychotherapy that identifies and modifies unhelpful thinking patterns (cognitions) and behaviours to improve emotional wellbeing. Related therapies include DBT, ACT, and mindfulness-based CBT.

Core Clinical Content

CBT principles

Cognitive model (thoughts → feelings → behaviour); collaborative empiricism; Socratic questioning

CBT techniques

Thought records, behavioural activation, exposure, cognitive restructuring, problem-solving

CBT duration

Usually 12-20 sessions; structured; homework between sessions

CBT effectiveness

Strong evidence for depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, eating disorders

Cognitive distortions

Catastrophising, all-or-nothing thinking, mind-reading, overgeneralisation, personalisation

Behavioural activation

Schedule activities; counteracts withdrawal in depression

Exposure therapy

Graded exposure to feared stimuli; for phobias, OCD, PTSD

Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)

For BPD; combines CBT + mindfulness; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)

Acceptance + values-based action; for chronic conditions

Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT)

For relapse prevention in recurrent depression; combines mindfulness + CBT

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
CBT principlesCognitive model (thoughts → feelings → behaviour); collaborative empiricism; Soc
CBT techniquesThought records, behavioural activation, exposure, cognitive restructuring, prob
CBT durationUsually 12-20 sessions; structured; homework between sessions
CBT effectivenessStrong evidence for depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, eating disorders
Cognitive distortionsCatastrophising, all-or-nothing thinking, mind-reading, overgeneralisation, pers
Behavioural activationSchedule activities; counteracts withdrawal in depression
Exposure therapyGraded exposure to feared stimuli; for phobias, OCD, PTSD
Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)For BPD; combines CBT + mindfulness; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance,
Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)Acceptance + values-based action; for chronic conditions
Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT)For relapse prevention in recurrent depression; combines mindfulness + CBT

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a structured, time-limited, present-focused psychotherapy that identifies and modifies unhelpful thinking patterns (cognitions) and behaviours to improve emotional wellbeing. Related therapies include DBT, ACT, and mindfulness-based CBT.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Key features include: cbt principles, cbt techniques, cbt duration.

Q3. How is Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.12.1 Cognitive Behavioural Therapy Cbt And Related Therapies

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Psychological Therapies

topic: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies

Definition

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a structured, time-limited, present-focused psychotherapy that identifies and modifies unhelpful thinking patterns (cognitions) and behaviours to improve emotional wellbeing. Related therapies include DBT, ACT, and mindfulness-based CBT.

Core Clinical Content

CBT principles

Cognitive model (thoughts → feelings → behaviour); collaborative empiricism; Socratic questioning

CBT techniques

Thought records, behavioural activation, exposure, cognitive restructuring, problem-solving

CBT duration

Usually 12-20 sessions; structured; homework between sessions

CBT effectiveness

Strong evidence for depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, eating disorders

Cognitive distortions

Catastrophising, all-or-nothing thinking, mind-reading, overgeneralisation, personalisation

Behavioural activation

Schedule activities; counteracts withdrawal in depression

Exposure therapy

Graded exposure to feared stimuli; for phobias, OCD, PTSD

Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)

For BPD; combines CBT + mindfulness; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)

Acceptance + values-based action; for chronic conditions

Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT)

For relapse prevention in recurrent depression; combines mindfulness + CBT

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
CBT principlesCognitive model (thoughts → feelings → behaviour); collaborative empiricism; Soc
CBT techniquesThought records, behavioural activation, exposure, cognitive restructuring, prob
CBT durationUsually 12-20 sessions; structured; homework between sessions
CBT effectivenessStrong evidence for depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, eating disorders
Cognitive distortionsCatastrophising, all-or-nothing thinking, mind-reading, overgeneralisation, pers
Behavioural activationSchedule activities; counteracts withdrawal in depression
Exposure therapyGraded exposure to feared stimuli; for phobias, OCD, PTSD
Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)For BPD; combines CBT + mindfulness; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance,
Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)Acceptance + values-based action; for chronic conditions
Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT)For relapse prevention in recurrent depression; combines mindfulness + CBT

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a structured, time-limited, present-focused psychotherapy that identifies and modifies unhelpful thinking patterns (cognitions) and behaviours to improve emotional wellbeing. Related therapies include DBT, ACT, and mindfulness-based CBT.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Key features include: cbt principles, cbt techniques, cbt duration.

Q3. How is Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.12.2 Other Therapies Dbt Emdr Mindfulness

Definition

Other evidence-based psychotherapies include dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) for BPD, eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR) for PTSD, mindfulness-based therapies, and motivational interviewing for substance use.

Core Clinical Content

DBT for BPD

Individual + group therapy + phone coaching; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

DBT duration

1-3 years; weekly individual + group sessions

DBT key skills

DEAR MAN (interpersonal), TIPP (distress tolerance), PLEASE (emotion regulation)

EMDR

Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing; for PTSD; 8 phases

EMDR mechanism

Bilateral stimulation (eye movements) while recalling traumatic memory; debated

Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)

8-week program; for chronic stress, anxiety, depression relapse

Motivational interviewing (MI)

For substance use; collaborative, evocative, autonomy-supporting

MI principles

Express empathy, develop discrepancy, roll with resistance, support self-efficacy

Exposure therapy for PTSD

Prolonged exposure (PE), cognitive processing therapy (CPT)

EMDR vs exposure

EMDR includes bilateral stimulation; PE is pure exposure; both effective for PTSD

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DBT for BPDIndividual + group therapy + phone coaching; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress to
DBT duration1-3 years; weekly individual + group sessions
DBT key skillsDEAR MAN (interpersonal), TIPP (distress tolerance), PLEASE (emotion regulation)
EMDREye movement desensitisation and reprocessing; for PTSD; 8 phases
EMDR mechanismBilateral stimulation (eye movements) while recalling traumatic memory; debated
Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)8-week program; for chronic stress, anxiety, depression relapse
Motivational interviewing (MI)For substance use; collaborative, evocative, autonomy-supporting
MI principlesExpress empathy, develop discrepancy, roll with resistance, support self-efficac
Exposure therapy for PTSDProlonged exposure (PE), cognitive processing therapy (CPT)
EMDR vs exposureEMDR includes bilateral stimulation; PE is pure exposure; both effective for PTS

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness)?

Other evidence-based psychotherapies include dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) for BPD, eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR) for PTSD, mindfulness-based therapies, and motivational interviewing for substance use.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness)?

Key features include: dbt for bpd, dbt duration, dbt key skills.

Q3. How is Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | 1-3 years; weekly individual + group sessions
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | DEAR MAN (interpersonal), TIPP (distress tolerance), PLEASE (emotion regulation)
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing; for PTSD; 8 phases
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | Bilateral stimulation (eye movements) while recalling traumatic memory; debated
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | 8-week program; for chronic stress, anxiety, depression relapse
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | For substance use; collaborative, evocative, autonomy-supporting
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | Express empathy, develop discrepancy, roll with resistance, support self-efficacy
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | Prolonged exposure (PE), cognitive processing therapy (CPT)
  1. Patient presents with features of Other therapies (DBT, EMDR, mindfulness). Which is most appropriate? | EMDR includes bilateral stimulation; PE is pure exposure; both effective for PTSD

Flashcards

Q: DBT for BPD?

A: Individual + group therapy + phone coaching; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion reg

Q: DBT duration?

A: 1-3 years; weekly individual + group sessions

Q: DBT key skills?

A: DEAR MAN (interpersonal), TIPP (distress tolerance), PLEASE (emotion regulation)

Q: EMDR?

A: Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing; for PTSD; 8 phases

Q: EMDR mechanism?

A: Bilateral stimulation (eye movements) while recalling traumatic memory; debated

Q: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)?

A: 8-week program; for chronic stress, anxiety, depression relapse

Q: Motivational interviewing (MI)?

A: For substance use; collaborative, evocative, autonomy-supporting

Q: MI principles?

A: Express empathy, develop discrepancy, roll with resistance, support self-efficacy

Answer Key with Explanations

  8. D — MI principles

▸ 29.12.3 Psychodynamic And Other Psychotherapies

Definition

Psychodynamic psychotherapy explores unconscious conflicts, early life experiences, and the therapeutic relationship to promote insight and change. Other psychotherapies include interpersonal therapy (IPT), family therapy, and group therapy.

Core Clinical Content

Psychodynamic therapy

Explores unconscious conflicts, transference, defence mechanisms; longer-term

Defence mechanisms

Denial, projection, displacement, sublimation, rationalisation, reaction formation, repression

Transference

Patient's feelings about therapist (from past relationships)

Countertransference

Therapist's emotional response to patient

Psychodynamic therapy uses

Personality disorders, chronic depression, anxiety, eating disorders

Interpersonal therapy (IPT)

Time-limited (12-16 sessions); focuses on interpersonal problems; for depression

IPT focus areas

Grief, role disputes, role transitions, interpersonal deficits

Family therapy

Treats family as a system; for schizophrenia, eating disorders, family conflict

Group therapy

Therapeutic factors: universality, altruism, instillation of hope, catharsis

Supportive psychotherapy

Active listening, empathy, reassurance; for chronic conditions, crisis

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Psychodynamic therapyExplores unconscious conflicts, transference, defence mechanisms; longer-term
Defence mechanismsDenial, projection, displacement, sublimation, rationalisation, reaction formati
TransferencePatient's feelings about therapist (from past relationships)
CountertransferenceTherapist's emotional response to patient
Psychodynamic therapy usesPersonality disorders, chronic depression, anxiety, eating disorders
Interpersonal therapy (IPT)Time-limited (12-16 sessions); focuses on interpersonal problems; for depression
IPT focus areasGrief, role disputes, role transitions, interpersonal deficits
Family therapyTreats family as a system; for schizophrenia, eating disorders, family conflict
Group therapyTherapeutic factors: universality, altruism, instillation of hope, catharsis
Supportive psychotherapyActive listening, empathy, reassurance; for chronic conditions, crisis

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Psychodynamic and other psychotherapies?

Psychodynamic psychotherapy explores unconscious conflicts, early life experiences, and the therapeutic relationship to promote insight and change. Other psychotherapies include interpersonal therapy (IPT), family therapy, and group therapy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Psychodynamic and other psychotherapies?

Key features include: psychodynamic therapy, defence mechanisms, transference.

Q3. How is Psychodynamic and other psychotherapies diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Psychodynamic and other psychotherapies?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Psychodynamic and other psychotherapies?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Psychodynamic and other psychotherapies — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.13 Psychopharmacology 11
▸ 29.13.1 Antidepressants Ssri Snri Tca Maoi Atypicals

Definition

Antidepressants are medications used to treat major depressive disorder and other psychiatric conditions. They work by modulating monoamine neurotransmitters (serotonin, noradrenaline, dopamine) and include SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, and atypical agents.

Core Clinical Content

SSRI mechanism

Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor; ↑ serotonin in synapse

Common SSRIs

Fluoxetine, sertraline, citalopram, escitalopram, paroxetine

SSRI side effects

GI upset, sexual dysfunction, hyponatraemia (SIADH), bleeding risk, QTc (citalopram)

SSRI discontinuation

Discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, nausea, paresthesia) — taper slowly

SNRI

Duloxetine, venlafaxine; serotonin + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition

SNRI uses

Depression, GAD, neuropathic pain (duloxetine), fibromyalgia

TCA mechanism

Serotonin + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition (also anticholinergic, antihistamine)

TCA side effects

Anticholinergic (dry mouth, urinary retention), sedation, orthostatic hypotension, QTc prolongation, fatal in overdose

TCA overdose

Cardiotoxic (VT, torsades), seizures, anticholinergic; sodium bicarbonate for VT

MAOI

Phenelzine, tranylcypromine; dietary restrictions (tyramine → hypertensive crisis); rarely used

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

Common SSRIsFluoxetine, sertraline, citalopram, escitalopram, paroxetine
SSRI side effectsGI upset, sexual dysfunction, hyponatraemia (SIADH), bleeding risk, QTc (citalop
SSRI discontinuationDiscontinuation syndrome (dizziness, nausea, paresthesia) — taper slowly
SNRIDuloxetine, venlafaxine; serotonin + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition
SNRI usesDepression, GAD, neuropathic pain (duloxetine), fibromyalgia
TCA mechanismSerotonin + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition (also anticholinergic, antihistami
TCA side effectsAnticholinergic (dry mouth, urinary retention), sedation, orthostatic hypotensio
TCA overdoseCardiotoxic (VT, torsades), seizures, anticholinergic; sodium bicarbonate for VT
MAOIPhenelzine, tranylcypromine; dietary restrictions (tyramine → hypertensive crisi

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals)?

Antidepressants are medications used to treat major depressive disorder and other psychiatric conditions. They work by modulating monoamine neurotransmitters (serotonin, noradrenaline, dopamine) and include SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, and atypical agents.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals)?

Key features include: ssri mechanism, common ssris, ssri side effects.

Q3. How is Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.13.2 Antipsychotic Induced Movement Disorders Eps And A

Definition

Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders include extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia) and related conditions. Anticholinergic toxicity is a serious adverse effect of drugs with anticholinergic properties (TCAs, antipsychotics), characterised by classic antimuscarinic features.

Core Clinical Content

EPS types

Acute dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive dyskinesia (DAPT)

Acute dystonia

Hours-days; sustained muscle contraction (torticollis, oculogyric crisis); treat with anticholinergic (procyclidine IM)

Akathisia

Days-weeks; subjective restlessness; treat with β-blocker (propranolol), benzodiazepine, dose reduction

Drug-induced parkinsonism

Weeks-months; bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor; treat with anticholinergic; switch to atypical

Tardive dyskinesia (TD)

Months-years; orofacial dyskinesia, choreoathetoid movements; often IRREVERSIBLE

TD risk factors

Older age, female, longer duration, higher dose, typical antipsychotics, mood disorder

TD treatment

Stop/reduce antipsychotic; switch to clozapine; VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine)

Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)

Hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness; emergency

Anticholinergic toxicity — central

Confusion, agitation, hallucinations, hyperthermia, seizures

Anticholinergic toxicity — peripheral

Dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation, mydriasis, tachycardia, flushed skin

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EPS typesAcute dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive dyskinesia (DAPT)
Acute dystoniaHours-days; sustained muscle contraction (torticollis, oculogyric crisis); treat
AkathisiaDays-weeks; subjective restlessness; treat with β-blocker (propranolol), benzodi
Drug-induced parkinsonismWeeks-months; bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor; treat with anticholinergic; switch
Tardive dyskinesia (TD)Months-years; orofacial dyskinesia, choreoathetoid movements; often IRREVERSIBLE
TD risk factorsOlder age, female, longer duration, higher dose, typical antipsychotics, mood di
TD treatmentStop/reduce antipsychotic; switch to clozapine; VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, d
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)Hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness; emergency
Anticholinergic toxicity — centralConfusion, agitation, hallucinations, hyperthermia, seizures
Anticholinergic toxicity — peripheralDry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation, mydriasis, tachycard

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders include extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia) and related conditions. Anticholinergic toxicity is a serious adverse effect of drugs with anticholinergic properties (TCAs, antipsychotics), characterised by classic antimuscarinic features.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Key features include: eps types, acute dystonia, akathisia.

Q3. How is Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

Flashcards

Q: EPS types?

A: Acute dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive dyskinesia (DAPT)

Q: Acute dystonia?

A: Hours-days; sustained muscle contraction (torticollis, oculogyric crisis); treat with anticholinergi

Q: Akathisia?

A: Days-weeks; subjective restlessness; treat with β-blocker (propranolol), benzodiazepine, dose reduct

Q: Drug-induced parkinsonism?

A: Weeks-months; bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor; treat with anticholinergic; switch to atypical

Q: Tardive dyskinesia (TD)?

A: Months-years; orofacial dyskinesia, choreoathetoid movements; often IRREVERSIBLE

Q: TD risk factors?

A: Older age, female, longer duration, higher dose, typical antipsychotics, mood disorder

Q: TD treatment?

A: Stop/reduce antipsychotic; switch to clozapine; VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine)

Q: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)?

A: Hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness; emergency

Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity

Definition

Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders include extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia) and related conditions. Anticholinergic toxicity is a serious adverse effect of drugs with anticholinergic properties (TCAs, antipsychotics), characterised by classic antimuscarinic features.

Core Clinical Content

EPS types

Acute dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive dyskinesia (DAPT)

Acute dystonia

Hours-days; sustained muscle contraction (torticollis, oculogyric crisis); treat with anticholinergic (procyclidine IM)

Akathisia

Days-weeks; subjective restlessness; treat with β-blocker (propranolol), benzodiazepine, dose reduction

Drug-induced parkinsonism

Weeks-months; bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor; treat with anticholinergic; switch to atypical

Tardive dyskinesia (TD)

Months-years; orofacial dyskinesia, choreoathetoid movements; often IRREVERSIBLE

TD risk factors

Older age, female, longer duration, higher dose, typical antipsychotics, mood disorder

TD treatment

Stop/reduce antipsychotic; switch to clozapine; VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine)

Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)

Hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness; emergency

Anticholinergic toxicity — central

Confusion, agitation, hallucinations, hyperthermia, seizures

Anticholinergic toxicity — peripheral

Dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation, mydriasis, tachycardia, flushed skin

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders include extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia) and related conditions. Anticholinergic toxicity is a serious adverse effect of drugs with anticholinergic properties (TCAs, antipsychotics), characterised by classic antimuscarinic features.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Key features include: eps types, acute dystonia, akathisia.

Q3. How is Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • Mnemonic for Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

▸ 29.13.3 Antipsychotics Typical Atypical And Clozapine

Definition

Antipsychotics are dopamine D2 receptor antagonists used to treat psychotic disorders. They are classified as typical (first-generation) or atypical (second-generation) based on side effect profiles and serotonin/dopamine receptor activity.

Core Clinical Content

Typical (first-gen)

Haloperidol, chlorpromazine, fluphenazine, zuclopenthixol; higher EPS risk

Atypical (second-gen)

Risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, clozapine; lower EPS risk

Dopamine pathways

Mesolimbic (positive symptoms), mesocortical (negative), nigrostriatal (EPS), tuberoinfundibular (prolactin)

Mechanism of antipsychotics

D2 receptor antagonism; atypicals also block 5-HT2A

Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS)

Acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia

Metabolic side effects (atypicals)

Weight gain, dyslipidaemia, diabetes (especially olanzapine, clozapine)

Hyperprolactinaemia

Risperidone, amisulpride, paliperidone

QTc prolongation

Ziprasidone, sertindole; caution in cardiac disease

Clozapine — reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia

Requires regular FBC (agranulocytosis risk); register monitoring

Clozapine side effects

Agranulocytosis, myocarditis, weight gain, diabetes, sedation, sialorrhoea, seizures

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Typical (first-gen)Haloperidol, chlorpromazine, fluphenazine, zuclopenthixol; higher EPS risk
Atypical (second-gen)Risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, clozapine; lower EPS risk
Dopamine pathwaysMesolimbic (positive symptoms), mesocortical (negative), nigrostriatal (EPS), tu
Mechanism of antipsychoticsD2 receptor antagonism; atypicals also block 5-HT2A
Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS)Acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia
Metabolic side effects (atypicals)Weight gain, dyslipidaemia, diabetes (especially olanzapine, clozapine)
HyperprolactinaemiaRisperidone, amisulpride, paliperidone
QTc prolongationZiprasidone, sertindole; caution in cardiac disease
Clozapine — reserved for treatment-resistant schizRequires regular FBC (agranulocytosis risk); register monitoring
Clozapine side effectsAgranulocytosis, myocarditis, weight gain, diabetes, sedation, sialorrhoea, seiz

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine?

Antipsychotics are dopamine D2 receptor antagonists used to treat psychotic disorders. They are classified as typical (first-generation) or atypical (second-generation) based on side effect profiles and serotonin/dopamine receptor activity.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine?

Key features include: typical (first-gen), atypical (second-gen), dopamine pathways.

Q3. How is Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.13.4 Anxiolytics And Hypnotics Benzodiazepines Z Drugs

Definition

Anxiolytics and hypnotics are medications used to treat anxiety and insomnia. The main classes are benzodiazepines, Z-drugs (non-benzodiazepine hypnotics), buspirone, and beta-blockers (for performance anxiety).

Core Clinical Content

Benzodiazepine mechanism

↑ GABA-A receptor activity (positive allosteric modulator)

Benzodiazepine examples

Diazepam (long-acting), lorazepam (intermediate), temazepam (short-acting)

Benzodiazepine uses

Anxiety, insomnia, alcohol withdrawal, status epilepticus, procedural sedation

Benzodiazepine side effects

Sedation, dependence, tolerance, falls, respiratory depression (with opioids)

Benzodiazepine dependence

Develops within weeks; withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures)

Benzodiazepine withdrawal

Taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks); convert to long-acting (diazepam) first

Z-drugs

Zolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon; non-benzodiazepine hypnotics; same risks

Buspirone

5-HT1A partial agonist; anxiolytic; non-sedating; no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks

Pregabalin

α2δ ligand; reduces glutamate; anxiolytic (GAD), neuropathic pain, epilepsy

Beta-blockers (propranolol)

For performance anxiety (blocks peripheral symptoms); not for GAD

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Benzodiazepine mechanism↑ GABA-A receptor activity (positive allosteric modulator)
Benzodiazepine examplesDiazepam (long-acting), lorazepam (intermediate), temazepam (short-acting)
Benzodiazepine usesAnxiety, insomnia, alcohol withdrawal, status epilepticus, procedural sedation
Benzodiazepine side effectsSedation, dependence, tolerance, falls, respiratory depression (with opioids)
Benzodiazepine dependenceDevelops within weeks; withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures)
Benzodiazepine withdrawalTaper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks); convert to long-acting (diazepam) first
Z-drugsZolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon; non-benzodiazepine hypnotics; same risks
Buspirone5-HT1A partial agonist; anxiolytic; non-sedating; no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks
Pregabalinα2δ ligand; reduces glutamate; anxiolytic (GAD), neuropathic pain, epilepsy
Beta-blockers (propranolol)For performance anxiety (blocks peripheral symptoms); not for GAD

Q1. What is the definition of Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Anxiolytics and hypnotics are medications used to treat anxiety and insomnia. The main classes are benzodiazepines, Z-drugs (non-benzodiazepine hypnotics), buspirone, and beta-blockers (for performance anxiety).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Key features include: benzodiazepine mechanism, benzodiazepine examples, benzodiazepine uses.

Q3. How is Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Flashcards

Q: Benzodiazepine mechanism?

A: ↑ GABA-A receptor activity (positive allosteric modulator)

Q: Benzodiazepine examples?

A: Diazepam (long-acting), lorazepam (intermediate), temazepam (short-acting)

Q: Benzodiazepine uses?

A: Anxiety, insomnia, alcohol withdrawal, status epilepticus, procedural sedation

Q: Benzodiazepine side effects?

A: Sedation, dependence, tolerance, falls, respiratory depression (with opioids)

Q: Benzodiazepine dependence?

A: Develops within weeks; withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures)

Q: Benzodiazepine withdrawal?

A: Taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks); convert to long-acting (diazepam) first

Q: Z-drugs?

A: Zolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon; non-benzodiazepine hypnotics; same risks

Q: Buspirone?

A: 5-HT1A partial agonist; anxiolytic; non-sedating; no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks

▸ 29.13.4 Anxiolytics And Hypnotics Benzodiazepines Z Drugs Buspirone

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Psychopharmacology

topic: Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)

Definition

Anxiolytics and hypnotics are medications used to treat anxiety and insomnia. The main classes are benzodiazepines, Z-drugs (non-benzodiazepine hypnotics), buspirone, and beta-blockers (for performance anxiety).

Core Clinical Content

Benzodiazepine mechanism

↑ GABA-A receptor activity (positive allosteric modulator)

Benzodiazepine examples

Diazepam (long-acting), lorazepam (intermediate), temazepam (short-acting)

Benzodiazepine uses

Anxiety, insomnia, alcohol withdrawal, status epilepticus, procedural sedation

Benzodiazepine side effects

Sedation, dependence, tolerance, falls, respiratory depression (with opioids)

Benzodiazepine dependence

Develops within weeks; withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures)

Benzodiazepine withdrawal

Taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks); convert to long-acting (diazepam) first

Z-drugs

Zolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon; non-benzodiazepine hypnotics; same risks

Buspirone

5-HT1A partial agonist; anxiolytic; non-sedating; no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks

Pregabalin

α2δ ligand; reduces glutamate; anxiolytic (GAD), neuropathic pain, epilepsy

Beta-blockers (propranolol)

For performance anxiety (blocks peripheral symptoms); not for GAD

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Benzodiazepine mechanism↑ GABA-A receptor activity (positive allosteric modulator)
Benzodiazepine examplesDiazepam (long-acting), lorazepam (intermediate), temazepam (short-acting)
Benzodiazepine usesAnxiety, insomnia, alcohol withdrawal, status epilepticus, procedural sedation
Benzodiazepine side effectsSedation, dependence, tolerance, falls, respiratory depression (with opioids)
Benzodiazepine dependenceDevelops within weeks; withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures)
Benzodiazepine withdrawalTaper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks); convert to long-acting (diazepam) first
Z-drugsZolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon; non-benzodiazepine hypnotics; same risks
Buspirone5-HT1A partial agonist; anxiolytic; non-sedating; no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks
Pregabalinα2δ ligand; reduces glutamate; anxiolytic (GAD), neuropathic pain, epilepsy
Beta-blockers (propranolol)For performance anxiety (blocks peripheral symptoms); not for GAD

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Anxiolytics and hypnotics are medications used to treat anxiety and insomnia. The main classes are benzodiazepines, Z-drugs (non-benzodiazepine hypnotics), buspirone, and beta-blockers (for performance anxiety).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Key features include: benzodiazepine mechanism, benzodiazepine examples, benzodiazepine uses.

Q3. How is Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Anxiolytics and hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, buspirone) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.13.5 Ect Electroconvulsive Therapy And Other Biological

Definition

Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a safe and highly effective treatment for severe psychiatric conditions, especially treatment-resistant depression, with electrical current inducing a generalised seizure under anaesthesia. Other biological treatments include TMS, ketamine, and esketamine.

Core Clinical Content

ECT mechanism

Induced generalised seizure under anaesthesia + muscle relaxant

ECT indications

Severe/treatment-resistant depression, catatonia, psychotic depression, NMS, severe mania, pregnancy (preferred)

ECT efficacy

60-80% response in severe depression; rapid effect (1-2 weeks)

ECT course

Usually 6-12 sessions; 2-3 per week

ECT side effects

Memory impairment (retrograde/anterograde), headache, nausea, anaesthesia risks

ECT contraindications

No absolute contraindications; relative: ↑ ICP, recent MI/stroke, pregnancy (modified ECT safe)

ECT in pregnancy

Safe; preferred for severe depression; avoid hyperventilation; protect airway

Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS)

Non-invasive; for treatment-resistant depression; rTMS effective

Esketamine (Spravato)

NMDA antagonist; intranasal; for treatment-resistant depression (with SSRI)

Ketamine

IV; rapid antidepressant effect (hours); potential abuse; research/limited use

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ECT mechanismInduced generalised seizure under anaesthesia + muscle relaxant
ECT indicationsSevere/treatment-resistant depression, catatonia, psychotic depression, NMS, sev

ECT courseUsually 6-12 sessions; 2-3 per week
ECT side effectsMemory impairment (retrograde/anterograde), headache, nausea, anaesthesia risks
ECT contraindicationsNo absolute contraindications; relative: ↑ ICP, recent MI/stroke, pregnancy (mod
ECT in pregnancySafe; preferred for severe depression; avoid hyperventilation; protect airway
Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS)Non-invasive; for treatment-resistant depression; rTMS effective
Esketamine (Spravato)NMDA antagonist; intranasal; for treatment-resistant depression (with SSRI)
KetamineIV; rapid antidepressant effect (hours); potential abuse; research/limited use

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a safe and highly effective treatment for severe psychiatric conditions, especially treatment-resistant depression, with electrical current inducing a generalised seizure under anaesthesia. Other biological treatments include TMS, ketamine, and esketamine.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Key features include: ect mechanism, ect indications, ect efficacy.

Q3. How is ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.13.5 Ect Electroconvulsive Therapy And Other Biological Treatments

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Psychopharmacology

topic: ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments

Definition

Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a safe and highly effective treatment for severe psychiatric conditions, especially treatment-resistant depression, with electrical current inducing a generalised seizure under anaesthesia. Other biological treatments include TMS, ketamine, and esketamine.

Core Clinical Content

ECT mechanism

Induced generalised seizure under anaesthesia + muscle relaxant

ECT indications

Severe/treatment-resistant depression, catatonia, psychotic depression, NMS, severe mania, pregnancy (preferred)

ECT efficacy

60-80% response in severe depression; rapid effect (1-2 weeks)

ECT course

Usually 6-12 sessions; 2-3 per week

ECT side effects

Memory impairment (retrograde/anterograde), headache, nausea, anaesthesia risks

ECT contraindications

No absolute contraindications; relative: ↑ ICP, recent MI/stroke, pregnancy (modified ECT safe)

ECT in pregnancy

Safe; preferred for severe depression; avoid hyperventilation; protect airway

Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS)

Non-invasive; for treatment-resistant depression; rTMS effective

Esketamine (Spravato)

NMDA antagonist; intranasal; for treatment-resistant depression (with SSRI)

Ketamine

IV; rapid antidepressant effect (hours); potential abuse; research/limited use

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

ECT indicationsSevere/treatment-resistant depression, catatonia, psychotic depression, NMS, sev

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a safe and highly effective treatment for severe psychiatric conditions, especially treatment-resistant depression, with electrical current inducing a generalised seizure under anaesthesia. Other biological treatments include TMS, ketamine, and esketamine.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Key features include: ect mechanism, ect indications, ect efficacy.

Q3. How is ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.13.6 Mood Stabilisers Lithium Valproate Lamotrigine

Definition

Mood stabilisers are medications used to treat bipolar disorder, both for acute episodes (mania, depression) and for long-term prophylaxis. They include lithium, valproate, lamotrigine, and carbamazepine.

Core Clinical Content

Lithium

Gold standard for bipolar; effective in mania, depression, prophylaxis; ↓ suicide risk

Lithium mechanism

Unknown; modulates intracellular signalling, neuroprotection

Lithium monitoring

TFTs (hypothyroidism), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 months

Lithium therapeutic range

0.6-1.0 mmol/L (maintenance); 0.8-1.0 (acute mania); narrow therapeutic index

Lithium toxicity

1.5 mmol/L; tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures, cardiac; >2.0 = severe; haemodialysis

Lithium side effects

Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, hypothyroidism, tremor, weight gain, teratogenic (Ebstein anomaly)

Valproate

Effective in mania, mixed episodes, rapid cycling; teratogenic (neural tube defects)

Valproate monitoring

LFTs, FBC, level; avoid in women of childbearing age

Lamotrigine

Effective in bipolar depression; needs slow titration (Stevens-Johnson syndrome risk)

Lamotrigine side effects

Rash (incl. Stevens-Johnson), drowsiness, headache; SJS more common with rapid titration

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine)?

Mood stabilisers are medications used to treat bipolar disorder, both for acute episodes (mania, depression) and for long-term prophylaxis. They include lithium, valproate, lamotrigine, and carbamazepine.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine)?

Key features include: lithium, lithium mechanism, lithium monitoring.

Q3. How is Mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.13.7 Tricyclic Antidepressant Tca Overdose And Serotoni

Definition

TCA overdose is a life-threatening emergency with anticholinergic, cardiovascular, and CNS toxicity. Serotonin discontinuation syndrome is a transient syndrome occurring after abrupt cessation of serotonergic antidepressants, characterised by a constellation of symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

TCA toxicity triad

Anticholinergic, cardiovascular (arrhythmia, hypotension), CNS (seizures, coma)

TCA cardiovascular toxicity

Sodium channel blockade → QRS widening, VT, torsades; hypotension

TCA overdose treatment

Sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; supportive

TCA lethal dose

~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

TCA and serotonin syndrome

TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

Serotonin discontinuation syndrome

Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

Discontinuation timing

Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

Discontinuation treatment

Reintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months

Differentiating discontinuation from relapse

Discontinuation: rapid onset, somatic symptoms, brief; relapse: gradual onset, original symptoms return

Discontinuation prevention

Always taper antidepressants gradually; longer taper for short half-life drugs

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
TCA toxicity triadAnticholinergic, cardiovascular (arrhythmia, hypotension), CNS (seizures, coma)
TCA cardiovascular toxicitySodium channel blockade → QRS widening, VT, torsades; hypotension
TCA overdose treatmentSodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; suppo
TCA lethal dose~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal
TCA and serotonin syndromeTCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk
Serotonin discontinuation syndromeOccurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain za
Discontinuation timingShort-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)
Discontinuation treatmentReintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months
Differentiating discontinuation from relapseDiscontinuation: rapid onset, somatic symptoms, brief; relapse: gradual onset, o
Discontinuation preventionAlways taper antidepressants gradually; longer taper for short half-life drugs

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

TCA overdose is a life-threatening emergency with anticholinergic, cardiovascular, and CNS toxicity. Serotonin discontinuation syndrome is a transient syndrome occurring after abrupt cessation of serotonergic antidepressants, characterised by a constellation of symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Key features include: tca toxicity triad, tca cardiovascular toxicity, tca overdose treatment.

Q3. How is Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

  1. Key feature regarding Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

A) Anticholinergic, cardiovascular (arrhythmia, hypotension), CNS (seizures, coma)

C) Sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; supportive

D) ~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

  1. Key feature regarding Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

A) Sodium channel blockade → QRS widening, VT, torsades; hypotension

B) Sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; supportive

C) ~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

D) TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

Answer: B

  1. Key feature regarding Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

A) Sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; supportive

B) ~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

C) TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

D) Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

A) ~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

B) TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

C) Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

D) Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

Answer: D

  1. Key feature regarding Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

A) TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

B) Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

C) Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

D) Reintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

A) Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

B) Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

C) Reintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months

D) Discontinuation: rapid onset, somatic symptoms, brief; relapse: gradual onset, original sy

Answer: B

A) Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

B) Reintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months

C) Discontinuation: rapid onset, somatic symptoms, brief; relapse: gradual onset, original sy

Flashcards

Q: TCA toxicity triad?

A: Anticholinergic, cardiovascular (arrhythmia, hypotension), CNS (seizures, coma)

Q: TCA cardiovascular toxicity?

A: Sodium channel blockade → QRS widening, VT, torsades; hypotension

Q: TCA overdose treatment?

A: Sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; supportive

Q: TCA lethal dose?

A: ~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

Q: TCA and serotonin syndrome?

A: TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

Q: Serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

A: Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

Q: Discontinuation timing?

A: Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

Q: Discontinuation treatment?

A: Reintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.13.7 Tricyclic Antidepressant Tca Overdose And Serotonin Discontinuation Syndrome

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Psychopharmacology

topic: Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome

Definition

TCA overdose is a life-threatening emergency with anticholinergic, cardiovascular, and CNS toxicity. Serotonin discontinuation syndrome is a transient syndrome occurring after abrupt cessation of serotonergic antidepressants, characterised by a constellation of symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

TCA toxicity triad

Anticholinergic, cardiovascular (arrhythmia, hypotension), CNS (seizures, coma)

TCA cardiovascular toxicity

Sodium channel blockade → QRS widening, VT, torsades; hypotension

TCA overdose treatment

Sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; supportive

TCA lethal dose

~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

TCA and serotonin syndrome

TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

Serotonin discontinuation syndrome

Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

Discontinuation timing

Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

Discontinuation treatment

Reintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months

Differentiating discontinuation from relapse

Discontinuation: rapid onset, somatic symptoms, brief; relapse: gradual onset, original symptoms return

Discontinuation prevention

Always taper antidepressants gradually; longer taper for short half-life drugs

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
TCA toxicity triadAnticholinergic, cardiovascular (arrhythmia, hypotension), CNS (seizures, coma)
TCA cardiovascular toxicitySodium channel blockade → QRS widening, VT, torsades; hypotension
TCA overdose treatmentSodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; suppo
TCA lethal dose~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal
TCA and serotonin syndromeTCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk
Serotonin discontinuation syndromeOccurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain za
Discontinuation timingShort-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)
Discontinuation treatmentReintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months
Differentiating discontinuation from relapseDiscontinuation: rapid onset, somatic symptoms, brief; relapse: gradual onset, o
Discontinuation preventionAlways taper antidepressants gradually; longer taper for short half-life drugs

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

TCA overdose is a life-threatening emergency with anticholinergic, cardiovascular, and CNS toxicity. Serotonin discontinuation syndrome is a transient syndrome occurring after abrupt cessation of serotonergic antidepressants, characterised by a constellation of symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Key features include: tca toxicity triad, tca cardiovascular toxicity, tca overdose treatment.

Q3. How is Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

▸ 29.14 Psychotic Disorders 4
▸ 29.14.1 Delusional Disorder

Definition

Delusional disorder is a psychiatric condition characterised by one or more non-bizarre delusions (involving real-life situations that could occur in real life, e.g., persecution, infidelity, illness) lasting ≥1 month, with functioning relatively preserved outside the delusional theme.

Core Clinical Content

Key feature

Non-bizarre delusions (could occur in real life)

Duration

≥1 month

Types

Persecutory, jealous (Othello), erotomanic (de Clerambault), somatic, grandiose, mixed

Erotomanic

False belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them

Othello syndrome

Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

Somatic

Belief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions

Functioning

Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

Behaviour

Generally normal except for actions based on delusion

Treatment

Antipsychotic (poor response); psychotherapy; management of behaviour

Prognosis

Often chronic; insight often limited

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Key featureNon-bizarre delusions (could occur in real life)
Duration≥1 month
TypesPersecutory, jealous (Othello), erotomanic (de Clerambault), somatic, grandiose,
ErotomanicFalse belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them
Othello syndromePathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence
SomaticBelief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions
FunctioningRelatively preserved outside delusional theme
BehaviourGenerally normal except for actions based on delusion
TreatmentAntipsychotic (poor response); psychotherapy; management of behaviour
PrognosisOften chronic; insight often limited

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Delusional disorder?

Delusional disorder is a psychiatric condition characterised by one or more non-bizarre delusions (involving real-life situations that could occur in real life, e.g., persecution, infidelity, illness) lasting ≥1 month, with functioning relatively preserved outside the delusional theme.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Delusional disorder?

Key features include: key feature, duration, types. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Delusional disorder diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Delusional disorder?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Delusional disorder?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Delusional disorder — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

Mind Map

Delusional disorder
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Features
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute
│   └── Long-term
└── Complications

D) False belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them

  1. Regarding Delusional disorder?

A) ≥1 month

B) Persecutory, jealous (Othello), erotomanic (de Clerambault), somatic, grandiose,

C) False belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them

D) Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true about Delusional disorder?

A) Persecutory, jealous (Othello), erotomanic (de Clerambault), somatic, grandiose,

B) False belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them

C) Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

D) Belief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions

Answer: C

  1. Diagnostic criterion for Delusional disorder?

A) False belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them

B) Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

C) Belief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions

D) Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

Answer: D

  1. Management of Delusional disorder includes?

A) Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

B) Belief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions

C) Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

D) Generally normal except for actions based on delusion

Answer: A

  1. Prognosis of Delusional disorder?

A) Belief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions

B) Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

C) Generally normal except for actions based on delusion

D) Antipsychotic (poor response); psychotherapy; management of behaviour

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Delusional disorder?

A) Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

B) Generally normal except for actions based on delusion

C) Antipsychotic (poor response); psychotherapy; management of behaviour

D) Often chronic; insight often limited

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Delusional disorder?

A) Non-bizarre delusions (could occur in real life)

B) Persecutory, jealous (Othello), erotomanic (de Clerambault), somatic, grandiose,

C) Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

D) Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Delusional disorder?

A) ≥1 month

B) False belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them

C) Belief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions

D) Generally normal except for actions based on delusion

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Delusional disorder?

A) Persecutory, jealous (Othello), erotomanic (de Clerambault), somatic, grandiose,

B) Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

C) Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

D) Antipsychotic (poor response); psychotherapy; management of behaviour

Answer: C

▸ 29.14.2 Schizoaffective Disorder And Related Psychotic Dis

Definition

Schizoaffective disorder is a psychotic disorder with prominent mood symptoms (depressive or manic) coexisting with schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms, but with ≥2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

Schizoaffective criteria

Major mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Distinguishing from schizophrenia

Prominent mood symptoms (not just brief)

Distinguishing from bipolar/ MDD with psychotic features

≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Types

Bipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive episodes)

Schizophreniform disorder

Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

Brief psychotic disorder

Psychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning

Delusional disorder

Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning preserved

Treatment

Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-term

Prognosis

Better than schizophrenia; worse than mood disorder alone

Schizoaffective prevalence

Rare (~0.3%); often misdiagnosed

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Schizoaffective criteriaMajor mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood sy
Distinguishing from schizophreniaProminent mood symptoms (not just brief)
Distinguishing from bipolar/ MDD with psychotic fe≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms
TypesBipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive
Schizophreniform disorderSame as schizophrenia but <6 months duration
Brief psychotic disorderPsychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning
Delusional disorderNon-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning pre
TreatmentMood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-te
PrognosisBetter than schizophrenia; worse than mood disorder alone
Schizoaffective prevalenceRare (~0.3%); often misdiagnosed

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

Schizoaffective disorder is a psychotic disorder with prominent mood symptoms (depressive or manic) coexisting with schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms, but with ≥2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

Key features include: schizoaffective criteria, distinguishing from schizophrenia, distinguishing from bipolar/ mdd with psychotic features. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

A) ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

B) Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

C) Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning pre

Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders

Definition

Schizoaffective disorder is a psychotic disorder with prominent mood symptoms (depressive or manic) coexisting with schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms, but with ≥2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

Schizoaffective criteria

Major mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Distinguishing from schizophrenia

Prominent mood symptoms (not just brief)

Distinguishing from bipolar/ MDD with psychotic features

≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Types

Bipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive episodes)

Schizophreniform disorder

Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

Brief psychotic disorder

Psychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning

Delusional disorder

Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning preserved

Treatment

Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-term

Prognosis

Better than schizophrenia; worse than mood disorder alone

Schizoaffective prevalence

Rare (~0.3%); often misdiagnosed
