Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

Schizoaffective disorder is a psychotic disorder with prominent mood symptoms (depressive or manic) coexisting with schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms, but with ≥2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

Key features include: schizoaffective criteria, distinguishing from schizophrenia, distinguishing from bipolar/ mdd with psychotic features. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

  1. Prognosis of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

A) Psychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning

B) Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning pre

C) Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-te

D) Better than schizophrenia; worse than mood disorder alone

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

A) Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning pre

B) Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-te

C) Better than schizophrenia; worse than mood disorder alone

D) Rare (~0.3%); often misdiagnosed

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

A) Major mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood sy

B) ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

C) Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

D) Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning pre

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

A) Prominent mood symptoms (not just brief)

B) Bipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive

C) Psychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning

D) Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-te

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders?

A) ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

B) Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

C) Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning pre

D) Better than schizophrenia; worse than mood disorder alone

Answer: C

SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient presents with features of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders. Most appropriate first-line? | Major mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood sy
  1. Which investigation is most useful in Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  1. What is the prevalence of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | Prominent mood symptoms (not just brief)
  1. First-line management of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | Major mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood sy
  1. Which is a recognised risk factor for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms
  1. What is the typical course of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  1. Which complication should be screened for in Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  1. Duration criterion for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | Bipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive
  1. Most appropriate referral for Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders? | Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  1. Differential diagnosis of Schizoaffective disorder and related psychotic disorders includes? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

Flashcards

Q: Schizoaffective criteria?

A: Major mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Q: Distinguishing from schizophrenia?

A: Prominent mood symptoms (not just brief)

Q: Distinguishing from bipolar/ MDD with psychotic features?

A: ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Q: Types?

A: Bipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive episodes)

Q: Schizophreniform disorder?

A: Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

Q: Brief psychotic disorder?

A: Psychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning

Q: Delusional disorder?

A: Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning preserved

Q: Treatment?

A: Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-term

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.14.3 Schizophrenia

Definition

Schizophrenia is a chronic psychiatric disorder characterised by positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech), negative symptoms (avolition, alogia, flat affect), and cognitive impairment, lasting ≥6 months with ≥1 month of active symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥2 of 5 symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, grossly disorganised/catatonic behaviour, negative symptoms); ≥1 must be delusions/hallucinations/disorganised speech

Duration

≥6 months (≥1 month active phase)

Positive symptoms

Hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech, bizarre behaviour

Negative symptoms

Avolition, alogia, flat affect, anhedonia, asociality

First-rank symptoms (Schneider)

Auditory hallucinations (voices commenting, discussing, thought echo); thought insertion/withdrawal/broadcasting; delusions of control/passivity

Prevalence

Lifetime ~1%; equal M:F; onset M 18-25, F 25-35

Risk factors

Family history (8-10% if first-degree), obstetric complications, cannabis use, urban birth

Dopamine hypothesis

↑ Dopamine in mesolimbic pathway (positive); ↓ in mesocortical (negative)

First-line treatment

Antipsychotic (oral or LAI) + psychosocial support

Suicide risk

~5-10% lifetime; highest in first 5 years and after discharge

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteria≥2 of 5 symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, grossly disorg
Duration≥6 months (≥1 month active phase)
Positive symptomsHallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech, bizarre behaviour
Negative symptomsAvolition, alogia, flat affect, anhedonia, asociality
First-rank symptoms (Schneider)Auditory hallucinations (voices commenting, discussing, thought echo); thought i
PrevalenceLifetime ~1%; equal M:F; onset M 18-25, F 25-35
Risk factorsFamily history (8-10% if first-degree), obstetric complications, cannabis use, u
Dopamine hypothesis↑ Dopamine in mesolimbic pathway (positive); ↓ in mesocortical (negative)
First-line treatmentAntipsychotic (oral or LAI) + psychosocial support
Suicide risk~5-10% lifetime; highest in first 5 years and after discharge

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Schizophrenia?

Schizophrenia is a chronic psychiatric disorder characterised by positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech), negative symptoms (avolition, alogia, flat affect), and cognitive impairment, lasting ≥6 months with ≥1 month of active symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Schizophrenia?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, duration, positive symptoms. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Schizophrenia diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Schizophrenia?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Schizophrenia?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Schizophrenia — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.15 Sexual and Gender Disorders 2
▸ 29.15.1 Gender Dysphoria And Paraphilias

Definition

Gender dysphoria is distress caused by a marked incongruence between experienced/expressed gender and assigned gender, lasting ≥6 months. Paraphilias are persistent, intense, atypical sexual interests; paraphilic disorders cause harm/distress.

Core Clinical Content

Gender dysphoria definition

Distress from incongruence between experienced gender and assigned gender; ≥6 months

Gender dysphoria onset

Often childhood; some present in adolescence/adulthood

GD treatment in children

Reversible interventions (puberty blockers at Tanner stage 2); supportive care

GD treatment in adults

Affirming care; hormones, surgery, psychological support; no conversion therapy

WPATH standards

International guidelines for transgender health; individualised, multi-disciplinary

Paraphilias vs disorders

Paraphilia: atypical interest; paraphilic disorder: causes harm/distress/impairment

Common paraphilias

Paedophilia, exhibitionism, voyeurism, fetishism, frotteurism, sexual masochism/sadism

Paraphilic disorder assessment

Risk assessment (especially for child-related interests); treatment often court-mandated

Treatment

CBT, relapse prevention; anti-androgens or GnRH agonists for severe paraphilias

Co-occurring conditions

Substance use, depression, personality disorders, other paraphilias

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Gender dysphoria definitionDistress from incongruence between experienced gender and assigned gender; ≥6 mo
Gender dysphoria onsetOften childhood; some present in adolescence/adulthood
GD treatment in childrenReversible interventions (puberty blockers at Tanner stage 2); supportive care
GD treatment in adultsAffirming care; hormones, surgery, psychological support; no conversion therapy
WPATH standardsInternational guidelines for transgender health; individualised, multi-disciplin
Paraphilias vs disordersParaphilia: atypical interest; paraphilic disorder: causes harm/distress/impairm

Paraphilic disorder assessmentRisk assessment (especially for child-related interests); treatment often court-
TreatmentCBT, relapse prevention; anti-androgens or GnRH agonists for severe paraphilias
Co-occurring conditionsSubstance use, depression, personality disorders, other paraphilias

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gender dysphoria and paraphilias?

Gender dysphoria is distress caused by a marked incongruence between experienced/expressed gender and assigned gender, lasting ≥6 months. Paraphilias are persistent, intense, atypical sexual interests; paraphilic disorders cause harm/distress.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gender dysphoria and paraphilias?

Key features include: gender dysphoria definition, gender dysphoria onset, gd treatment in children.

Q3. How is Gender dysphoria and paraphilias diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gender dysphoria and paraphilias?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Gender dysphoria and paraphilias?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gender dysphoria and paraphilias — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.15.2 Sexual Dysfunction

Definition

Sexual dysfunctions are a group of disorders characterised by clinically significant disturbance in sexual response or experience, including desire, arousal, orgasm, and pain disorders, causing marked distress.

Core Clinical Content

Types

Desire (low), arousal (erectile, female), orgasm (premature, delayed, anorgasmia), pain (dyspareunia, vaginismus)

Erectile dysfunction (ED)

Inability to achieve/maintain erection; organic vs psychogenic causes

ED organic causes

Vascular (atherosclerosis), diabetes, hormonal, neurological, medications, alcohol

ED psychogenic causes

Anxiety (performance), depression, relationship problems, stress

ED investigations

Testosterone, prolactin, glucose, lipids, TSH; nocturnal penile tumescence test

ED treatment

PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) first-line; vacuum device; intracavernosal injection; implant

Premature ejaculation

Ejaculation sooner than desired; behavioural therapy, dapoxetine (SSRI)

Vaginismus

Involuntary vaginal muscle contraction preventing penetration; pelvic floor therapy, CBT

Anorgasmia in women

Often psychological; ensure adequate stimulation, address anxiety

Sexual dysfunction in psychiatric illness

Common in depression, anxiety, PTSD; screen routinely

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Sexual dysfunction?

Sexual dysfunctions are a group of disorders characterised by clinically significant disturbance in sexual response or experience, including desire, arousal, orgasm, and pain disorders, causing marked distress.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Sexual dysfunction?

Key features include: types, erectile dysfunction (ed), ed organic causes.

Q3. How is Sexual dysfunction diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Sexual dysfunction?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Sexual dysfunction?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sexual dysfunction — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.16 Sleep Disorders 4
▸ 29.16.1 Insomnia Disorder

Definition

Insomnia disorder is a sleep-wake disorder characterised by dissatisfaction with sleep quantity or quality, with difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early morning awakening, causing significant daytime impairment, occurring ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Dissatisfaction with sleep quantity/quality; ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months; daytime impairment; not better explained by another condition

Types

Sleep-onset, sleep-maintenance, early-morning awakening, mixed

Prevalence

Common (~30% adults have symptoms; 10% chronic)

Causes

Stress, medical conditions, psychiatric disorders, medications, substance use, poor sleep hygiene

3P model

Predisposing, Precipitating, Perpetuating factors

Sleep hygiene

Regular schedule, dark/cool room, no caffeine/alcohol/exercise before bed, no screens

CBT-I

Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia; first-line; lasting benefit

CBT-I components

Stimulus control, sleep restriction, relaxation, cognitive therapy, sleep hygiene

Pharmacotherapy

Short-term only: Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone) or melatonin; AVOID long-term benzodiazepines

Dependence risk

High with benzodiazepines and Z-drugs; reserved for short-term use

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Insomnia disorder?

Insomnia disorder is a sleep-wake disorder characterised by dissatisfaction with sleep quantity or quality, with difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early morning awakening, causing significant daytime impairment, occurring ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Insomnia disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, types, prevalence.

Q3. How is Insomnia disorder diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Insomnia disorder?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Insomnia disorder?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

▸ 29.16.2 Obstructive Sleep Apnoea And Narcolepsy

Definition

Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) is a sleep-related breathing disorder characterised by repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep causing apnoeas/hypopnoeas, oxygen desaturation, and sleep fragmentation. Narcolepsy is a central hypersomnolence disorder with excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations.

Core Clinical Content

OSA definition

Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea

OSA risk factors

Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

OSA symptoms

Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

OSA diagnosis

Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

OSA complications

Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

OSA treatment

CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (UPPP)

Narcolepsy tetrad

Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

Narcolepsy type 1

With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

Narcolepsy type 2

Without cataplexy

Narcolepsy treatment

Modafinil (first-line); sodium oxybate (for cataplexy); modafinil, methylphenidate

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
OSA definitionRepetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea
OSA risk factorsObesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tons
OSA symptomsSnoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentra
OSA diagnosisPolysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)
OSA complicationsHypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression
OSA treatmentCPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device;
Narcolepsy tetradExcessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinati
Narcolepsy type 1With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin
Narcolepsy type 2Without cataplexy
Narcolepsy treatmentModafinil (first-line); sodium oxybate (for cataplexy); modafinil, methylphenida

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) is a sleep-related breathing disorder characterised by repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep causing apnoeas/hypopnoeas, oxygen desaturation, and sleep fragmentation. Narcolepsy is a central hypersomnolence disorder with excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

Key features include: osa definition, osa risk factors, osa symptoms.

Q3. How is Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

B) Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

C) Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

D) Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

B) Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

C) Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

D) Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

Answer: B

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

B) Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

C) Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

D) CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

B) Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

C) CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (

D) Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

Answer: D

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

B) CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (

C) Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

D) With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (

B) Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

C) With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

D) Without cataplexy

Answer: B

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

B) With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

C) Without cataplexy

D) Modafinil (first-line); sodium oxybate (for cataplexy); modafinil, methylphenidate

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

B) Without cataplexy

C) Modafinil (first-line); sodium oxybate (for cataplexy); modafinil, methylphenidate

D) Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea

Answer: D

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) Without cataplexy

B) Modafinil (first-line); sodium oxybate (for cataplexy); modafinil, methylphenidate

C) Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea

D) Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy?

A) Modafinil (first-line); sodium oxybate (for cataplexy); modafinil, methylphenidate

B) Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea

C) Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

D) Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

Answer: B

Flashcards

Q: OSA definition?

A: Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea

Q: OSA risk factors?

A: Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

Q: OSA symptoms?

A: Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

Q: OSA diagnosis?

A: Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

Q: OSA complications?

A: Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

Q: OSA treatment?

A: CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (UPPP)

Q: Narcolepsy tetrad?

A: Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

Q: Narcolepsy type 1?

A: With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.16.3 Other Sleep Disorders Parasomnias Rbd Restless Leg

Definition

Other sleep disorders include parasomnias (abnormal behaviours during sleep — NREM and REM), REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD), and restless legs syndrome (RLS), each with distinct features and management.

Core Clinical Content

Parasomnias

Abnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM behaviour disorder)

Sleepwalking (somnambulism)

NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia

Sleep terrors

NREM parasomnia; screaming, autonomic arousal, no memory; common in children

REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD)

Loss of normal REM atonia; acting out dreams; risk of injury

RBD association

Strong marker for α-synucleinopathies (Parkinson's, DLB, MSA)

RBD treatment

Clonazepam, melatonin; bed safety

Restless legs syndrome (RLS)

Urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement

RLS associations

Iron deficiency (low ferritin), pregnancy, CKD, peripheral neuropathy

RLS treatment

Iron replacement if ferritin <75; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); gabapentin

Night terrors vs nightmares

Night terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ParasomniasAbnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM
Sleepwalking (somnambulism)NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia
Sleep terrorsNREM parasomnia; screaming, autonomic arousal, no memory; common in children
REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD)Loss of normal REM atonia; acting out dreams; risk of injury
RBD associationStrong marker for α-synucleinopathies (Parkinson's, DLB, MSA)
RBD treatmentClonazepam, melatonin; bed safety
Restless legs syndrome (RLS)Urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement
RLS associationsIron deficiency (low ferritin), pregnancy, CKD, peripheral neuropathy
RLS treatmentIron replacement if ferritin <75; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); g
Night terrors vs nightmaresNight terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

Other sleep disorders include parasomnias (abnormal behaviours during sleep — NREM and REM), REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD), and restless legs syndrome (RLS), each with distinct features and management.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

Key features include: parasomnias, sleepwalking (somnambulism), sleep terrors.

Q3. How is Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

modified: 2026-06-16

Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)

Definition

Other sleep disorders include parasomnias (abnormal behaviours during sleep — NREM and REM), REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD), and restless legs syndrome (RLS), each with distinct features and management.

Core Clinical Content

Parasomnias

Abnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM behaviour disorder)

Sleepwalking (somnambulism)

NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia

Sleep terrors

NREM parasomnia; screaming, autonomic arousal, no memory; common in children

REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD)

Loss of normal REM atonia; acting out dreams; risk of injury

RBD association

Strong marker for α-synucleinopathies (Parkinson's, DLB, MSA)

RBD treatment

Clonazepam, melatonin; bed safety

Restless legs syndrome (RLS)

Urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement

RLS associations

Iron deficiency (low ferritin), pregnancy, CKD, peripheral neuropathy

RLS treatment

Iron replacement if ferritin <75; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); gabapentin

Night terrors vs nightmares

Night terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

Other sleep disorders include parasomnias (abnormal behaviours during sleep — NREM and REM), REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD), and restless legs syndrome (RLS), each with distinct features and management.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

Key features include: parasomnias, sleepwalking (somnambulism), sleep terrors.

Q3. How is Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

A) Urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement

B) Iron deficiency (low ferritin), pregnancy, CKD, peripheral neuropathy

C) Iron replacement if ferritin <75; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); gabapentin

D) Night terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

A) Iron deficiency (low ferritin), pregnancy, CKD, peripheral neuropathy

B) Iron replacement if ferritin <75; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); gabapentin

C) Night terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

D) Abnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM behaviour

Answer: D

  1. Key feature regarding Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

A) Iron replacement if ferritin <75; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); gabapentin

B) Night terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

C) Abnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM behaviour

D) NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs)?

A) Night terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

B) Abnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM behaviour

C) NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia

D) NREM parasomnia; screaming, autonomic arousal, no memory; common in children

Answer: B

  1. Patient presents with features of Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs). Which is most appropriate? | NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia

  1. Patient presents with features of Other sleep disorders (parasomnias, RBD, restless legs). Which is most appropriate? | Clonazepam, melatonin; bed safety

Flashcards

Q: Parasomnias?

A: Abnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM behaviour disorder)

Q: Sleepwalking (somnambulism)?

A: NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia

Q: Sleep terrors?

A: NREM parasomnia; screaming, autonomic arousal, no memory; common in children

Q: REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD)?

A: Loss of normal REM atonia; acting out dreams; risk of injury

Q: RBD association?

A: Strong marker for α-synucleinopathies (Parkinson's, DLB, MSA)

Q: RBD treatment?

A: Clonazepam, melatonin; bed safety

Q: Restless legs syndrome (RLS)?

A: Urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement

Q: RLS associations?

A: Iron deficiency (low ferritin), pregnancy, CKD, peripheral neuropathy

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  3. C — Sleep terrors
  4. D — REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD)
  5. A — RBD association
  6. B — RBD treatment
  7. C — Restless legs syndrome (RLS)
  8. D — RLS associations
  9. A — RLS treatment
  10. B — Night terrors vs nightmares

▸ 29.17 Special Populations and Liaison 10
▸ 29.17.1 Adult Adhd And Tic Disorders

Definition

Adult attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder persisting into adulthood, characterised by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, are characterised by sudden, rapid, recurrent motor movements or vocalisations.

Core Clinical Content

Adult ADHD prevalence

~3-5% of adults; 50% of childhood ADHD persists into adulthood

ADHD triad

Inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity; ICD-11 also includes emotional dysregulation

Adult presentation

Inattention predominates; hyperactivity less prominent; emotional dysregulation

Comorbidities

Anxiety, depression, substance use, OCD, personality disorders, sleep disorders

Diagnosis

Clinical; DIVA-5 or ACE+; collateral history important; consider QB tests

Treatment

Stimulants (methylphenidate, lisdexamfetamine) first-line; atomoxetine (non-stimulant)

Stimulant side effects

Appetite ↓, weight loss, insomnia, ↑ HR/BP, tics; abuse potential

Atomoxetine

Non-stimulant; less effective; useful in substance use comorbidity

Tourette syndrome

Multiple motor + ≥1 vocal tics; >1 year; onset <18; ADHD 50%

Tourette treatment

Behavioural therapy first; α2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine); antipsychotics for severe

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

ADHD triadInattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity; ICD-11 also includes emotional dysregul
Adult presentationInattention predominates; hyperactivity less prominent; emotional dysregulation
ComorbiditiesAnxiety, depression, substance use, OCD, personality disorders, sleep disorders
DiagnosisClinical; DIVA-5 or ACE+; collateral history important; consider QB tests
TreatmentStimulants (methylphenidate, lisdexamfetamine) first-line; atomoxetine (non-stim

Tourette syndromeMultiple motor + ≥1 vocal tics; >1 year; onset <18; ADHD 50%

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Adult ADHD and tic disorders?

Adult attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder persisting into adulthood, characterised by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, are characterised by sudden, rapid, recurrent motor movements or vocalisations.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Adult ADHD and tic disorders?

Key features include: adult adhd prevalence, adhd triad, adult presentation.

Q3. How is Adult ADHD and tic disorders diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Adult ADHD and tic disorders?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Adult ADHD and tic disorders?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adult ADHD and tic disorders — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.17.2 Autism Spectrum Disorder And Intellectual Disabili

Definition

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behaviour, interests, or activities. Intellectual disability (ID) is characterised by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behaviour, with onset in the developmental period.

Core Clinical Content

ASD prevalence

~1-2% of children; 4:1 male:female ratio

ASD triad (DSM-5)

Persistent deficits in: (1) social communication/interaction, (2) restricted/repetitive behaviours, (3) early onset

ASD onset

Recognisable in early childhood; some regression in language/social skills

ASD severity levels

Level 1: requiring support; Level 2: substantial support; Level 3: very substantial support

ASD comorbidities

Intellectual disability (~30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, OCD, tics, epilepsy

ASD causes

Mostly genetic; specific genes (SHANK3, CHD8, FMR1); environmental (advanced parental age)

ASD management

Early intervention; ABA (applied behaviour analysis); speech therapy; OT; school support; SSRI for OCD/anxiety

ASD pharmacotherapy

No curative medication; antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole) for irritability; melatonin for sleep

Intellectual disability (ID)

IQ <70; deficits in adaptive functioning; onset <18 years

ID severity

Mild (IQ 50-69), moderate (35-49), severe (20-34), profound (<20)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ASD prevalence~1-2% of children; 4:1 male:female ratio
ASD triad (DSM-5)Persistent deficits in: (1) social communication/interaction, (2) restricted/rep

ASD severity levelsLevel 1: requiring support; Level 2: substantial support; Level 3: very substant

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behaviour, interests, or activities. Intellectual disability (ID) is characterised by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behaviour, with onset in the developmental period.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Key features include: asd prevalence, asd triad (dsm-5), asd onset.

Q3. How is Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

A: Level 1: requiring support; Level 2: substantial support; Level 3: very substantial support

Q: ASD comorbidities?

A: Intellectual disability (~30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, OCD, tics, epilepsy

Q: ASD causes?

A: Mostly genetic; specific genes (SHANK3, CHD8, FMR1); environmental (advanced parental age)

Q: ASD management?

A: Early intervention; ABA (applied behaviour analysis); speech therapy; OT; school support; SSRI for O

Q: ASD pharmacotherapy?

A: No curative medication; antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole) for irritability; melatonin for sl

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  2. B — ASD triad (DSM-5)
  3. C — ASD onset

▸ 29.17.2 Autism Spectrum Disorder And Intellectual Disability

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Special Populations and Liaison

topic: Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability

Definition

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behaviour, interests, or activities. Intellectual disability (ID) is characterised by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behaviour, with onset in the developmental period.

Core Clinical Content

ASD prevalence

~1-2% of children; 4:1 male:female ratio

ASD triad (DSM-5)

Persistent deficits in: (1) social communication/interaction, (2) restricted/repetitive behaviours, (3) early onset

ASD onset

Recognisable in early childhood; some regression in language/social skills

ASD severity levels

Level 1: requiring support; Level 2: substantial support; Level 3: very substantial support

ASD comorbidities

Intellectual disability (~30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, OCD, tics, epilepsy

ASD causes

Mostly genetic; specific genes (SHANK3, CHD8, FMR1); environmental (advanced parental age)

ASD management

Early intervention; ABA (applied behaviour analysis); speech therapy; OT; school support; SSRI for OCD/anxiety

ASD pharmacotherapy

No curative medication; antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole) for irritability; melatonin for sleep

Intellectual disability (ID)

IQ <70; deficits in adaptive functioning; onset <18 years

ID severity

Mild (IQ 50-69), moderate (35-49), severe (20-34), profound (<20)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

ASD triad (DSM-5)Persistent deficits in: (1) social communication/interaction, (2) restricted/rep
ASD onsetRecognisable in early childhood; some regression in language/social skills
ASD severity levelsLevel 1: requiring support; Level 2: substantial support; Level 3: very substant

ASD managementEarly intervention; ABA (applied behaviour analysis); speech therapy; OT; school

Intellectual disability (ID)IQ <70; deficits in adaptive functioning; onset <18 years
ID severityMild (IQ 50-69), moderate (35-49), severe (20-34), profound (<20)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behaviour, interests, or activities. Intellectual disability (ID) is characterised by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behaviour, with onset in the developmental period.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Key features include: asd prevalence, asd triad (dsm-5), asd onset.

Q3. How is Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

▸ 29.17.3 Child And Adolescent Psychiatry Overview

Definition

Child and adolescent psychiatry addresses mental health conditions in children and adolescents, with disorders often presenting differently from adults and requiring developmentally appropriate assessment and treatment.

Core Clinical Content

ADHD

Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder; 5% children; inattentive/hyperactive/combined; stimulants first-line

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD)

Persistent deficits in social communication + restricted/repetitive behaviours; early onset

Conduct disorder

Repetitive violation of others' rights/age-appropriate norms; <18 years; precursor to ASPD

Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD)

Angry/irritable mood, argumentative, vindictive behaviour; less severe than CD

Tourette syndrome

Multiple motor + ≥1 vocal tics; >1 year; onset <18; ADHD comorbidity 50%

Specific learning disorder

Persistent difficulties in reading (dyslexia), writing, mathematics

Enuresis

Involuntary urination ≥2/week in ≥5-year-old; primary vs secondary; enuresis alarm first-line

Encopresis

Faecal incontinence; usually functional; consider abuse if secondary

Adolescent depression

Often masked (irritability, somatic); ↑ suicide risk; SSRIs (fluoxetine approved)

Eating disorders in adolescents

Family-based treatment (FBT) first-line; medical stabilisation priority

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Child and adolescent psychiatry overview?

Child and adolescent psychiatry addresses mental health conditions in children and adolescents, with disorders often presenting differently from adults and requiring developmentally appropriate assessment and treatment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Child and adolescent psychiatry overview?

Key features include: adhd, autism spectrum disorder (asd), conduct disorder.

Q3. How is Child and adolescent psychiatry overview diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Child and adolescent psychiatry overview?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Child and adolescent psychiatry overview?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Child and adolescent psychiatry overview — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.17.4 Conduct Disorder And Oppositional Defiant Disorder

Definition

Conduct disorder (CD) is characterised by repetitive and persistent patterns of behaviour that violate the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting ≥6 months, less severe than CD.

Core Clinical Content

ODD features

Angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, vindictiveness ≥6 months

ODD prevalence

~3-5% of children; equal M:F before puberty; more males after

ODD vs CD

ODD: defiance, hostility, no major rights violations; CD: violates others' rights/norms

Conduct disorder (CD)

Aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, serious rule violation

CD prevalence

~4-5% of children; 3-4x more common in males

CD course

Often persists; precursor to antisocial personality disorder in adulthood (≥18 years)

CD risk factors

Genetic (heritability ~50%), neurobiological (↓ MAO-A), family/parenting, socioeconomic, peer

Comorbidities

ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disorders

Treatment

Parent management training (PMT) - first-line; cognitive problem-solving; multi-systemic therapy; SSRI for aggression

Prognosis

Better in ODD (1/3 remit); worse in CD with high callous-unemotional traits (poor prognosis)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ODD featuresAngry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, vindictiveness ≥6 months
ODD prevalence~3-5% of children; equal M:F before puberty; more males after
ODD vs CDODD: defiance, hostility, no major rights violations; CD: violates others' right
Conduct disorder (CD)Aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, seri
CD prevalence~4-5% of children; 3-4x more common in males
CD courseOften persists; precursor to antisocial personality disorder in adulthood (≥18 y
CD risk factorsGenetic (heritability ~50%), neurobiological (↓ MAO-A), family/parenting, socioe
ComorbiditiesADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disorders
TreatmentParent management training (PMT) - first-line; cognitive problem-solving; multi-
PrognosisBetter in ODD (1/3 remit); worse in CD with high callous-unemotional traits (poo

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Conduct disorder (CD) is characterised by repetitive and persistent patterns of behaviour that violate the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting ≥6 months, less severe than CD.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Key features include: odd features, odd prevalence, odd vs cd.

Q3. How is Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

▸ 29.17.4 Conduct Disorder And Oppositional Defiant Disorder In Children

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Special Populations and Liaison

topic: Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children

Definition

Conduct disorder (CD) is characterised by repetitive and persistent patterns of behaviour that violate the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting ≥6 months, less severe than CD.

Core Clinical Content

ODD features

Angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, vindictiveness ≥6 months

ODD prevalence

~3-5% of children; equal M:F before puberty; more males after

ODD vs CD

ODD: defiance, hostility, no major rights violations; CD: violates others' rights/norms

Conduct disorder (CD)

Aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, serious rule violation

CD prevalence

~4-5% of children; 3-4x more common in males

CD course

Often persists; precursor to antisocial personality disorder in adulthood (≥18 years)

CD risk factors

Genetic (heritability ~50%), neurobiological (↓ MAO-A), family/parenting, socioeconomic, peer

Comorbidities

ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disorders

Treatment

Parent management training (PMT) - first-line; cognitive problem-solving; multi-systemic therapy; SSRI for aggression

Prognosis

Better in ODD (1/3 remit); worse in CD with high callous-unemotional traits (poor prognosis)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

ODD featuresAngry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, vindictiveness ≥6 months
ODD prevalence~3-5% of children; equal M:F before puberty; more males after
ODD vs CDODD: defiance, hostility, no major rights violations; CD: violates others' right
Conduct disorder (CD)Aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, seri

CD risk factorsGenetic (heritability ~50%), neurobiological (↓ MAO-A), family/parenting, socioe
ComorbiditiesADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disorders
TreatmentParent management training (PMT) - first-line; cognitive problem-solving; multi-
PrognosisBetter in ODD (1/3 remit); worse in CD with high callous-unemotional traits (poo

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Conduct disorder (CD) is characterised by repetitive and persistent patterns of behaviour that violate the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting ≥6 months, less severe than CD.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Key features include: odd features, odd prevalence, odd vs cd.

Q3. How is Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

▸ 29.17.5 Old Age Psychiatry And Dementia Syndromes

Definition

Old age psychiatry addresses mental health in older adults, including dementia, late-life depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD). Comorbid physical illness and polypharmacy are common considerations.

Core Clinical Content

Late-life depression

Common but underdiagnosed; often somatic; may present as pseudodementia

Pseudodementia

Cognitive impairment from depression; reversible; differs from dementia (depressed says 'I don't know')

BPSD

Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia; non-cognitive symptoms

BPSD features

Agitation, aggression, psychosis, depression, wandering, sleep disturbance

BPSD non-pharmacological

Person-centred care, environment, activities, communication strategies

BPSD pharmacological

Antipsychotic (risperidone, haloperidol) only if severe/aggressive; risk of cerebrovascular events

Antipsychotic in dementia

↑ Mortality, ↑ CVA risk; only use after non-pharmacological, shortest time, regular review

Depression in elderly

SSRI first-line; avoid TCAs (anticholinergic, falls); longer time to response

Sleep in elderly

Reduced need, fragmented; non-pharmacological first; melatonin may help

Polypharmacy

Common; review regularly; anticholinergic burden affects cognition

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Late-life depressionCommon but underdiagnosed; often somatic; may present as pseudodementia
PseudodementiaCognitive impairment from depression; reversible; differs from dementia (depress
BPSDBehavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia; non-cognitive symptoms
BPSD featuresAgitation, aggression, psychosis, depression, wandering, sleep disturbance
BPSD non-pharmacologicalPerson-centred care, environment, activities, communication strategies
BPSD pharmacologicalAntipsychotic (risperidone, haloperidol) only if severe/aggressive; risk of cere
Antipsychotic in dementia↑ Mortality, ↑ CVA risk; only use after non-pharmacological, shortest time, regu
Depression in elderlySSRI first-line; avoid TCAs (anticholinergic, falls); longer time to response
Sleep in elderlyReduced need, fragmented; non-pharmacological first; melatonin may help
PolypharmacyCommon; review regularly; anticholinergic burden affects cognition

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes?

Old age psychiatry addresses mental health in older adults, including dementia, late-life depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD). Comorbid physical illness and polypharmacy are common considerations.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes?

Key features include: late-life depression, pseudodementia, bpsd.

Q3. How is Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Q: Depression in elderly?

A: SSRI first-line; avoid TCAs (anticholinergic, falls); longer time to response

▸ 29.17.6 Psychiatric Aspects Of Physical Illness

Definition

Psychiatric conditions frequently co-occur with physical illness, either as a consequence of the medical condition, its treatment, or as an independent comorbidity. Recognition and management are essential for outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Cardiovascular disease

Depression ↑ mortality in post-MI; SSRI (sertraline) safe; caution with TCAs (cardiotoxic)

Stroke

Post-stroke depression 30-40%; peaks 3-6 months; often missed; treat with SSRI

Cancer

Depression in 20-40%; screen with PHQ-9; SSRI first-line; avoid in tamoxifen (paroxetine, fluoxetine)

Diabetes

Depression ↑ risk; ↑ poor control; consider SSRI

Parkinson's disease

Depression in 40-50%; SSRI (caution with tremor); avoid antipsychotics (EPS)

Epilepsy

Depression ↑ suicide risk; avoid TCAs, lowering seizure threshold; consider CBT

HIV/AIDS

Depression, anxiety, psychosis; high suicide risk; screen all HIV patients

Endocrine

Hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, Cushing's, Addison's — all can present with psychiatric symptoms

Vitamin deficiencies

B12, folate, thiamine (Wernicke), vitamin D — all linked to depression/cognition

Steroid-induced psychosis

High-dose corticosteroids; treat by reducing dose; antipsychotic if needed

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

StrokePost-stroke depression 30-40%; peaks 3-6 months; often missed; treat with SSRI
CancerDepression in 20-40%; screen with PHQ-9; SSRI first-line; avoid in tamoxifen (pa
DiabetesDepression ↑ risk; ↑ poor control; consider SSRI
Parkinson's diseaseDepression in 40-50%; SSRI (caution with tremor); avoid antipsychotics (EPS)
EpilepsyDepression ↑ suicide risk; avoid TCAs, lowering seizure threshold; consider CBT
HIV/AIDSDepression, anxiety, psychosis; high suicide risk; screen all HIV patients
EndocrineHypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, Cushing's, Addison's — all can present with psy
Vitamin deficienciesB12, folate, thiamine (Wernicke), vitamin D — all linked to depression/cognition
Steroid-induced psychosisHigh-dose corticosteroids; treat by reducing dose; antipsychotic if needed

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Psychiatric aspects of physical illness?

Psychiatric conditions frequently co-occur with physical illness, either as a consequence of the medical condition, its treatment, or as an independent comorbidity. Recognition and management are essential for outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Psychiatric aspects of physical illness?

Key features include: cardiovascular disease, stroke, cancer.

Q3. How is Psychiatric aspects of physical illness diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Psychiatric aspects of physical illness?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Psychiatric aspects of physical illness?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Psychiatric aspects of physical illness — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.17.7 Psychiatric Aspects Of Pregnancy And Postpartum

Definition

Perinatal psychiatry addresses mental health conditions occurring during pregnancy (antenatal) and up to one year postpartum (postnatal). Conditions include baby blues, postpartum depression, puerperal psychosis, and pre-existing psychiatric conditions in pregnancy.

Core Clinical Content

Antenatal depression

10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression, lack of support

Baby blues

50-80%; 3-5 days postpartum; mild; resolves by 10-14 days

Postpartum depression

10-15%; within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; same criteria as MDD

Puerperal psychosis

1-2 per 1000; psychiatric emergency; within 2 weeks; high suicide/infanticide risk

Treatment in pregnancy

SSRI (sertraline preferred); avoid paroxetine (cardiac malformations); ECT if severe

Treatment in breastfeeding

Sertraline preferred (lowest infant levels); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines, lamotrigine

Antidepressant in pregnancy

Risks: persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn, neonatal adaptation syndrome

Valproate in pregnancy

HIGHLY teratogenic (neural tube defects); AVOID in women of childbearing age

Lithium in pregnancy

Ebstein anomaly (cardiac malformation); avoid in 1st trimester; use cautiously

Perinatal mental health team

Joint obstetric-psychiatric care; mother-baby unit if needed

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Antenatal depression10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression,
Baby blues50-80%; 3-5 days postpartum; mild; resolves by 10-14 days
Postpartum depression10-15%; within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; same criteria as MDD
Puerperal psychosis1-2 per 1000; psychiatric emergency; within 2 weeks; high suicide/infanticide ri
Treatment in pregnancySSRI (sertraline preferred); avoid paroxetine (cardiac malformations); ECT if se
Treatment in breastfeedingSertraline preferred (lowest infant levels); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines, lam
Antidepressant in pregnancyRisks: persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn, neonatal adaptation syndrom
Valproate in pregnancyHIGHLY teratogenic (neural tube defects); AVOID in women of childbearing age
Lithium in pregnancyEbstein anomaly (cardiac malformation); avoid in 1st trimester; use cautiously
Perinatal mental health teamJoint obstetric-psychiatric care; mother-baby unit if needed

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

Perinatal psychiatry addresses mental health conditions occurring during pregnancy (antenatal) and up to one year postpartum (postnatal). Conditions include baby blues, postpartum depression, puerperal psychosis, and pre-existing psychiatric conditions in pregnancy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

Key features include: antenatal depression, baby blues, postpartum depression.

Q3. How is Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

D) Ebstein anomaly (cardiac malformation); avoid in 1st trimester; use cautiously

  1. Key feature regarding Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

A) Risks: persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn, neonatal adaptation syndrome

B) HIGHLY teratogenic (neural tube defects); AVOID in women of childbearing age

C) Ebstein anomaly (cardiac malformation); avoid in 1st trimester; use cautiously

D) Joint obstetric-psychiatric care; mother-baby unit if needed

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

A) HIGHLY teratogenic (neural tube defects); AVOID in women of childbearing age

B) Ebstein anomaly (cardiac malformation); avoid in 1st trimester; use cautiously

C) Joint obstetric-psychiatric care; mother-baby unit if needed

D) 10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression, lack of su

Answer: D

  1. Key feature regarding Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

A) Ebstein anomaly (cardiac malformation); avoid in 1st trimester; use cautiously

B) Joint obstetric-psychiatric care; mother-baby unit if needed

C) 10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression, lack of su

D) 50-80%; 3-5 days postpartum; mild; resolves by 10-14 days

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

A) Joint obstetric-psychiatric care; mother-baby unit if needed

B) 10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression, lack of su

C) 50-80%; 3-5 days postpartum; mild; resolves by 10-14 days

D) 10-15%; within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; same criteria as MDD

Answer: B

SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient presents with features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum. Which is most appropriate? | 10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression, lack of su
  1. Patient presents with features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum. Which is most appropriate? | 50-80%; 3-5 days postpartum; mild; resolves by 10-14 days
  1. Patient presents with features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum. Which is most appropriate? | 10-15%; within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; same criteria as MDD
  1. Patient presents with features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum. Which is most appropriate? | 1-2 per 1000; psychiatric emergency; within 2 weeks; high suicide/infanticide risk
  1. Patient presents with features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum. Which is most appropriate? | SSRI (sertraline preferred); avoid paroxetine (cardiac malformations); ECT if severe
  1. Patient presents with features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum. Which is most appropriate? | Sertraline preferred (lowest infant levels); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines, lamotrigine

Flashcards

Q: Antenatal depression?

A: 10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression, lack of support

Q: Baby blues?

A: 50-80%; 3-5 days postpartum; mild; resolves by 10-14 days

Q: Postpartum depression?

A: 10-15%; within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; same criteria as MDD

Q: Puerperal psychosis?

A: 1-2 per 1000; psychiatric emergency; within 2 weeks; high suicide/infanticide risk

Q: Treatment in pregnancy?

A: SSRI (sertraline preferred); avoid paroxetine (cardiac malformations); ECT if severe

Q: Treatment in breastfeeding?

A: Sertraline preferred (lowest infant levels); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines, lamotrigine

Q: Antidepressant in pregnancy?

A: Risks: persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn, neonatal adaptation syndrome

Q: Valproate in pregnancy?

A: HIGHLY teratogenic (neural tube defects); AVOID in women of childbearing age

Answer Key with Explanations

  3. C — Postpartum depression
  4. D — Puerperal psychosis
  5. A — Treatment in pregnancy
  6. B — Treatment in breastfeeding

  8. D — Valproate in pregnancy
  9. A — Lithium in pregnancy

▸ 29.17.8 Specific Learning Disorders And Enuresis

Definition

Specific learning disorders are neurodevelopmental disorders characterised by persistent difficulties in learning academic skills, with onset in school age. Enuresis is involuntary urination in a child ≥5 years old; encopresis is involuntary faecal soiling. Both are common in childhood and often have psychological components.

Core Clinical Content

Specific learning disorder types

Dyslexia (reading), dyscalculia (maths), dysgraphia (writing)

Dyslexia prevalence

~5-10% of school-age children; M:F 2:1; phonological processing deficit

Dyslexia features

Letter/word reversal, slow reading, poor spelling, phoneme awareness problems

Dyscalculia prevalence

~3-6%; difficulty with numbers, arithmetic, mathematical reasoning

Dyslexia diagnosis

Achievement below expected for age + IQ + education; psychometric testing

Dyslexia management

Phonics-based reading instruction; educational support; screen for comorbidities (ADHD, anxiety)

Enuresis definition

Involuntary urination ≥2/week in ≥5-year-old; ≥3 months

Enuresis types

Primary (never dry) vs secondary (dry then relapses); monosymptomatic vs non-monosymptomatic

Enuresis causes

Genetic, maturational delay, small bladder, ↑ ADH, psychological, UTI, sleep apnoea

Enuresis treatment

Enuresis alarm (first-line, most effective); desmopressin; reward systems; address comorbidities

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Specific learning disorder typesDyslexia (reading), dyscalculia (maths), dysgraphia (writing)
Dyslexia prevalence~5-10% of school-age children; M:F 2:1; phonological processing deficit
Dyslexia featuresLetter/word reversal, slow reading, poor spelling, phoneme awareness problems
Dyscalculia prevalence~3-6%; difficulty with numbers, arithmetic, mathematical reasoning
Dyslexia diagnosisAchievement below expected for age + IQ + education; psychometric testing
Dyslexia managementPhonics-based reading instruction; educational support; screen for comorbidities
Enuresis definitionInvoluntary urination ≥2/week in ≥5-year-old; ≥3 months
Enuresis typesPrimary (never dry) vs secondary (dry then relapses); monosymptomatic vs non-mon
Enuresis causesGenetic, maturational delay, small bladder, ↑ ADH, psychological, UTI, sleep apn
Enuresis treatmentEnuresis alarm (first-line, most effective); desmopressin; reward systems; addre

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Specific learning disorders and enuresis?

Specific learning disorders are neurodevelopmental disorders characterised by persistent difficulties in learning academic skills, with onset in school age. Enuresis is involuntary urination in a child ≥5 years old; encopresis is involuntary faecal soiling. Both are common in childhood and often have psychological components.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Specific learning disorders and enuresis?

Key features include: specific learning disorder types, dyslexia prevalence, dyslexia features.

Q3. How is Specific learning disorders and enuresis diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Specific learning disorders and enuresis?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Specific learning disorders and enuresis?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Specific learning disorders and enuresis — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

▸ 29.18 Substance Use Disorders 6
▸ 29.18.1 Alcohol Use Disorder

Definition

Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a chronic, relapsing condition characterised by problematic alcohol use causing significant impairment or distress, with ≥2 of 11 DSM-5 criteria over 12 months.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-down, time spent, role impairment, social impairment, hazardous use, use despite problems, craving)

Screening tools

AUDIT (10-item), CAGE (4-item)

CAGE

Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener

UK alcohol limits

≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)

Complications

Hepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy

Wernicke's encephalopathy

Triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

Korsakoff syndrome

Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

Treatment of dependence

Detoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)

Naltrexone

μ-opioid antagonist; reduces craving; monitor LFTs

Acamprosate

NMDA antagonist; reduces craving; safe in liver disease

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteria≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-d
Screening toolsAUDIT (10-item), CAGE (4-item)
CAGECut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener
UK alcohol limits≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)
ComplicationsHepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy
Wernicke's encephalopathyTriad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia
Korsakoff syndromeAnterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible
Treatment of dependenceDetoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)
Naltrexoneμ-opioid antagonist; reduces craving; monitor LFTs
AcamprosateNMDA antagonist; reduces craving; safe in liver disease

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Alcohol use disorder?

Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a chronic, relapsing condition characterised by problematic alcohol use causing significant impairment or distress, with ≥2 of 11 DSM-5 criteria over 12 months.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Alcohol use disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, screening tools, cage. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Alcohol use disorder diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Alcohol use disorder?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Alcohol use disorder?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Alcohol use disorder — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)
