  1. Diagnostic criterion for Alcohol use disorder?

A) ≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)

B) Hepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy

C) Triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

D) Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

Answer: D

  1. Management of Alcohol use disorder includes?

A) Hepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy

B) Triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

C) Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

D) Detoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)

Answer: A

  1. Prognosis of Alcohol use disorder?

A) Triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

B) Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

C) Detoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)

D) μ-opioid antagonist; reduces craving; monitor LFTs

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Alcohol use disorder?

A) Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

B) Detoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)

C) μ-opioid antagonist; reduces craving; monitor LFTs

D) NMDA antagonist; reduces craving; safe in liver disease

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Alcohol use disorder?

A) ≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-d

B) Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener

C) Hepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy

D) Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Alcohol use disorder?

A) AUDIT (10-item), CAGE (4-item)

B) ≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)

C) Triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

D) Detoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Alcohol use disorder?

A) Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener

B) Hepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy

C) Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

D) μ-opioid antagonist; reduces craving; monitor LFTs

Answer: C

SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient presents with features of Alcohol use disorder. Most appropriate first-line? | ≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-d
  1. Which investigation is most useful in Alcohol use disorder? | Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  1. What is the prevalence of Alcohol use disorder? | AUDIT (10-item), CAGE (4-item)
  1. First-line management of Alcohol use disorder? | ≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-d
  1. Which is a recognised risk factor for Alcohol use disorder? | Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener
  1. What is the typical course of Alcohol use disorder? | Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  1. Which complication should be screened for in Alcohol use disorder? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  1. Duration criterion for Alcohol use disorder? | ≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)
  1. Most appropriate referral for Alcohol use disorder? | Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  1. Differential diagnosis of Alcohol use disorder includes? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

Flashcards

Q: DSM-5 criteria?

A: ≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-down, time spent, role impairment, social impairment, hazardous use, use despite problems, craving)

Q: Screening tools?

A: AUDIT (10-item), CAGE (4-item)

Q: CAGE?

A: Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener

Q: UK alcohol limits?

A: ≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)

Q: Complications?

A: Hepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy

Q: Wernicke's encephalopathy?

A: Triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

Q: Korsakoff syndrome?

A: Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

Q: Treatment of dependence?

A: Detoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)

  1. A — Key feature of Alcohol use disorder?
  2. B — Regarding Alcohol use disorder?
  3. C — Which of the following is true about Alcohol use disorder?
  4. D — Diagnostic criterion for Alcohol use disorder?
  5. A — Management of Alcohol use disorder includes?
  6. B — Prognosis of Alcohol use disorder?
  7. C — Complication of Alcohol use disorder?
  8. A — Epidemiology of Alcohol use disorder?
  9. B — Risk factor for Alcohol use disorder?
  10. C — First-line treatment for Alcohol use disorder?

SBAs

  1. ≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-d
  2. Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  3. AUDIT (10-item), CAGE (4-item)
  4. ≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-d
  5. Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener
  6. Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  7. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  8. ≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)
  9. Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  10. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

▸ 29.18.2 Alcohol Withdrawal And Delirium Tremens

Definition

Alcohol withdrawal is a clinical syndrome that develops 6-24 hours after cessation in dependent drinkers, ranging from mild tremor to severe delirium tremens (DTs) with autonomic hyperactivity, confusion, and seizures.

Core Clinical Content

Minor withdrawal

6-24 h after last drink: tremor, anxiety, insomnia, GI upset, autonomic hyperactivity

Seizures

12-48 h; generalised tonic-clonic; usually self-limiting

Alcoholic hallucinosis

12-24 h; visual/auditory hallucinations with clear sensorium

Delirium tremens (DTs)

48-96 h (up to 5 days); confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, hallucinations, tremor

DTs mortality

5-15% untreated; <1% with treatment

Treatment

Long-acting benzodiazepine (chlordiazepoxide, diazepam) — symptom-triggered or fixed dose

CIWA-Ar

Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol; guides dosing

Thiamine

BEFORE glucose; Pabrinex IV for Wernicke's prevention

Pabrinex

IV thiamine + pyridoxine; give before glucose to prevent Wernicke's

Seizure prophylaxis

Not routinely; treat seizures with benzodiazepine; avoid phenytoin (ineffective)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Minor withdrawal6-24 h after last drink: tremor, anxiety, insomnia, GI upset, autonomic hyperact
Seizures12-48 h; generalised tonic-clonic; usually self-limiting
Alcoholic hallucinosis12-24 h; visual/auditory hallucinations with clear sensorium
Delirium tremens (DTs)48-96 h (up to 5 days); confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, hallucinations, trem
DTs mortality5-15% untreated; <1% with treatment
TreatmentLong-acting benzodiazepine (chlordiazepoxide, diazepam) — symptom-triggered or f
CIWA-ArClinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol; guides dosing
ThiamineBEFORE glucose; Pabrinex IV for Wernicke's prevention
PabrinexIV thiamine + pyridoxine; give before glucose to prevent Wernicke's
Seizure prophylaxisNot routinely; treat seizures with benzodiazepine; avoid phenytoin (ineffective)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

Alcohol withdrawal is a clinical syndrome that develops 6-24 hours after cessation in dependent drinkers, ranging from mild tremor to severe delirium tremens (DTs) with autonomic hyperactivity, confusion, and seizures.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

Key features include: minor withdrawal, seizures, alcoholic hallucinosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.18.3 Benzodiazepine And Z Drug Use Disorder

Definition

Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder is a chronic condition characterised by problematic use of these sedative-hypnotic agents, with significant risk of dependence and withdrawal that can be life-threatening.

Core Clinical Content

Mechanism

Benzodiazepines: ↑ GABA-A; Z-drugs: similar but more selective for α1 subunit

Common drugs

Benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam, temazepam); Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon)

Indication for long-term use

Limited; only for refractory epilepsy, severe anxiety unresponsive to other treatments

Tolerance

Develops over weeks to months

Withdrawal symptoms

Rebound anxiety, insomnia, tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, perceptual disturbance

Severe withdrawal

Seizures, delirium, similar to alcohol withdrawal

Withdrawal timing

Short-acting (lorazepam): 1-2 days onset; long-acting (diazepam): 5-7 days onset

Withdrawal management

Convert to long-acting (diazepam), then taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks)

Elderly

Increased sensitivity, falls, cognitive impairment; AVOID long-term

Overdose

Reversed with flumazenil (cautiously — risk of seizures in dependent users)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
MechanismBenzodiazepines: ↑ GABA-A; Z-drugs: similar but more selective for α1 subunit
Common drugsBenzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam, temazepam); Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone,
Indication for long-term useLimited; only for refractory epilepsy, severe anxiety unresponsive to other trea
ToleranceDevelops over weeks to months
Withdrawal symptomsRebound anxiety, insomnia, tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, perceptual disturban
Severe withdrawalSeizures, delirium, similar to alcohol withdrawal
Withdrawal timingShort-acting (lorazepam): 1-2 days onset; long-acting (diazepam): 5-7 days onset
Withdrawal managementConvert to long-acting (diazepam), then taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks)
ElderlyIncreased sensitivity, falls, cognitive impairment; AVOID long-term
OverdoseReversed with flumazenil (cautiously — risk of seizures in dependent users)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder?

Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder is a chronic condition characterised by problematic use of these sedative-hypnotic agents, with significant risk of dependence and withdrawal that can be life-threatening.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder?

Key features include: mechanism, common drugs, indication for long-term use. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal

  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.18.4 Gambling Disorder And Behavioural Addictions

Definition

Gambling disorder is characterised by persistent, recurrent problematic gambling behaviour leading to significant impairment or distress. Behavioural addictions (also called 'process addictions' or 'addictions without substance') include gambling, gaming, internet, food, sex, shopping, and exercise, with similar features to substance use disorders.

Core Clinical Content

Gambling disorder DSM-5

Persistent problematic gambling; ≥4 of 9 criteria in 12 months (similar to SUD)

Gambling prevalence

~1-2%; more common in males; adolescence and young adults

Gambling types

Pathological gambling (behavioural), problem gambling (mild)

Gambling risk factors

Impulsivity, depression, anxiety, substance use, family history, accessibility

Gambling comorbidities

Depression (50%), anxiety, substance use, suicide risk ↑↑

Gambling treatment

CBT (most evidence), motivational interviewing, Gamblers Anonymous, naltrexone/opioid antagonists

Internet gaming disorder (ICD-11)

Persistent gaming despite harm; impaired control; prioritised over other activities

Other behavioural addictions

Compulsive sexual behaviour, binge eating, shopping, exercise

Compulsive sexual behaviour (ICD-11)

Persistent failure to control sexual urges; significant distress/impairment

Common features of behavioural addictions

Salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, relapse

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Gambling disorder DSM-5Persistent problematic gambling; ≥4 of 9 criteria in 12 months (similar to SUD)
Gambling prevalence~1-2%; more common in males; adolescence and young adults
Gambling typesPathological gambling (behavioural), problem gambling (mild)
Gambling risk factorsImpulsivity, depression, anxiety, substance use, family history, accessibility
Gambling comorbiditiesDepression (50%), anxiety, substance use, suicide risk ↑↑
Gambling treatmentCBT (most evidence), motivational interviewing, Gamblers Anonymous, naltrexone/o
Internet gaming disorder (ICD-11)Persistent gaming despite harm; impaired control; prioritised over other activit
Other behavioural addictionsCompulsive sexual behaviour, binge eating, shopping, exercise
Compulsive sexual behaviour (ICD-11)Persistent failure to control sexual urges; significant distress/impairment
Common features of behavioural addictionsSalience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, relapse

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions?

Gambling disorder is characterised by persistent, recurrent problematic gambling behaviour leading to significant impairment or distress. Behavioural addictions (also called 'process addictions' or 'addictions without substance') include gambling, gaming, internet, food, sex, shopping, and exercise, with similar features to substance use disorders.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions?

Key features include: gambling disorder dsm-5, gambling prevalence, gambling types.

Q3. How is Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • 'DAPT' — antipsychotic movement disorders (Dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive)

▸ 29.18.5 Opioid Use Disorder And Overdose

Definition

Opioid use disorder is a chronic relapsing condition characterised by problematic opioid use, with opioid overdose being a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent reversal with naloxone.

Core Clinical Content

Common opioids

Heroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, codeine, tramadol

Overdose triad

Respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, decreased consciousness (NOT miosis alone)

Other overdose signs

Hypoxia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia

Naloxone

μ-opioid antagonist; rapid onset (1-2 min); short-acting (30-90 min)

Repeat naloxone

May need repeated doses as naloxone wears off before opioid

Naloxone administration

IV/IM/SC/intranasal; start at 0.4-2 mg, titrate to respiratory effort

Duration monitoring

At least 4-6 hours after last naloxone dose (longer for long-acting opioids)

Methadone

Long-acting opioid agonist; risk of QTc prolongation; used in maintenance therapy

Buprenorphine

Partial μ-agonist; ceiling effect (safer); used in maintenance therapy

Withdrawal

Yawning, lacrimation, piloerection, mydriasis, GI upset, myalgia; not life-threatening

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Common opioidsHeroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, codeine, tramadol
Overdose triadRespiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, decreased consciousness (NOT miosis alo
Other overdose signsHypoxia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia
Naloxoneμ-opioid antagonist; rapid onset (1-2 min); short-acting (30-90 min)
Repeat naloxoneMay need repeated doses as naloxone wears off before opioid
Naloxone administrationIV/IM/SC/intranasal; start at 0.4-2 mg, titrate to respiratory effort
Duration monitoringAt least 4-6 hours after last naloxone dose (longer for long-acting opioids)
MethadoneLong-acting opioid agonist; risk of QTc prolongation; used in maintenance therap
BuprenorphinePartial μ-agonist; ceiling effect (safer); used in maintenance therapy
WithdrawalYawning, lacrimation, piloerection, mydriasis, GI upset, myalgia; not life-threa

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

Opioid use disorder is a chronic relapsing condition characterised by problematic opioid use, with opioid overdose being a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent reversal with naloxone.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

Key features include: common opioids, overdose triad, other overdose signs. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Opioid use disorder and overdose diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Opioid use disorder and overdose?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Opioid use disorder and overdose — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

D) Partial μ-agonist; ceiling effect (safer); used in maintenance therapy

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

A) At least 4-6 hours after last naloxone dose (longer for long-acting opioids)

D) Yawning, lacrimation, piloerection, mydriasis, GI upset, myalgia; not life-threa

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

A) Heroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, codeine, tramadol

B) Hypoxia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia

C) May need repeated doses as naloxone wears off before opioid

D) At least 4-6 hours after last naloxone dose (longer for long-acting opioids)

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Opioid use disorder and overdose?

A) Respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, decreased consciousness (NOT miosis alo

B) μ-opioid antagonist; rapid onset (1-2 min); short-acting (30-90 min)

C) IV/IM/SC/intranasal; start at 0.4-2 mg, titrate to respiratory effort

D) Long-acting opioid agonist; risk of QTc prolongation; used in maintenance therap

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Opioid use disorder and overdose?

A) Hypoxia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia

B) May need repeated doses as naloxone wears off before opioid

C) At least 4-6 hours after last naloxone dose (longer for long-acting opioids)

D) Partial μ-agonist; ceiling effect (safer); used in maintenance therapy

Answer: C

▸ 29.18.6 Stimulant And Other Substance Use Disorders

Definition

Stimulant and other substance use disorders involve problematic use of substances including cocaine, amphetamines, cannabis, hallucinogens, and tobacco, with significant health, social, and psychological consequences.

Core Clinical Content

Cocaine

Blocks dopamine, noradrenaline, serotonin reuptake; intense euphoria followed by crash

Cocaine withdrawal

Depression, fatigue, hypersomnia, craving (not life-threatening)

Cocaine complications

MI, stroke, aortic dissection, arrhythmia, seizures, nasal septum perforation

Amphetamines

Similar to cocaine; longer duration; risk of psychosis (paranoid, tactile hallucinations)

Cannabis

Most used illicit drug; acute effects: euphoria, anxiety, paranoia, perceptual changes

Cannabis withdrawal

Irritability, anxiety, insomnia, anorexia; mild

Cannabis and psychosis

Dose-related risk of schizophrenia-spectrum disorders; earlier onset

Hallucinogens (LSD, psilocybin)

Perceptual disturbances, synaesthesia; bad trips, flashbacks (HPPD)

Tobacco

Leading preventable cause of death; nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion for cessation

Volatile substance abuse

Solvents, glue; acute: euphoria, hallucinations; chronic: neurotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
CocaineBlocks dopamine, noradrenaline, serotonin reuptake; intense euphoria followed by
Cocaine withdrawalDepression, fatigue, hypersomnia, craving (not life-threatening)
Cocaine complicationsMI, stroke, aortic dissection, arrhythmia, seizures, nasal septum perforation
AmphetaminesSimilar to cocaine; longer duration; risk of psychosis (paranoid, tactile halluc
CannabisMost used illicit drug; acute effects: euphoria, anxiety, paranoia, perceptual c
Cannabis withdrawalIrritability, anxiety, insomnia, anorexia; mild
Cannabis and psychosisDose-related risk of schizophrenia-spectrum disorders; earlier onset
Hallucinogens (LSD, psilocybin)Perceptual disturbances, synaesthesia; bad trips, flashbacks (HPPD)
TobaccoLeading preventable cause of death; nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion
Volatile substance abuseSolvents, glue; acute: euphoria, hallucinations; chronic: neurotoxicity, periphe

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

Stimulant and other substance use disorders involve problematic use of substances including cocaine, amphetamines, cannabis, hallucinogens, and tobacco, with significant health, social, and psychological consequences.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

Key features include: cocaine, cocaine withdrawal, cocaine complications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Stimulant and other substance use disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Stimulant and other substance use disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

Answer: A

  1. Prognosis of Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

B) Dose-related risk of schizophrenia-spectrum disorders; earlier onset

Answer: B

  1. Which investigation is most useful in Stimulant and other substance use disorders? | Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria

Flashcards

Q: Cocaine?

A: Blocks dopamine, noradrenaline, serotonin reuptake; intense euphoria followed by crash

Q: Cocaine withdrawal?

A: Depression, fatigue, hypersomnia, craving (not life-threatening)

Q: Cocaine complications?

A: MI, stroke, aortic dissection, arrhythmia, seizures, nasal septum perforation

Q: Amphetamines?

A: Similar to cocaine; longer duration; risk of psychosis (paranoid, tactile hallucinations)

Q: Cannabis?

A: Most used illicit drug; acute effects: euphoria, anxiety, paranoia, perceptual changes

Q: Cannabis withdrawal?

A: Irritability, anxiety, insomnia, anorexia; mild

Q: Cannabis and psychosis?

A: Dose-related risk of schizophrenia-spectrum disorders; earlier onset

Q: Hallucinogens (LSD, psilocybin)?

A: Perceptual disturbances, synaesthesia; bad trips, flashbacks (HPPD)

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.19 Suicide and Self-Harm 2
▸ 29.19.1 Non Suicidal Self Injury Nssi And Postvention

Definition

Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) is intentional self-damage (cutting, burning, hitting) without suicidal intent, often used for emotional regulation. Postvention refers to interventions after a suicide to support affected individuals and prevent further suicides.

Core Clinical Content

NSSI definition

Intentional self-damage without suicidal intent; ≥5 days in past year for DSM-5 diagnosis

NSSI prevalence

17% in adolescents; 5% in adults; more common in females

Common methods

Cutting, burning, scratching, hitting self

Functions of NSSI

Affect regulation, self-punishment, anti-dissociation, interpersonal communication

Risk factors

Childhood trauma, BPD, depression, eating disorders, substance use

Differential from suicide attempt

NSSI: no intent to die; often for relief; usually superficial

Differential from BD

NSSI: not suicidal; BD: suicidal; can co-occur

Management

DBT (first-line for NSSI/BPD), CBT, addressing underlying distress

Postvention

Support for bereaved after suicide; psychoeducation; reducing contagion

Papageno effect vs Werther effect

Papageno: protective media (recovery stories); Werther: imitative suicide after media coverage

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
NSSI definitionIntentional self-damage without suicidal intent; ≥5 days in past year for DSM-5
NSSI prevalence17% in adolescents; 5% in adults; more common in females
Common methodsCutting, burning, scratching, hitting self
Functions of NSSIAffect regulation, self-punishment, anti-dissociation, interpersonal communicati
Risk factorsChildhood trauma, BPD, depression, eating disorders, substance use
Differential from suicide attemptNSSI: no intent to die; often for relief; usually superficial
Differential from BDNSSI: not suicidal; BD: suicidal; can co-occur
ManagementDBT (first-line for NSSI/BPD), CBT, addressing underlying distress
PostventionSupport for bereaved after suicide; psychoeducation; reducing contagion
Papageno effect vs Werther effectPapageno: protective media (recovery stories); Werther: imitative suicide after

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and postvention?

Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) is intentional self-damage (cutting, burning, hitting) without suicidal intent, often used for emotional regulation. Postvention refers to interventions after a suicide to support affected individuals and prevent further suicides.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and postvention?

Key features include: nssi definition, nssi prevalence, common methods.

Q3. How is Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and postvention diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and postvention?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and postvention?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and postvention — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.19.2 Suicide Risk Assessment And Prevention

Definition

Suicide is the act of intentionally ending one's own life. Suicide risk assessment is a structured evaluation of factors that increase or decrease suicide risk, used to guide safety planning and intervention. Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-damage without suicidal intent.

Core Clinical Content

Suicide epidemiology

~700,000 deaths/year worldwide; 1 every 40 seconds; leading cause of death in young adults

Risk factors (SAD PERSONS)

Sex (M), Age (extremes), Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social support lacking, Organised plan, No spouse, Sickness

Strongest risk factor

Previous suicide attempt (40x increased risk)

Protective factors

Family support, religious beliefs, engagement with treatment, reasons for living, access to care

Warning signs

Talking about wanting to die, looking for means, withdrawal, mood changes, giving possessions

Direct questioning

Asking about suicide does NOT increase risk; can decrease it

Suicide plan

Specificity of plan, availability of means, timeframe all increase risk

Self-harm (NSSI)

Intentional self-injury without suicidal intent; often for emotional regulation

Treatment

Safety plan, remove means, CBT-DBT, medications, hospitalisation if high risk

Means restriction

Effective prevention strategy (e.g., bridge barriers, medication blister packs)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Suicide epidemiology~700,000 deaths/year worldwide; 1 every 40 seconds; leading cause of death in yo
Risk factors (SAD PERSONS)Sex (M), Age (extremes), Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss
Strongest risk factorPrevious suicide attempt (40x increased risk)
Protective factorsFamily support, religious beliefs, engagement with treatment, reasons for living
Warning signsTalking about wanting to die, looking for means, withdrawal, mood changes, givin
Direct questioningAsking about suicide does NOT increase risk; can decrease it
Suicide planSpecificity of plan, availability of means, timeframe all increase risk
Self-harm (NSSI)Intentional self-injury without suicidal intent; often for emotional regulation
TreatmentSafety plan, remove means, CBT-DBT, medications, hospitalisation if high risk
Means restrictionEffective prevention strategy (e.g., bridge barriers, medication blister packs)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Suicide risk assessment and prevention?

Suicide is the act of intentionally ending one's own life. Suicide risk assessment is a structured evaluation of factors that increase or decrease suicide risk, used to guide safety planning and intervention. Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-damage without suicidal intent.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Suicide risk assessment and prevention?

Key features include: suicide epidemiology, risk factors (sad persons), strongest risk factor.

Q3. How is Suicide risk assessment and prevention diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Suicide risk assessment and prevention?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Suicide risk assessment and prevention?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Suicide risk assessment and prevention — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

  1. Patient presents with features of Suicide risk assessment and prevention. Which is most appropriate? | Asking about suicide does NOT increase risk; can decrease it
  1. Patient presents with features of Suicide risk assessment and prevention. Which is most appropriate? | Specificity of plan, availability of means, timeframe all increase risk
  1. Patient presents with features of Suicide risk assessment and prevention. Which is most appropriate? | Intentional self-injury without suicidal intent; often for emotional regulation
  1. Patient presents with features of Suicide risk assessment and prevention. Which is most appropriate? | Safety plan, remove means, CBT-DBT, medications, hospitalisation if high risk
  1. Patient presents with features of Suicide risk assessment and prevention. Which is most appropriate? | Effective prevention strategy (e.g., bridge barriers, medication blister packs)

Flashcards

Q: Suicide epidemiology?

A: ~700,000 deaths/year worldwide; 1 every 40 seconds; leading cause of death in young adults

Q: Risk factors (SAD PERSONS)?

A: Sex (M), Age (extremes), Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social support lac

Q: Strongest risk factor?

A: Previous suicide attempt (40x increased risk)

Q: Protective factors?

A: Family support, religious beliefs, engagement with treatment, reasons for living, access to care

Q: Warning signs?

A: Talking about wanting to die, looking for means, withdrawal, mood changes, giving possessions

Q: Direct questioning?

A: Asking about suicide does NOT increase risk; can decrease it

Q: Suicide plan?

A: Specificity of plan, availability of means, timeframe all increase risk

Q: Self-harm (NSSI)?

A: Intentional self-injury without suicidal intent; often for emotional regulation

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Suicide epidemiology
  2. B — Risk factors (SAD PERSONS)
  3. C — Strongest risk factor
  4. D — Protective factors
  5. A — Warning signs
  6. B — Direct questioning
  7. C — Suicide plan
  8. D — Self-harm (NSSI)

  10. B — Means restriction

▸ 29.2 Bereavement and Adjustment 1
▸ 29.2.1 Bereavement Grief And Adjustment Disorders

Definition

Bereavement is the loss of a loved one through death. Grief is the normal emotional response to loss. Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive response to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that resolve within 6 months of stressor removal.

Core Clinical Content

Normal grief stages (Kübler-Ross)

Denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance (note: not linear)

Worden's tasks of mourning

Accept reality, work through pain, adjust to life without deceased, find lasting connection

Anticipatory grief

Before the loss; common in terminal illness

Prolonged grief disorder (PGD)

Grief >12 months; yearning for deceased; functional impairment; new DSM-5 diagnosis

Complicated grief

Severe, prolonged grief; may need intervention

Bereavement vs MDD

MDD: pervasive depression, anhedonia, suicidal ideation, low self-esteem; grief: waves, focused on deceased

Adjustment disorder subtypes

Depressed mood, anxiety, mixed, disturbance of conduct

Adjustment disorder criteria

Symptoms within 3 months of stressor; not meeting criteria for another disorder; resolves within 6 months of stressor removal

Treatment of complicated grief

CBT, complicated grief therapy, support groups; avoid routine antidepressants

Pharmacological considerations

AVOID benzodiazepines; short-term hypnotics for sleep; SSRI for comorbid depression

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Normal grief stages (Kübler-Ross)Denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance (note: not linear)
Worden's tasks of mourningAccept reality, work through pain, adjust to life without deceased, find lasting
Anticipatory griefBefore the loss; common in terminal illness
Prolonged grief disorder (PGD)Grief >12 months; yearning for deceased; functional impairment; new DSM-5 diagno
Complicated griefSevere, prolonged grief; may need intervention
Bereavement vs MDDMDD: pervasive depression, anhedonia, suicidal ideation, low self-esteem; grief:
Adjustment disorder subtypesDepressed mood, anxiety, mixed, disturbance of conduct
Adjustment disorder criteriaSymptoms within 3 months of stressor; not meeting criteria for another disorder;
Treatment of complicated griefCBT, complicated grief therapy, support groups; avoid routine antidepressants
Pharmacological considerationsAVOID benzodiazepines; short-term hypnotics for sleep; SSRI for comorbid depress

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Bereavement is the loss of a loved one through death. Grief is the normal emotional response to loss. Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive response to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that resolve within 6 months of stressor removal.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Key features include: normal grief stages (kübler-ross), worden's tasks of mourning, anticipatory grief.

Q3. How is Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.3 Chronic Pain and Psychosomatic Medicine 2
▸ 29.3.1 Chronic Pain And Medically Unexplained Pain

Definition

Chronic pain is pain persisting >3 months, often with limited response to standard treatments. It involves complex interactions between physical, psychological, and social factors (biopsychosocial model). Medically unexplained pain (somatic symptom disorder with predominant pain) overlaps with this.

Core Clinical Content

Chronic pain definition

Pain persisting >3 months; >normal healing time

Biopsychosocial model

Pain = interaction of biological, psychological (anxiety, depression, catastrophising), social factors

Yellow flags

Psychosocial risk factors for chronicity: beliefs, emotions, behaviours, social context

Depression in chronic pain

30-50% comorbidity; bidirectional relationship; under-recognised

Catastrophising

Negative cognitive style ('this is terrible, will never end'); predicts pain outcomes

Opioid use

Limited benefit beyond 4-6 weeks; high risk dependence; AVOID long-term

Gabapentinoids

Gabapentin, pregabalin; for neuropathic pain; risk of dependence, sedation

Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs)

Amitriptyline, nortriptyline; for neuropathic pain, chronic headache; start low

SNRIs

Duloxetine; for neuropathic pain (diabetic), fibromyalgia, chronic musculoskeletal pain

Multidisciplinary pain management

Combination of medication, physiotherapy, CBT, occupational therapy, self-management

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Chronic pain definitionPain persisting >3 months; >normal healing time
Biopsychosocial modelPain = interaction of biological, psychological (anxiety, depression, catastroph
Yellow flagsPsychosocial risk factors for chronicity: beliefs, emotions, behaviours, social
Depression in chronic pain30-50% comorbidity; bidirectional relationship; under-recognised
CatastrophisingNegative cognitive style ('this is terrible, will never end'); predicts pain out
Opioid useLimited benefit beyond 4-6 weeks; high risk dependence; AVOID long-term
GabapentinoidsGabapentin, pregabalin; for neuropathic pain; risk of dependence, sedation
Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs)Amitriptyline, nortriptyline; for neuropathic pain, chronic headache; start low
SNRIsDuloxetine; for neuropathic pain (diabetic), fibromyalgia, chronic musculoskelet
Multidisciplinary pain managementCombination of medication, physiotherapy, CBT, occupational therapy, self-manage

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain?

Chronic pain is pain persisting >3 months, often with limited response to standard treatments. It involves complex interactions between physical, psychological, and social factors (biopsychosocial model). Medically unexplained pain (somatic symptom disorder with predominant pain) overlaps with this.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain?

Key features include: chronic pain definition, biopsychosocial model, yellow flags.

Q3. How is Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.3.2 Functional Disorders And Stress Related Illness

Definition

Functional disorders (medically unexplained symptoms) are conditions with distressing symptoms not explained by organic disease, often triggered or exacerbated by stress, with significant functional impact. They are managed with the biopsychosocial model and avoiding excessive investigation.

Core Clinical Content

Functional disorders

Symptoms not explained by organic disease; real, not 'faked'

Common functional disorders

Fibromyalgia, IBS, chronic fatigue syndrome, functional dyspepsia, non-cardiac chest pain

Fibromyalgia

Widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, tender points

Fibromyalgia treatment

Exercise (most effective), CBT, duloxetine, pregabalin; multidisciplinary

Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS/ME)

Severe fatigue >6 months, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction

CFS/ME management

Pacing, activity management, CBT, supportive care; avoid excessive rest

Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)

Abdominal pain, altered bowel habit; consider as functional if no organic cause

Functional neurological disorder (FND)

See Conversion disorder (separate file)

Stress-related illness

Adjustment disorder, acute stress reaction; often self-limiting

Medically unexplained symptoms (MUS)

20-30% of primary care consultations; avoid dismissive 'all in the head' approach

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Functional disordersSymptoms not explained by organic disease; real, not 'faked'
Common functional disordersFibromyalgia, IBS, chronic fatigue syndrome, functional dyspepsia, non-cardiac c
FibromyalgiaWidespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, tender points
Fibromyalgia treatmentExercise (most effective), CBT, duloxetine, pregabalin; multidisciplinary
Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS/ME)Severe fatigue >6 months, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive
CFS/ME managementPacing, activity management, CBT, supportive care; avoid excessive rest
Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)Abdominal pain, altered bowel habit; consider as functional if no organic cause
Functional neurological disorder (FND)See Conversion disorder (separate file)
Stress-related illnessAdjustment disorder, acute stress reaction; often self-limiting
Medically unexplained symptoms (MUS)20-30% of primary care consultations; avoid dismissive 'all in the head' approac

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Functional disorders (medically unexplained symptoms) are conditions with distressing symptoms not explained by organic disease, often triggered or exacerbated by stress, with significant functional impact. They are managed with the biopsychosocial model and avoiding excessive investigation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Key features include: functional disorders, common functional disorders, fibromyalgia.

Q3. How is Functional disorders and stress-related illness diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Functional disorders and stress-related illness — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

A: Widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, tender points

Q: Fibromyalgia treatment?

A: Exercise (most effective), CBT, duloxetine, pregabalin; multidisciplinary

Q: Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS/ME)?

A: Severe fatigue >6 months, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction

Q: CFS/ME management?

A: Pacing, activity management, CBT, supportive care; avoid excessive rest

Q: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)?

A: Abdominal pain, altered bowel habit; consider as functional if no organic cause

Q: Functional neurological disorder (FND)?

A: See Conversion disorder (separate file)

▸ 29.4 Cognitive Disorders 6
▸ 29.4.1 Alzheimer S Disease And Other Dementias

Definition

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia (60-70%), characterised by progressive memory impairment, other cognitive deficits, and behavioural changes, with characteristic neuropathology of amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles.

Core Clinical Content

Dementia definition

Acquired cognitive decline in ≥2 domains severe enough to impair function

Alzheimer's prevalence

60-70% of all dementia; 10% of >65 years; 30% of >85 years

Pathology

Amyloid (Aβ) plaques + neurofibrillary tangles (hyperphosphorylated tau) + cortical atrophy

Risk factors

Age, APOE4, family history, low education, cardiovascular risk factors

Early symptoms

Episodic memory loss (recent events), disorientation to time, word-finding difficulty

Late symptoms

Personality change, behavioural disturbance, motor symptoms, incontinence

Investigations

MMSE/MoCA, bloods to exclude reversible causes, CT/MRI (atrophy), FDG-PET or CSF biomarkers

Treatment

Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine); memantine for moderate-severe

Memantine

NMDA antagonist; for moderate-severe AD; monotherapy or add-on

Prognosis

Progressive; 3-10 years from diagnosis; common cause of death

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Alzheimer's disease and other dementias?

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia (60-70%), characterised by progressive memory impairment, other cognitive deficits, and behavioural changes, with characteristic neuropathology of amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Alzheimer's disease and other dementias?

Key features include: dementia definition, alzheimer's prevalence, pathology.

Q3. How is Alzheimer's disease and other dementias diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Alzheimer's disease and other dementias?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Alzheimer's disease and other dementias?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Alzheimer's disease and other dementias — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 29.4.2 Delirium

Definition

Delirium (acute confusional state) is an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention, awareness, and cognition, usually caused by an underlying medical condition, substance, or medication, with onset over hours to days.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Disturbance of attention, awareness, cognition; acute onset (hours-days); fluctuation; evidence of physiological cause

Prevalence in hospital

10-30% of medical inpatients; 50% in ICU; 50% post-hip fracture

Key features

Acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganised thinking, altered consciousness

Hyperactive vs hypoactive

Hyperactive: agitation, hallucinations; Hypoactive: withdrawn, sleepy (more common, often missed)

Causes (DIMES)

Drugs, Infection, Metabolic, Environmental/Emotional, Structural (stroke, bleed)

Predisposing factors

Dementia, advanced age, polypharmacy, sensory impairment, frailty

Investigations

Bloods (FBC, U&E, Ca, glucose, LFT, TSH, B12), sepsis screen, ECG, CXR, CT head if indicated

Treatment

Treat underlying cause; reorient; avoid restraints; antipsychotic if severely agitated

Antipsychotic choice

Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg or olanzapine; lowest dose, shortest time

Prognosis

Associated with increased mortality, prolonged hospital stay, increased dementia risk

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Delirium?

Delirium (acute confusional state) is an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention, awareness, and cognition, usually caused by an underlying medical condition, substance, or medication, with onset over hours to days.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Delirium?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, prevalence in hospital, key features.

Q3. How is Delirium diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Delirium?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Delirium?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • Mnemonic for Delirium — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms

  1. Key feature regarding Delirium?

A) Drugs, Infection, Metabolic, Environmental/Emotional, Structural (stroke, bleed)

B) Dementia, advanced age, polypharmacy, sensory impairment, frailty

C) Bloods (FBC, U&E, Ca, glucose, LFT, TSH, B12), sepsis screen, ECG, CXR, CT head if indicat

D) Treat underlying cause; reorient; avoid restraints; antipsychotic if severely agitated

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Delirium?

A) Dementia, advanced age, polypharmacy, sensory impairment, frailty

B) Bloods (FBC, U&E, Ca, glucose, LFT, TSH, B12), sepsis screen, ECG, CXR, CT head if indicat

C) Treat underlying cause; reorient; avoid restraints; antipsychotic if severely agitated

D) Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg or olanzapine; lowest dose, shortest time

Answer: B

  1. Key feature regarding Delirium?

A) Bloods (FBC, U&E, Ca, glucose, LFT, TSH, B12), sepsis screen, ECG, CXR, CT head if indicat

B) Treat underlying cause; reorient; avoid restraints; antipsychotic if severely agitated

C) Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg or olanzapine; lowest dose, shortest time

D) Associated with increased mortality, prolonged hospital stay, increased dementia risk

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Delirium?

A) Treat underlying cause; reorient; avoid restraints; antipsychotic if severely agitated

B) Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg or olanzapine; lowest dose, shortest time

C) Associated with increased mortality, prolonged hospital stay, increased dementia risk

D) Disturbance of attention, awareness, cognition; acute onset (hours-days); fluctuation; evi

Answer: D

  1. Key feature regarding Delirium?

A) Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg or olanzapine; lowest dose, shortest time

B) Associated with increased mortality, prolonged hospital stay, increased dementia risk

C) Disturbance of attention, awareness, cognition; acute onset (hours-days); fluctuation; evi

D) 10-30% of medical inpatients; 50% in ICU; 50% post-hip fracture

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Delirium?

A) Associated with increased mortality, prolonged hospital stay, increased dementia risk

B) Disturbance of attention, awareness, cognition; acute onset (hours-days); fluctuation; evi

C) 10-30% of medical inpatients; 50% in ICU; 50% post-hip fracture

D) Acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganised thinking, altered consciousness

Answer: B

Flashcards

Q: DSM-5 criteria?

A: Disturbance of attention, awareness, cognition; acute onset (hours-days); fluctuation; evidence of p

Q: Prevalence in hospital?

A: 10-30% of medical inpatients; 50% in ICU; 50% post-hip fracture

Q: Key features?

A: Acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganised thinking, altered consciousness

Q: Hyperactive vs hypoactive?

A: Hyperactive: agitation, hallucinations; Hypoactive: withdrawn, sleepy (more common, often missed)

Q: Causes (DIMES)?

A: Drugs, Infection, Metabolic, Environmental/Emotional, Structural (stroke, bleed)

Q: Predisposing factors?

A: Dementia, advanced age, polypharmacy, sensory impairment, frailty

Q: Investigations?

A: Bloods (FBC, U&E, Ca, glucose, LFT, TSH, B12), sepsis screen, ECG, CXR, CT head if indicated

Q: Treatment?

A: Treat underlying cause; reorient; avoid restraints; antipsychotic if severely agitated

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.4.3 Frontotemporal Dementia And Other Dementias

Definition

Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder primarily affecting the frontal and temporal lobes, with early personality, behaviour, or language changes (rather than memory), often with onset in middle age (45-65 years).

Core Clinical Content

FTD prevalence

5-10% of all dementia; common cause of young-onset dementia (<65)

FTD subtypes

Behavioural variant (bvFTD) and primary progressive aphasia (PPA)

Behavioural variant (bvFTD)

Early disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, compulsive behaviours, hyperorality

PPA subtypes

Non-fluent (grammar), semantic (word meaning), logopenic (word retrieval)

Pick disease

FTD with Pick bodies (tau-positive intraneuronal inclusions)

Pathology

Tau or TDP-43 inclusions; frontotemporal atrophy on imaging

Imaging

MRI: focal frontal and/or temporal atrophy; 'knife-edge' gyri

Genetics

30% familial; MAPT, GRN, C9ORF72 mutations

Treatment

No disease-modifying therapy; SSRI for behavioural symptoms; AVOID antipsychotics

Prognosis

Progressive; 6-11 years from diagnosis; younger onset often more rapid

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
FTD prevalence5-10% of all dementia; common cause of young-onset dementia (<65)
FTD subtypesBehavioural variant (bvFTD) and primary progressive aphasia (PPA)
Behavioural variant (bvFTD)Early disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, compulsive behaviours, hyperoralit
PPA subtypesNon-fluent (grammar), semantic (word meaning), logopenic (word retrieval)
Pick diseaseFTD with Pick bodies (tau-positive intraneuronal inclusions)
PathologyTau or TDP-43 inclusions; frontotemporal atrophy on imaging
ImagingMRI: focal frontal and/or temporal atrophy; 'knife-edge' gyri
Genetics30% familial; MAPT, GRN, C9ORF72 mutations
TreatmentNo disease-modifying therapy; SSRI for behavioural symptoms; AVOID antipsychotic
PrognosisProgressive; 6-11 years from diagnosis; younger onset often more rapid

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Frontotemporal dementia and other dementias?

Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder primarily affecting the frontal and temporal lobes, with early personality, behaviour, or language changes (rather than memory), often with onset in middle age (45-65 years).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Frontotemporal dementia and other dementias?

Key features include: ftd prevalence, ftd subtypes, behavioural variant (bvftd).

Q3. How is Frontotemporal dementia and other dementias diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Frontotemporal dementia and other dementias?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Frontotemporal dementia and other dementias?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Frontotemporal dementia and other dementias — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.4.4 Vascular Dementia And Dementia With Lewy Bodies

Definition

Vascular dementia (VaD) is cognitive decline due to cerebrovascular disease (multi-infarct, strategic infarct, small vessel disease). Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) is a synucleinopathy with fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism.

Core Clinical Content

Vascular dementia — second most common

15-25% of dementia; preventable via vascular risk factor control

VaD features

Stepwise decline, focal neurological signs, early executive dysfunction, gait disturbance

VaD risk factors

Hypertension, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidaemia, atrial fibrillation

VaD prevention

BP control, antiplatelet, statin, lifestyle modifications

DLB prevalence

10-15% of dementia; male predominance

DLB core features

Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, REM sleep behaviour disorder

DLB supportive features

Severe neuroleptic sensitivity, autonomic dysfunction, hyposmia, depression

DLB vs Parkinson's dementia

DLB: dementia within 1 year of parkinsonism; PDD: parkinsonism precedes dementia by >1 year

DLB treatment

Cholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine); AVOID antipsychotics (severe sensitivity)

Prognosis

VaD: variable, stepwise; DLB: progressive, falls, fluctuating course

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Vascular dementia — second most common15-25% of dementia; preventable via vascular risk factor control
VaD featuresStepwise decline, focal neurological signs, early executive dysfunction, gait di
VaD risk factorsHypertension, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidaemia, atrial fibrillation
VaD preventionBP control, antiplatelet, statin, lifestyle modifications
DLB prevalence10-15% of dementia; male predominance
DLB core featuresFluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, REM sleep behaviour
DLB supportive featuresSevere neuroleptic sensitivity, autonomic dysfunction, hyposmia, depression
DLB vs Parkinson's dementiaDLB: dementia within 1 year of parkinsonism; PDD: parkinsonism precedes dementia
DLB treatmentCholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine); AVOID antipsychotics (severe sensitivi
PrognosisVaD: variable, stepwise; DLB: progressive, falls, fluctuating course

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Vascular dementia and dementia with Lewy bodies?

Vascular dementia (VaD) is cognitive decline due to cerebrovascular disease (multi-infarct, strategic infarct, small vessel disease). Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) is a synucleinopathy with fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Vascular dementia and dementia with Lewy bodies?

Key features include: vascular dementia — second most common, vad features, vad risk factors.

Q3. How is Vascular dementia and dementia with Lewy bodies diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Vascular dementia and dementia with Lewy bodies?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Vascular dementia and dementia with Lewy bodies?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms

▸ 29.4.5 Wernicke Korsakoff Syndrome And Alcohol Related Co

Definition

Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome comprises Wernicke's encephalopathy (acute, reversible with treatment) and Korsakoff syndrome (chronic amnestic syndrome, often irreversible), both due to thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, commonly in alcohol use disorder.

Core Clinical Content

Wernicke's triad

Confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus, 6th nerve palsy)

Wernicke's mechanism

Thiamine deficiency; often precipitated by glucose infusion in deficient patients

Wernicke's treatment

Pabrinex IV (thiamine + pyridoxine) BEFORE glucose; high-dose

Korsakoff syndrome

Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, relatively preserved other cognition

Korsakoff prognosis

Often irreversible; only 20% recover significantly; confabulation may persist

Wernicke to Korsakoff

Untreated Wernicke's progresses to Korsakoff in 80%

Memory in Korsakoff

Anterograde amnesia (cannot form new memories); retrograde amnesia (variable); confabulation

Other alcohol-related cognitive disorders

Alcohol-related dementia (possibly reversible with abstinence)

Marchiafava-Bignami disease

Alcohol-related corpus callosum demyelination; rare

Thiamine-rich foods

Wholegrains, pork, beans, nuts, yeast extract

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Wernicke's triadConfusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus, 6th nerve palsy)
Wernicke's mechanismThiamine deficiency; often precipitated by glucose infusion in deficient patient
Wernicke's treatmentPabrinex IV (thiamine + pyridoxine) BEFORE glucose; high-dose
Korsakoff syndromeAnterograde amnesia, confabulation, relatively preserved other cognition
Korsakoff prognosisOften irreversible; only 20% recover significantly; confabulation may persist
Wernicke to KorsakoffUntreated Wernicke's progresses to Korsakoff in 80%
Memory in KorsakoffAnterograde amnesia (cannot form new memories); retrograde amnesia (variable); c
Other alcohol-related cognitive disordersAlcohol-related dementia (possibly reversible with abstinence)
Marchiafava-Bignami diseaseAlcohol-related corpus callosum demyelination; rare
Thiamine-rich foodsWholegrains, pork, beans, nuts, yeast extract

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome comprises Wernicke's encephalopathy (acute, reversible with treatment) and Korsakoff syndrome (chronic amnestic syndrome, often irreversible), both due to thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, commonly in alcohol use disorder.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Key features include: wernicke's triad, wernicke's mechanism, wernicke's treatment.

Q3. How is Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.4.5 Wernicke Korsakoff Syndrome And Alcohol Related Cognitive Disorders

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Cognitive Disorders

topic: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders

Definition

Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome comprises Wernicke's encephalopathy (acute, reversible with treatment) and Korsakoff syndrome (chronic amnestic syndrome, often irreversible), both due to thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, commonly in alcohol use disorder.

Core Clinical Content

Wernicke's triad

Confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus, 6th nerve palsy)

Wernicke's mechanism

Thiamine deficiency; often precipitated by glucose infusion in deficient patients

Wernicke's treatment

Pabrinex IV (thiamine + pyridoxine) BEFORE glucose; high-dose

Korsakoff syndrome

Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, relatively preserved other cognition

Korsakoff prognosis

Often irreversible; only 20% recover significantly; confabulation may persist

Wernicke to Korsakoff

Untreated Wernicke's progresses to Korsakoff in 80%

Memory in Korsakoff

Anterograde amnesia (cannot form new memories); retrograde amnesia (variable); confabulation

Other alcohol-related cognitive disorders

Alcohol-related dementia (possibly reversible with abstinence)

Marchiafava-Bignami disease

Alcohol-related corpus callosum demyelination; rare

Thiamine-rich foods

Wholegrains, pork, beans, nuts, yeast extract

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome comprises Wernicke's encephalopathy (acute, reversible with treatment) and Korsakoff syndrome (chronic amnestic syndrome, often irreversible), both due to thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, commonly in alcohol use disorder.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Key features include: wernicke's triad, wernicke's mechanism, wernicke's treatment.

Q3. How is Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms

  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.5 Eating Disorders 3
▸ 29.5.1 Anorexia Nervosa

Definition

Anorexia nervosa is an eating disorder characterised by restriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, and disturbance in self-perceived body weight/shape, with significantly low body weight. It often begins in adolescence and is more common in females.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Restriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, body image disturbance, significantly low weight

Types

Restricting type (no binge/purge) and binge-eating/purging type

Prevalence

0.5-1% of females; rare in males; onset 13-20 years

BMI categories (DSM-5)

Mild ≥17, moderate 16-16.99, severe 15-15.99, extreme <15

Physical signs

Lanugo, bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, amenorrhoea, osteoporosis

Investigations

FBC, U&E, Ca/Mg/Phos, LFTs, TFTs, ECG, DEXA for bone density

Hypokalaemia

Common (especially purging type); risk of cardiac arrhythmia

Refeeding syndrome

Hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, fluid overload with reintroduction of nutrition

Refeeding prevention

Start slowly (10-20 kcal/kg/day), supplement thiamine, monitor electrolytes

Treatment

Medical stabilisation + nutritional rehabilitation + psychotherapy (FBT for adolescents, CBT)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DSM-5 criteriaRestriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, body image disturb

BMI categories (DSM-5)Mild ≥17, moderate 16-16.99, severe 15-15.99, extreme <15

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Anorexia nervosa?

Anorexia nervosa is an eating disorder characterised by restriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, and disturbance in self-perceived body weight/shape, with significantly low body weight. It often begins in adolescence and is more common in females.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Anorexia nervosa?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, types, prevalence.

Q3. How is Anorexia nervosa diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Anorexia nervosa?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Anorexia nervosa?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Anorexia nervosa — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.5.2 Binge Eating Disorder And Arfid

Definition

Binge eating disorder (BED) is an eating disorder characterised by recurrent binge eating episodes WITHOUT compensatory behaviours, causing marked distress. ARFID (avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder) is avoidance of food without weight/shape concerns, often in children.

Core Clinical Content

BED criteria

Recurrent binge eating (≥1/week × 3 months) with distress, NO compensatory behaviours

BED prevalence

Most common eating disorder (~2-3%); affects all genders; associated with obesity

BED features

Eating rapidly, eating when not hungry, eating alone, feeling guilty after

BED vs bulimia

BED: no compensatory behaviours; bulimia: recurrent purging/fasting/exercise

BED treatment

CBT (first-line), interpersonal therapy; lisdexamfetamine (FDA approved)

ARFID

Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder; food avoidance WITHOUT body image concerns

ARFID features

Sensory sensitivity, fear of choking/vomiting, lack of interest in eating

ARFID demographics

Children/adolescents; equal M:F; associated with autism, anxiety

ARFID complications

Weight loss, nutritional deficiencies, dependence on supplements

ARFID treatment

Family-based, CBT-AR, exposure therapy; medical/nutritional support

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Binge eating disorder and ARFID?

Binge eating disorder (BED) is an eating disorder characterised by recurrent binge eating episodes WITHOUT compensatory behaviours, causing marked distress. ARFID (avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder) is avoidance of food without weight/shape concerns, often in children.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Binge eating disorder and ARFID?

Key features include: bed criteria, bed prevalence, bed features.

Q3. How is Binge eating disorder and ARFID diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Binge eating disorder and ARFID?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Binge eating disorder and ARFID?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

▸ 29.5.3 Bulimia Nervosa

Definition

Bulimia nervosa is an eating disorder characterised by recurrent episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviours (vomiting, laxatives, excessive exercise) to prevent weight gain, occurring at least weekly for 3 months, with self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape/weight.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Recurrent binge eating + compensatory behaviours ≥1/week × 3 months; self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape

Prevalence

1-2% of females; onset late teens/early 20s

Body weight

Usually normal or slightly above (unlike anorexia)

Binge eating

Eating large amount in discrete period (2 h) with sense of loss of control

Compensatory behaviours

Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, fasting, excessive exercise

Physical signs

Dental enamel erosion, parotid enlargement, calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign), electrolyte disturbance

Hypokalaemia

Common due to vomiting/laxative abuse; risk of arrhythmia

Differential

Anorexia binge-purge type, binge eating disorder, MDD

Treatment

CBT (first-line); SSRI (fluoxetine 60 mg); no medication licensed specifically

Prognosis

Better than anorexia; 70% recover with treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteriaRecurrent binge eating + compensatory behaviours ≥1/week × 3 months; self-evalua
Prevalence1-2% of females; onset late teens/early 20s
Body weightUsually normal or slightly above (unlike anorexia)
Binge eatingEating large amount in discrete period (2 h) with sense of loss of control
Compensatory behavioursSelf-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, fasting, excessive exercise
Physical signsDental enamel erosion, parotid enlargement, calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign
HypokalaemiaCommon due to vomiting/laxative abuse; risk of arrhythmia
DifferentialAnorexia binge-purge type, binge eating disorder, MDD
TreatmentCBT (first-line); SSRI (fluoxetine 60 mg); no medication licensed specifically
PrognosisBetter than anorexia; 70% recover with treatment

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Bulimia nervosa?

Bulimia nervosa is an eating disorder characterised by recurrent episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviours (vomiting, laxatives, excessive exercise) to prevent weight gain, occurring at least weekly for 3 months, with self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape/weight.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bulimia nervosa?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, prevalence, body weight.

Q3. How is Bulimia nervosa diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bulimia nervosa?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Bulimia nervosa?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • Mnemonic for Bulimia nervosa — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Answer: B

  1. Key feature regarding Bulimia nervosa?

A) Usually normal or slightly above (unlike anorexia)

B) Eating large amount in discrete period (2 h) with sense of loss of control

C) Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, fasting, excessive exercise

D) Dental enamel erosion, parotid enlargement, calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign), electro

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Bulimia nervosa?

A) Eating large amount in discrete period (2 h) with sense of loss of control

B) Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, fasting, excessive exercise

C) Dental enamel erosion, parotid enlargement, calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign), electro

D) Common due to vomiting/laxative abuse; risk of arrhythmia

Answer: D

A) Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, fasting, excessive exercise

B) Dental enamel erosion, parotid enlargement, calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign), electro
