Flashcards

Q: DSM-5 criteria?

A: Recurrent binge eating + compensatory behaviours ≥1/week × 3 months; self-evaluation unduly influenc

Q: Prevalence?

A: 1-2% of females; onset late teens/early 20s

Q: Body weight?

A: Usually normal or slightly above (unlike anorexia)

Q: Binge eating?

A: Eating large amount in discrete period (2 h) with sense of loss of control

Q: Compensatory behaviours?

A: Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, fasting, excessive exercise

Q: Physical signs?

A: Dental enamel erosion, parotid enlargement, calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign), electrolyte distu

Q: Hypokalaemia?

A: Common due to vomiting/laxative abuse; risk of arrhythmia

Q: Differential?

A: Anorexia binge-purge type, binge eating disorder, MDD

▸ 29.6 Legal and Ethical Aspects 3
▸ 29.6.1 Ethics Confidentiality And Compulsory Treatment

Definition

Medical ethics in psychiatry involves the four principles of biomedical ethics (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) applied to mental health contexts, including confidentiality, consent, duty to warn, and decisions about compulsory treatment.

Core Clinical Content

Four principles (Beauchamp & Childress)

Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice

Autonomy

Patient's right to self-determination; requires capacity and informed consent

Beneficence

Acting in patient's best interest

Non-maleficence

First do no harm; avoid causing harm

Justice

Fair distribution of resources; treat similar patients similarly

Confidentiality

Default; can be broken in specific circumstances (risk to self/others, public interest)

Duty to warn (Tarasoff)

If patient poses serious threat to identifiable person, clinician has duty to protect (warn)

GMC confidentiality guidance

Disclose only if necessary, lawful, proportionate; inform patient first if possible

Compulsory treatment ethics

Justified when patient lacks capacity or poses serious risk; less restrictive options first

Capacity-based vs risk-based

UK: Mental Health Act (risk-based) vs Mental Capacity Act (capacity-based)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Four principles (Beauchamp & Childress)Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice

BeneficenceActing in patient's best interest
Non-maleficenceFirst do no harm; avoid causing harm
JusticeFair distribution of resources; treat similar patients similarly
ConfidentialityDefault; can be broken in specific circumstances (risk to self/others, public in
Duty to warn (Tarasoff)If patient poses serious threat to identifiable person, clinician has duty to pr
GMC confidentiality guidanceDisclose only if necessary, lawful, proportionate; inform patient first if possi
Compulsory treatment ethicsJustified when patient lacks capacity or poses serious risk; less restrictive op
Capacity-based vs risk-basedUK: Mental Health Act (risk-based) vs Mental Capacity Act (capacity-based)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Ethics, confidentiality, and compulsory treatment?

Medical ethics in psychiatry involves the four principles of biomedical ethics (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) applied to mental health contexts, including confidentiality, consent, duty to warn, and decisions about compulsory treatment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Ethics, confidentiality, and compulsory treatment?

Key features include: four principles (beauchamp & childress), autonomy, beneficence.

Q3. How is Ethics, confidentiality, and compulsory treatment diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Ethics, confidentiality, and compulsory treatment?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Ethics, confidentiality, and compulsory treatment?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Ethics, confidentiality, and compulsory treatment — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.6.2 Mental Capacity Act And Consent

Definition

The Mental Capacity Act (MCA) 2005 provides a legal framework for decision-making for people aged ≥16 who lack capacity to make specific decisions. It is based on 5 principles and supports best-interests decision-making.

Core Clinical Content

MCA 5 principles

Presume capacity; support to make decisions; unwise decisions; best interests; least restrictive option

Two-stage capacity test

1) Diagnostic — does impairment of mind/brain exist? 2) Functional — does impairment affect this decision?

Four functional criteria

Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate (decision at this time)

Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA)

Property/financial LPA + Health/welfare LPA; must be registered

Court of Protection

Makes decisions for those without capacity; appoints Deputies

Independent Mental Capacity Advocate (IMCA)

For serious medical decisions, accommodation changes when no family/friends

Advance Decision (ADRT)

Refusal of treatment in advance; binding if valid and applicable

Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS)

Protect those who lack capacity but are deprived of liberty (care homes, hospitals)

Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS)

Replacing DoLS; new framework under MCA 2019 amendments

Best interests decision

Multi-disciplinary; considers past/present wishes, beliefs, feelings; less restrictive option

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

Independent Mental Capacity Advocate (IMCA)For serious medical decisions, accommodation changes when no family/friends
Advance Decision (ADRT)Refusal of treatment in advance; binding if valid and applicable

Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS)Replacing DoLS; new framework under MCA 2019 amendments

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Mental Capacity Act and consent?

The Mental Capacity Act (MCA) 2005 provides a legal framework for decision-making for people aged ≥16 who lack capacity to make specific decisions. It is based on 5 principles and supports best-interests decision-making.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mental Capacity Act and consent?

Key features include: mca 5 principles, two-stage capacity test, four functional criteria.

Q3. How is Mental Capacity Act and consent diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Mental Capacity Act and consent?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Mental Capacity Act and consent?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Mental Capacity Act and consent — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Core Clinical Content

▸ 29.6.3 Mental Health Act Mha And Compulsory Detention

Definition

The Mental Health Act (MHA) 1983 (amended 2007) provides the legal framework for the assessment, treatment, and detention of people with mental disorders in England and Wales. It allows compulsory treatment for patients who lack capacity or pose a risk to themselves or others.

Core Clinical Content

MHA criteria for detention

Mental disorder of nature/degree warranting detention; appropriate treatment available; risk to self/others

Section 2 (MHA)

Admission for assessment; up to 28 days; AMHP + 2 doctors (one Section 12 approved)

Section 3 (MHA)

Admission for treatment; up to 6 months (renewable)

Section 4 (MHA)

Emergency admission; up to 72 hours; 1 doctor + AMHP (when Section 12 doctor not available)

Section 5(2) (MHA)

Doctor's holding power; up to 72 hours for already admitted patients

Section 135 (MHA)

Warrant to enter premises and remove person to place of safety

Section 136 (MHA)

Police power to remove person from public place to place of safety; up to 24 h

Nearest Relative

Defined by Act; can be displaced by court; has specific rights (request assessment, discharge)

Mental Health Act Tribunals

Patients detained under Section 2/3 can apply for tribunal hearing

Consent to treatment (Part IV)

Section 57 (ECT, neurosurgery) requires patient + SOAD consent; Section 58 (3 months) for detained patients

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Mental Health Act (MHA) and compulsory detention?

The Mental Health Act (MHA) 1983 (amended 2007) provides the legal framework for the assessment, treatment, and detention of people with mental disorders in England and Wales. It allows compulsory treatment for patients who lack capacity or pose a risk to themselves or others.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mental Health Act (MHA) and compulsory detention?

Key features include: mha criteria for detention, section 2 (mha), section 3 (mha).

Q3. How is Mental Health Act (MHA) and compulsory detention diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Mental Health Act (MHA) and compulsory detention?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Mental Health Act (MHA) and compulsory detention?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Mental Health Act (MHA) and compulsory detention — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.7 Medically Unexplained Symptoms 6
▸ 29.7.1 Conversion Disorder Functional Neurological Sympto

Definition

Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) is characterised by one or more symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function incompatible with recognised neurological/medical conditions, not better explained by another disorder, causing significant distress or impairment.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥1 symptom of altered voluntary motor/sensory function; clinical findings provide evidence of incompatibility; symptom not better explained by another disorder; causes distress/impairment

Common presentations

Weakness, paralysis, tremor, dystonia, gait disorder, seizures (PNES), blindness, deafness, anaesthesia

La belle indifférence

Lack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)

Prevalence

~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

Positive signs

Hoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness

Hoover's sign

Hip extension weakness corrects with contralateral hip flexion against resistance

PNES (psychogenic non-epileptic seizures)

Resemble epilepsy but no EEG changes; prolonged, eyes closed, waxing/waning

Risk factors

Recent stress, prior trauma, childhood abuse, female sex

Treatment

Clear positive diagnosis, physiotherapy, CBT, address comorbidities

Prognosis

Initial improvement in 50%; long-term symptoms persist in many

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteria≥1 symptom of altered voluntary motor/sensory function; clinical findings provid
Common presentationsWeakness, paralysis, tremor, dystonia, gait disorder, seizures (PNES), blindness
La belle indifférenceLack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)
Prevalence~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood
Positive signsHoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness
Hoover's signHip extension weakness corrects with contralateral hip flexion against resistanc
PNES (psychogenic non-epileptic seizures)Resemble epilepsy but no EEG changes; prolonged, eyes closed, waxing/waning
Risk factorsRecent stress, prior trauma, childhood abuse, female sex
TreatmentClear positive diagnosis, physiotherapy, CBT, address comorbidities
PrognosisInitial improvement in 50%; long-term symptoms persist in many

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) is characterised by one or more symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function incompatible with recognised neurological/medical conditions, not better explained by another disorder, causing significant distress or impairment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, common presentations, la belle indifférence.

Q3. How is Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.7.1 Conversion Disorder Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder

heading: Medically Unexplained Symptoms

modified: 2026-06-16

Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)

Definition

Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) is characterised by one or more symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function incompatible with recognised neurological/medical conditions, not better explained by another disorder, causing significant distress or impairment.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥1 symptom of altered voluntary motor/sensory function; clinical findings provide evidence of incompatibility; symptom not better explained by another disorder; causes distress/impairment

Common presentations

Weakness, paralysis, tremor, dystonia, gait disorder, seizures (PNES), blindness, deafness, anaesthesia

La belle indifférence

Lack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)

Prevalence

~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

Positive signs

Hoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness

Hoover's sign

Hip extension weakness corrects with contralateral hip flexion against resistance

PNES (psychogenic non-epileptic seizures)

Resemble epilepsy but no EEG changes; prolonged, eyes closed, waxing/waning

Risk factors

Recent stress, prior trauma, childhood abuse, female sex

Treatment

Clear positive diagnosis, physiotherapy, CBT, address comorbidities

Prognosis

Initial improvement in 50%; long-term symptoms persist in many

Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) is characterised by one or more symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function incompatible with recognised neurological/medical conditions, not better explained by another disorder, causing significant distress or impairment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, common presentations, la belle indifférence.

Q3. How is Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

MCQs (10)

  1. Key feature regarding Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

A) ≥1 symptom of altered voluntary motor/sensory function; clinical findings provide evidence

B) Weakness, paralysis, tremor, dystonia, gait disorder, seizures (PNES), blindness, deafness

C) Lack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)

D) ~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

A) Weakness, paralysis, tremor, dystonia, gait disorder, seizures (PNES), blindness, deafness

B) Lack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)

C) ~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

D) Hoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness

Answer: B

  1. Key feature regarding Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

A) Lack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)

B) ~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

C) Hoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness

D) Hip extension weakness corrects with contralateral hip flexion against resistance

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)?

A) ~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

B) Hoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness

C) Hip extension weakness corrects with contralateral hip flexion against resistance

D) Resemble epilepsy but no EEG changes; prolonged, eyes closed, waxing/waning

Answer: D

Flashcards

Q: DSM-5 criteria?

A: ≥1 symptom of altered voluntary motor/sensory function; clinical findings provide evidence of incomp

Q: Common presentations?

A: Weakness, paralysis, tremor, dystonia, gait disorder, seizures (PNES), blindness, deafness, anaesthe

Q: La belle indifférence?

A: Lack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)

Q: Prevalence?

A: ~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

Q: Positive signs?

A: Hoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness

Q: Hoover's sign?

A: Hip extension weakness corrects with contralateral hip flexion against resistance

Q: PNES (psychogenic non-epileptic seizures)?

A: Resemble epilepsy but no EEG changes; prolonged, eyes closed, waxing/waning

Q: Risk factors?

A: Recent stress, prior trauma, childhood abuse, female sex

▸ 29.7.2 Dissociative Disorders And Depersonalisation

Definition

Dissociative disorders are a group of conditions involving disruption of consciousness, memory, identity, emotion, perception, body representation, motor control, or behaviour. Common presentations include dissociative amnesia, fugue, depersonalisation/derealisation, and DID.

Core Clinical Content

Dissociative disorders (F44)

Dissociative amnesia, fugue, stupor, trance, motor, convulsions, anaesthesia

Dissociative amnesia

Inability to recall important autobiographical information; not due to medical condition

Dissociative fugue

Dissociative amnesia + purposeful travel; new identity may be assumed

Depersonalisation/derealisation

Feeling of unreality of self (depersonalisation) or surroundings (derealisation)

DPD (depersonalisation disorder)

Persistent or recurrent; significant distress; not due to substance/medical

Dissociative identity disorder (DID)

Formerly multiple personality disorder; ≥2 distinct personality states

DID controversy

Disputed validity; some consider iatrogenic; high comorbidity with childhood trauma

Dissociation association

Often with childhood trauma, borderline personality, PTSD

Differential

PTSD, complex PTSD, factitious, malingering, neurological (TLE, post-ictal)

Treatment

Trauma-focused therapy; phase-oriented (stabilisation, processing, integration); DBT skills

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Dissociative disorders (F44)Dissociative amnesia, fugue, stupor, trance, motor, convulsions, anaesthesia
Dissociative amnesiaInability to recall important autobiographical information; not due to medical c
Dissociative fugueDissociative amnesia + purposeful travel; new identity may be assumed
Depersonalisation/derealisationFeeling of unreality of self (depersonalisation) or surroundings (derealisation)
DPD (depersonalisation disorder)Persistent or recurrent; significant distress; not due to substance/medical
Dissociative identity disorder (DID)Formerly multiple personality disorder; ≥2 distinct personality states
DID controversyDisputed validity; some consider iatrogenic; high comorbidity with childhood tra
Dissociation associationOften with childhood trauma, borderline personality, PTSD
DifferentialPTSD, complex PTSD, factitious, malingering, neurological (TLE, post-ictal)
TreatmentTrauma-focused therapy; phase-oriented (stabilisation, processing, integration);

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation?

Dissociative disorders are a group of conditions involving disruption of consciousness, memory, identity, emotion, perception, body representation, motor control, or behaviour. Common presentations include dissociative amnesia, fugue, depersonalisation/derealisation, and DID.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation?

Key features include: dissociative disorders (f44), dissociative amnesia, dissociative fugue.

Q3. How is Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.7.3 Factitious Disorder And Malingering

Definition

Factitious disorder is the intentional production of symptoms to assume the sick role (no external gain). Malingering is the intentional production of symptoms for external gain (avoiding work, obtaining drugs, financial compensation). Both differ from somatic symptom disorder (unconscious).

Core Clinical Content

Factitious disorder criteria

Falsification of signs/symptoms, presents self as ill, deceptive behaviour, no external incentives

Munchausen syndrome

Severe chronic form of factitious disorder; wandering, dramatic stories, multiple scars

Malingering

Intentional production for external gain (work avoidance, drugs, money)

Malingering — DSM-5

Not a psychiatric diagnosis; listed as 'Other conditions for clinical focus'

Distinguish from SSD

SSD: unconscious; factitious: conscious (no external gain); malingering: conscious (external gain)

Factitious disorder imposed on another (formerly Munchausen by proxy)

Caregiver produces illness in child; child protection issue

Management

Direct but non-confrontational discussion; avoid unnecessary investigations; address underlying needs

Prognosis

Poor; chronic, recurrent; difficult to treat

Legal aspects

Factitious disorder imposed on another = criminal offence; mandatory reporting

Sick role

Primary motivation in factitious disorder (no external gain)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Factitious disorder criteriaFalsification of signs/symptoms, presents self as ill, deceptive behaviour, no e
Munchausen syndromeSevere chronic form of factitious disorder; wandering, dramatic stories, multipl
MalingeringIntentional production for external gain (work avoidance, drugs, money)
Malingering — DSM-5Not a psychiatric diagnosis; listed as 'Other conditions for clinical focus'
Distinguish from SSDSSD: unconscious; factitious: conscious (no external gain); malingering: conscio
Factitious disorder imposed on another (formerly MCaregiver produces illness in child; child protection issue
ManagementDirect but non-confrontational discussion; avoid unnecessary investigations; add
PrognosisPoor; chronic, recurrent; difficult to treat
Legal aspectsFactitious disorder imposed on another = criminal offence; mandatory reporting
Sick rolePrimary motivation in factitious disorder (no external gain)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Factitious disorder and malingering?

Factitious disorder is the intentional production of symptoms to assume the sick role (no external gain). Malingering is the intentional production of symptoms for external gain (avoiding work, obtaining drugs, financial compensation). Both differ from somatic symptom disorder (unconscious).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Factitious disorder and malingering?

Key features include: factitious disorder criteria, munchausen syndrome, malingering.

Q3. How is Factitious disorder and malingering diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Factitious disorder and malingering?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Factitious disorder and malingering?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Factitious disorder and malingering — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

  1. Patient presents with features of Factitious disorder and malingering. Which is most appropriate? | Not a psychiatric diagnosis; listed as 'Other conditions for clinical focus'
  1. Patient presents with features of Factitious disorder and malingering. Which is most appropriate? | SSD: unconscious; factitious: conscious (no external gain); malingering: conscious (extern
  1. Patient presents with features of Factitious disorder and malingering. Which is most appropriate? | Caregiver produces illness in child; child protection issue
  1. Patient presents with features of Factitious disorder and malingering. Which is most appropriate? | Direct but non-confrontational discussion; avoid unnecessary investigations; address under
  1. Patient presents with features of Factitious disorder and malingering. Which is most appropriate? | Poor; chronic, recurrent; difficult to treat
  1. Patient presents with features of Factitious disorder and malingering. Which is most appropriate? | Factitious disorder imposed on another = criminal offence; mandatory reporting
  1. Patient presents with features of Factitious disorder and malingering. Which is most appropriate? | Primary motivation in factitious disorder (no external gain)

Flashcards

Q: Factitious disorder criteria?

A: Falsification of signs/symptoms, presents self as ill, deceptive behaviour, no external incentives

Q: Munchausen syndrome?

A: Severe chronic form of factitious disorder; wandering, dramatic stories, multiple scars

Q: Malingering?

A: Intentional production for external gain (work avoidance, drugs, money)

Q: Malingering — DSM-5?

A: Not a psychiatric diagnosis; listed as 'Other conditions for clinical focus'

Q: Distinguish from SSD?

A: SSD: unconscious; factitious: conscious (no external gain); malingering: conscious (external gain)

Q: Factitious disorder imposed on another (formerly Munchausen by proxy)?

A: Caregiver produces illness in child; child protection issue

Q: Management?

A: Direct but non-confrontational discussion; avoid unnecessary investigations; address underlying need

Q: Prognosis?

A: Poor; chronic, recurrent; difficult to treat

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Factitious disorder criteria
  2. B — Munchausen syndrome
  3. C — Malingering
  4. D — Malingering — DSM-5
  5. A — Distinguish from SSD
  6. B — Factitious disorder imposed on another (formerly Munchausen by proxy)
  7. C — Management
  8. D — Prognosis
  9. A — Legal aspects
  10. B — Sick role

▸ 29.7.4 Illness Anxiety Disorder

Definition

Illness anxiety disorder (formerly hypochondriasis) is characterised by preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness, with high health anxiety, despite minimal or no somatic symptoms. Patients have excessive health-related behaviours (seeking reassurance, checking body) or maladaptive avoidance.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Preoccupation with having/acquiring serious illness; somatic symptoms not present or mild; high anxiety; excessive health-related behaviours; ≥6 months

Distinguish from SSD

IAD: minimal somatic symptoms but high anxiety; SSD: real distressing symptoms + excessive reaction

Distinguish from OCD

IAD: focus on having illness; OCD: focus on contamination/harm regardless of health

Distinguish from somatic delusion

IAD: recognise fear as excessive (insight preserved); somatic delusion: fixed, unshakeable belief

Prevalence

1-2% of general population

Comorbidity

Anxiety, depression, OCD, personality disorders

Behaviour patterns

Reassurance seeking OR avoidance (cancel appointments, avoid news)

Treatment

CBT (first-line); SSRI; minimise investigations/reassurance-seeking

Reassurance trap

Brief reassurance helps; extensive reassurance-seeking reinforces anxiety

Prognosis

Chronic, fluctuating; can improve with treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteriaPreoccupation with having/acquiring serious illness; somatic symptoms not presen
Distinguish from SSDIAD: minimal somatic symptoms but high anxiety; SSD: real distressing symptoms +
Distinguish from OCDIAD: focus on having illness; OCD: focus on contamination/harm regardless of hea
Distinguish from somatic delusionIAD: recognise fear as excessive (insight preserved); somatic delusion: fixed, u
Prevalence1-2% of general population
ComorbidityAnxiety, depression, OCD, personality disorders
Behaviour patternsReassurance seeking OR avoidance (cancel appointments, avoid news)
TreatmentCBT (first-line); SSRI; minimise investigations/reassurance-seeking
Reassurance trapBrief reassurance helps; extensive reassurance-seeking reinforces anxiety
PrognosisChronic, fluctuating; can improve with treatment

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Illness anxiety disorder?

Illness anxiety disorder (formerly hypochondriasis) is characterised by preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness, with high health anxiety, despite minimal or no somatic symptoms. Patients have excessive health-related behaviours (seeking reassurance, checking body) or maladaptive avoidance.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Illness anxiety disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, distinguish from ssd, distinguish from ocd.

Q3. How is Illness anxiety disorder diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Illness anxiety disorder?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Illness anxiety disorder?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Illness anxiety disorder — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

▸ 29.7.5 Somatic Symptom Disorder

Definition

Somatic symptom disorder (formerly somatisation disorder / somatoform disorder) is characterised by one or more distressing somatic symptoms with excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviours related to the symptoms, persisting >6 months.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥1 distressing somatic symptom; excessive thoughts/feelings/behaviours; ≥6 months

Prevalence

5-7% of general population; more common in women; often presents in primary care

Common symptoms

Pain, fatigue, GI, neurological, sexual — often multiple

Key feature

Distress disproportionate to objective findings; NOT a 'malingered' condition

Distinguish from factitious/malingering

SSD: genuine symptoms, not consciously produced; factitious: conscious production; malingering: conscious production for external gain

Comorbidity

Depression, anxiety, other medical conditions (sometimes)

Management principles

Validate symptoms; regular short appointments; avoid unnecessary investigations

Treatment

CBT (first-line); SSRI for comorbid depression; address health anxiety

Prognosis

Chronic, fluctuating; early treatment improves outcome

Avoid

Dismissing as 'all in the head'; excessive investigations reinforce illness behaviour

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteria≥1 distressing somatic symptom; excessive thoughts/feelings/behaviours; ≥6 month
Prevalence5-7% of general population; more common in women; often presents in primary care
Common symptomsPain, fatigue, GI, neurological, sexual — often multiple
Key featureDistress disproportionate to objective findings; NOT a 'malingered' condition
Distinguish from factitious/malingeringSSD: genuine symptoms, not consciously produced; factitious: conscious productio
ComorbidityDepression, anxiety, other medical conditions (sometimes)
Management principlesValidate symptoms; regular short appointments; avoid unnecessary investigations
TreatmentCBT (first-line); SSRI for comorbid depression; address health anxiety
PrognosisChronic, fluctuating; early treatment improves outcome
AvoidDismissing as 'all in the head'; excessive investigations reinforce illness beha

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Somatic symptom disorder?

Somatic symptom disorder (formerly somatisation disorder / somatoform disorder) is characterised by one or more distressing somatic symptoms with excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviours related to the symptoms, persisting >6 months.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Somatic symptom disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, prevalence, common symptoms.

Q3. How is Somatic symptom disorder diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Somatic symptom disorder?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Somatic symptom disorder?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

▸ 29.8 Mood Disorders 5
▸ 29.8.1 Bipolar Disorder Type I And Ii

Definition

Bipolar disorder is a recurrent mood disorder characterised by episodes of mania (type I) or hypomania (type II) alternating with depressive episodes. The lifetime prevalence is ~2-3%.

Core Clinical Content

Bipolar I

Mania (≥1 week, hospitalisation may be needed) + depressive episodes

Bipolar II

Hypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania

Mania symptoms (DIG FAST)

Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase, Sleep deficit, Talkativeness

Mania duration

≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

Hypomania

Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

Mixed features

Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

First-line for acute mania

Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapine)

Long-term prophylaxis

Lithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine

Lithium monitoring

TFTs (hypothyroid), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 months

Antidepressant caution

Can trigger mania; use with mood stabiliser in bipolar

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Bipolar IMania (≥1 week, hospitalisation may be needed) + depressive episodes
Bipolar IIHypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania
Mania symptoms (DIG FAST)Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase,
Mania duration≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)
HypomaniaSimilar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis
Mixed featuresMania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk
First-line for acute maniaLithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapi
Long-term prophylaxisLithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine
Lithium monitoringTFTs (hypothyroid), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 mont
Antidepressant cautionCan trigger mania; use with mood stabiliser in bipolar

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

Bipolar disorder is a recurrent mood disorder characterised by episodes of mania (type I) or hypomania (type II) alternating with depressive episodes. The lifetime prevalence is ~2-3%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

Key features include: bipolar i, bipolar ii, mania symptoms (dig fast). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Bipolar disorder type I and II diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bipolar disorder type I and II?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Bipolar disorder type I and II — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

MCQs (10)

  1. Key feature of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Mania (≥1 week, hospitalisation may be needed) + depressive episodes

B) Hypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania

C) Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase,

D) ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

Answer: A

  1. Regarding Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Hypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania

B) Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase,

C) ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

D) Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true about Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase,

B) ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

C) Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

D) Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

Answer: C

  1. Diagnostic criterion for Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

B) Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

C) Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

D) Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapi

Answer: D

  1. Management of Bipolar disorder type I and II includes?

A) Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

B) Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

C) Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapi

D) Lithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine

Answer: A

  1. Prognosis of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

B) Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapi

C) Lithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine

D) TFTs (hypothyroid), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 mont

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapi

B) Lithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine

C) TFTs (hypothyroid), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 mont

D) Can trigger mania; use with mood stabiliser in bipolar

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Mania (≥1 week, hospitalisation may be needed) + depressive episodes

B) Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase,

C) Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

D) Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapi

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Hypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania

B) ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

C) Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

D) Lithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Bipolar disorder type I and II?

A) Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase,

B) Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

C) Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapi

D) TFTs (hypothyroid), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 mont

Answer: C

Flashcards

Q: Bipolar I?

A: Mania (≥1 week, hospitalisation may be needed) + depressive episodes

Q: Bipolar II?

A: Hypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania

Q: Mania symptoms (DIG FAST)?

A: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase, Sleep deficit, Talkativeness

Q: Mania duration?

A: ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

Q: Hypomania?

A: Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

Q: Mixed features?

A: Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

Q: First-line for acute mania?

A: Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapine)

Q: Long-term prophylaxis?

A: Lithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine

▸ 29.8.2 Depression In Medical Illness

Definition

Depression comorbid with medical illness is common, often under-recognised, and worsens medical outcomes. It occurs in 20-40% of patients with chronic medical conditions and requires active screening and treatment.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence in medical illness

20-40% in chronic conditions (cancer, stroke, MI, diabetes)

Post-stroke depression

~30-40%; peaks 3-6 months post-stroke; often missed

Post-MI depression

~20%; increases mortality and reinfarction risk

Cancer-related depression

Common in pancreatic, lung, breast; requires active screening

Parkinson's disease

Depression in 40-50%; may precede motor symptoms

Hypothyroidism

Common cause of depressive symptoms; check TFTs

Diagnosis in medical illness

Same criteria; somatic symptoms less specific in medical patients

Treatment

SSRI first-line; mirtazapine useful with insomnia/appetite loss

Drug interactions

SSRIs + tramadol (serotonin syndrome), SSRIs + warfarin (bleeding)

Bereavement-related depression

Distinguish normal grief from MDD; treat if severe/prolonged

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence in medical illness20-40% in chronic conditions (cancer, stroke, MI, diabetes)
Post-stroke depression~30-40%; peaks 3-6 months post-stroke; often missed
Post-MI depression~20%; increases mortality and reinfarction risk
Cancer-related depressionCommon in pancreatic, lung, breast; requires active screening
Parkinson's diseaseDepression in 40-50%; may precede motor symptoms
HypothyroidismCommon cause of depressive symptoms; check TFTs
Diagnosis in medical illnessSame criteria; somatic symptoms less specific in medical patients
TreatmentSSRI first-line; mirtazapine useful with insomnia/appetite loss
Drug interactionsSSRIs + tramadol (serotonin syndrome), SSRIs + warfarin (bleeding)
Bereavement-related depressionDistinguish normal grief from MDD; treat if severe/prolonged

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Depression in medical illness?

Depression comorbid with medical illness is common, often under-recognised, and worsens medical outcomes. It occurs in 20-40% of patients with chronic medical conditions and requires active screening and treatment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Depression in medical illness?

Key features include: prevalence in medical illness, post-stroke depression, post-mi depression. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Depression in medical illness diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Depression in medical illness?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Depression in medical illness?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Depression in medical illness — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

C) Common cause of depressive symptoms; check TFTs

D) SSRI first-line; mirtazapine useful with insomnia/appetite loss

▸ 29.8.3 Major Depressive Disorder

Definition

Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a mood disorder characterised by persistent low mood and/or anhedonia for at least 2 weeks, with associated cognitive, behavioural, and somatic symptoms causing significant functional impairment.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria (SIG E CAPS)

Sleep, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation + depressed mood or anhedonia ≥2 weeks

At least 5 of 9 symptoms

Must include either depressed mood OR anhedonia

Duration

≥2 weeks (usually much longer)

Prevalence

Lifetime ~15-20%; 2x more common in women

Risk factors

Previous episode, family history, chronic illness, substance use, stress, loss

Suicide risk

~15% of severe MDD; highest in first month of treatment

First-line treatment

SSRI or SNRI + CBT or IPT (combination > monotherapy)

Duration of treatment

Continue for 6-12 months after remission (first episode); longer for recurrent

ECT

For severe, treatment-resistant, psychotic, or life-threatening depression

Antidepressant onset

2-4 weeks for full effect; assess at 4-6 weeks

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteria (SIG E CAPS)Sleep, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration, Appetite change, Psycho
At least 5 of 9 symptomsMust include either depressed mood OR anhedonia
Duration≥2 weeks (usually much longer)
PrevalenceLifetime ~15-20%; 2x more common in women
Risk factorsPrevious episode, family history, chronic illness, substance use, stress, loss
Suicide risk~15% of severe MDD; highest in first month of treatment
First-line treatmentSSRI or SNRI + CBT or IPT (combination > monotherapy)
Duration of treatmentContinue for 6-12 months after remission (first episode); longer for recurrent
ECTFor severe, treatment-resistant, psychotic, or life-threatening depression
Antidepressant onset2-4 weeks for full effect; assess at 4-6 weeks

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Major depressive disorder?

Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a mood disorder characterised by persistent low mood and/or anhedonia for at least 2 weeks, with associated cognitive, behavioural, and somatic symptoms causing significant functional impairment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Major depressive disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria (sig e caps), at least 5 of 9 symptoms, duration. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Major depressive disorder diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Major depressive disorder?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Major depressive disorder?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Major depressive disorder — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

  1. What is the typical course of Major depressive disorder? | Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  1. Which complication should be screened for in Major depressive disorder? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  1. Duration criterion for Major depressive disorder? | Lifetime ~15-20%; 2x more common in women
  1. Most appropriate referral for Major depressive disorder? | Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  1. Differential diagnosis of Major depressive disorder includes? | Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

Flashcards

Q: DSM-5 criteria (SIG E CAPS)?

A: Sleep, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation + depressed mood or anhedonia ≥2 weeks

Q: At least 5 of 9 symptoms?

A: Must include either depressed mood OR anhedonia

Q: Duration?

A: ≥2 weeks (usually much longer)

Q: Prevalence?

A: Lifetime ~15-20%; 2x more common in women

Q: Risk factors?

A: Previous episode, family history, chronic illness, substance use, stress, loss

Q: Suicide risk?

A: ~15% of severe MDD; highest in first month of treatment

Q: First-line treatment?

A: SSRI or SNRI + CBT or IPT (combination > monotherapy)

Q: Duration of treatment?

A: Continue for 6-12 months after remission (first episode); longer for recurrent

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Major depressive disorder?
  2. B — Regarding Major depressive disorder?
  3. C — Which of the following is true about Major depressive disorder?
  4. D — Diagnostic criterion for Major depressive disorder?
  5. A — Management of Major depressive disorder includes?
  6. B — Prognosis of Major depressive disorder?
  7. C — Complication of Major depressive disorder?
  8. A — Epidemiology of Major depressive disorder?
  9. B — Risk factor for Major depressive disorder?
  10. C — First-line treatment for Major depressive disorder?

  2. Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  3. Must include either depressed mood OR anhedonia
  4. Sleep, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration, Appetite change, Psycho
  5. ≥2 weeks (usually much longer)
  6. Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  7. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  8. Lifetime ~15-20%; 2x more common in women
  9. Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  10. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

▸ 29.8.4 Persistent Depressive Disorder Dysthymia

Definition

Persistent depressive disorder (formerly dysthymia) is a chronic form of depression with depressed mood for most of the day, more days than not, for at least 2 years (1 year in children/adolescents).

Core Clinical Content

Duration

≥2 years (1 year in children/adolescents)

Symptoms

Same as MDD but fewer symptoms (≥2 of 6: poor appetite, sleep disturbance, low energy, low self-esteem, poor concentration, hopelessness)

Course

Chronic, often lifelong; less acute episodes than MDD

Prevalence

2-3% of adults; 1.5-3x more common in women

Comorbidity

High comorbidity with anxiety, substance use, personality disorders

Differential

MDD (episodic), cyclothymia, depression secondary to medical condition

Treatment

Same as MDD: SSRI/SNRI + psychotherapy; longer-term maintenance

Response rate

Lower than MDD; chronic course limits response

Risk of MDD

May develop superimposed MDD ('double depression')

Function

Significant functional impairment despite mild symptoms

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Duration≥2 years (1 year in children/adolescents)
SymptomsSame as MDD but fewer symptoms (≥2 of 6: poor appetite, sleep disturbance, low e
CourseChronic, often lifelong; less acute episodes than MDD
Prevalence2-3% of adults; 1.5-3x more common in women
ComorbidityHigh comorbidity with anxiety, substance use, personality disorders
DifferentialMDD (episodic), cyclothymia, depression secondary to medical condition
TreatmentSame as MDD: SSRI/SNRI + psychotherapy; longer-term maintenance
Response rateLower than MDD; chronic course limits response
Risk of MDDMay develop superimposed MDD ('double depression')
FunctionSignificant functional impairment despite mild symptoms

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)?

Persistent depressive disorder (formerly dysthymia) is a chronic form of depression with depressed mood for most of the day, more days than not, for at least 2 years (1 year in children/adolescents).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)?

Key features include: duration, symptoms, course. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.8.5 Postpartum Depression And Puerperal Psychosis

Definition

Postpartum depression is a major depressive episode occurring within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months of delivery. Puerperal psychosis is a severe postpartum psychiatric emergency with mania, depression, and psychosis, typically presenting within 2 weeks of delivery.

Core Clinical Content

Baby blues

Mild mood lability, tearful, 3-5 days postpartum, resolves by 10-14 days

Postpartum depression

Major depression within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; 10-15% of mothers

Risk factors

Previous postpartum depression, family history, perinatal complications, lack of support

Puerperal psychosis

Psychiatric emergency; onset within 2 weeks; risk of infanticide

Puerperal psychosis features

Mania, depression, psychosis, disorganised behaviour, delirium-like features

Treatment of postpartum depression

SSRI (sertraline, sertraline safe in breastfeeding); CBT; ECT for severe

Treatment of puerperal psychosis

Urgent admission, antipsychotic, mood stabiliser; mother-baby unit if available

Breastfeeding considerations

Sertraline preferred (low levels in breast milk); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines

Suicide risk in postpartum

Suicide is a leading cause of maternal death in first year

Infanticide risk

Puerperal psychosis with command hallucinations or severe depression; urgent assessment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Baby bluesMild mood lability, tearful, 3-5 days postpartum, resolves by 10-14 days
Postpartum depressionMajor depression within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; 10-15% of mothers
Risk factorsPrevious postpartum depression, family history, perinatal complications, lack of
Puerperal psychosisPsychiatric emergency; onset within 2 weeks; risk of infanticide
Puerperal psychosis featuresMania, depression, psychosis, disorganised behaviour, delirium-like features
Treatment of postpartum depressionSSRI (sertraline, sertraline safe in breastfeeding); CBT; ECT for severe
Treatment of puerperal psychosisUrgent admission, antipsychotic, mood stabiliser; mother-baby unit if available
Breastfeeding considerationsSertraline preferred (low levels in breast milk); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines
Suicide risk in postpartumSuicide is a leading cause of maternal death in first year
Infanticide riskPuerperal psychosis with command hallucinations or severe depression; urgent ass

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis?

Postpartum depression is a major depressive episode occurring within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months of delivery. Puerperal psychosis is a severe postpartum psychiatric emergency with mania, depression, and psychosis, typically presenting within 2 weeks of delivery.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis?

Key features include: baby blues, postpartum depression, risk factors. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.9 Personality Disorders 8
▸ 29.9.1 Antisocial And Other Cluster B Personality Disorde

Definition

Antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by disregard for and violation of the rights of others, occurring after age 18, with evidence of conduct disorder before age 15.

Core Clinical Content

ASPD criteria

≥3 of 15: failure to conform to laws, deceitfulness, impulsivity, irritability, recklessness, irresponsibility, lack of remorse

Age requirement

≥18 years; evidence of conduct disorder <15 years

Prevalence

3% men, 1% women; higher in prisons

Conduct disorder

Repetitive violation of others' rights/age-appropriate norms in <18 years

Comorbidity

Substance use (80%), mood/anxiety disorders, ADHD, other PDs

Treatment

Difficult to treat; poor engagement; no effective pharmacotherapy

Therapy approaches

CBT, schema therapy, therapeutic community (e.g., Henderson Hospital)

Borderline vs Antisocial

BPD: self-harm, fear of abandonment; ASPD: aggression to others, lack of remorse

Other Cluster B

Histrionic (attention-seeking, dramatic), Narcissistic (grandiosity, lack of empathy)

Prognosis

Improves with age (40+); criminal behaviour often decreases; substance use continues

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ASPD criteria≥3 of 15: failure to conform to laws, deceitfulness, impulsivity, irritability,
Age requirement≥18 years; evidence of conduct disorder <15 years
Prevalence3% men, 1% women; higher in prisons
Conduct disorderRepetitive violation of others' rights/age-appropriate norms in <18 years
ComorbiditySubstance use (80%), mood/anxiety disorders, ADHD, other PDs
TreatmentDifficult to treat; poor engagement; no effective pharmacotherapy
Therapy approachesCBT, schema therapy, therapeutic community (e.g., Henderson Hospital)
Borderline vs AntisocialBPD: self-harm, fear of abandonment; ASPD: aggression to others, lack of remorse
Other Cluster BHistrionic (attention-seeking, dramatic), Narcissistic (grandiosity, lack of emp
PrognosisImproves with age (40+); criminal behaviour often decreases; substance use conti

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

Antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by disregard for and violation of the rights of others, occurring after age 18, with evidence of conduct disorder before age 15.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

Key features include: aspd criteria, age requirement, prevalence. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.9.1 Antisocial And Other Cluster B Personality Disorders

tags: [medicine, medical-psychiatry, davidson, full-fcps-mrcp-note]

chapter: Medical Psychiatry

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Personality Disorders

topic: Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP exam — key topic in liaison psychiatry. See MCQs/SBAs for high-yield exam content."

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders

Definition

Antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by disregard for and violation of the rights of others, occurring after age 18, with evidence of conduct disorder before age 15.

Core Clinical Content

ASPD criteria

≥3 of 15: failure to conform to laws, deceitfulness, impulsivity, irritability, recklessness, irresponsibility, lack of remorse

Age requirement

≥18 years; evidence of conduct disorder <15 years

Prevalence

3% men, 1% women; higher in prisons

Conduct disorder

Repetitive violation of others' rights/age-appropriate norms in <18 years

Comorbidity

Substance use (80%), mood/anxiety disorders, ADHD, other PDs

Treatment

Difficult to treat; poor engagement; no effective pharmacotherapy

Therapy approaches

CBT, schema therapy, therapeutic community (e.g., Henderson Hospital)

Borderline vs Antisocial

BPD: self-harm, fear of abandonment; ASPD: aggression to others, lack of remorse

Other Cluster B

Histrionic (attention-seeking, dramatic), Narcissistic (grandiosity, lack of empathy)

Prognosis

Improves with age (40+); criminal behaviour often decreases; substance use continues

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ASPD criteria≥3 of 15: failure to conform to laws, deceitfulness, impulsivity, irritability,
Age requirement≥18 years; evidence of conduct disorder <15 years
Prevalence3% men, 1% women; higher in prisons
Conduct disorderRepetitive violation of others' rights/age-appropriate norms in <18 years
ComorbiditySubstance use (80%), mood/anxiety disorders, ADHD, other PDs
TreatmentDifficult to treat; poor engagement; no effective pharmacotherapy
Therapy approachesCBT, schema therapy, therapeutic community (e.g., Henderson Hospital)
Borderline vs AntisocialBPD: self-harm, fear of abandonment; ASPD: aggression to others, lack of remorse
Other Cluster BHistrionic (attention-seeking, dramatic), Narcissistic (grandiosity, lack of emp
PrognosisImproves with age (40+); criminal behaviour often decreases; substance use conti

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

Antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by disregard for and violation of the rights of others, occurring after age 18, with evidence of conduct disorder before age 15.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

Key features include: aspd criteria, age requirement, prevalence. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antisocial and other Cluster B personality disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

▸ 29.9.2 Borderline Personality Disorder Bpd

Definition

Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by pervasive instability in mood, interpersonal relationships, self-image, and behaviour, with marked impulsivity beginning in early adulthood.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, suicidal behaviour, affective instability, chronic emptiness, inappropriate anger, dissociative/paranoid ideation

Prevalence

~1-2% of population; 75% female

Comorbidity

Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs

Suicide risk

10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)

First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

DBT components

Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

Other therapies

Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy (TFP)

Pharmacotherapy

No licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity

Hospitalisation

Reserved for imminent risk of self-harm; brief; avoid reinforcement of maladaptive patterns

Prognosis

Improves with age; 50% remit by 10 years; good response to DBT

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteria≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity
Prevalence~1-2% of population; 75% female
ComorbidityDepression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs
Suicide risk10x general population; ~8% die by suicide
Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching
DBT componentsMindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness
Other therapiesMentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy
PharmacotherapyNo licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity
HospitalisationReserved for imminent risk of self-harm; brief; avoid reinforcement of maladapti
PrognosisImproves with age; 50% remit by 10 years; good response to DBT

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by pervasive instability in mood, interpersonal relationships, self-image, and behaviour, with marked impulsivity beginning in early adulthood.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, prevalence, comorbidity. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Borderline personality disorder (BPD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Borderline personality disorder (BPD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

MCQs (10)

  1. Key feature of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

A) ≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity

B) ~1-2% of population; 75% female

C) Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs

D) 10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

Answer: A

  1. Regarding Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

A) ~1-2% of population; 75% female

B) Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs

C) 10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

D) First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

B) 10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

C) First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

D) Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

Answer: C

A) 10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

B) First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

C) Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

D) Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy

Answer: D

  1. Management of Borderline personality disorder (BPD) includes?

A) First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

B) Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

C) Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy

D) No licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity

Answer: A

  1. Prognosis of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

A) Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

B) Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy

C) No licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity

D) Reserved for imminent risk of self-harm; brief; avoid reinforcement of maladapti

Answer: B

  1. Complication of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

A) Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy

B) No licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity

C) Reserved for imminent risk of self-harm; brief; avoid reinforcement of maladapti

D) Improves with age; 50% remit by 10 years; good response to DBT

Answer: C

  1. Epidemiology of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

A) ≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity

B) Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs

C) First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

D) Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy

Answer: A

  1. Risk factor for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

A) ~1-2% of population; 75% female

B) 10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

C) Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

D) No licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity

Answer: B

  1. First-line treatment for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

A) Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs

B) First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

C) Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy

D) Reserved for imminent risk of self-harm; brief; avoid reinforcement of maladapti

Answer: C

  1. Patient presents with features of Borderline personality disorder (BPD). Most appropriate first-line? | ≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity
  1. Which investigation is most useful in Borderline personality disorder (BPD)? | Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  1. What is the prevalence of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)? | ~1-2% of population; 75% female
  1. First-line management of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)? | ≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity
  1. Which is a recognised risk factor for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)? | Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs
  1. What is the typical course of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)? | Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis

Flashcards

Q: DSM-5 criteria?

A: ≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, suicidal behaviour, affective instability, chronic emptiness, inappropriate anger, dissociative/paranoid ideation

Q: Prevalence?

A: ~1-2% of population; 75% female

Q: Comorbidity?

A: Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs

Q: Suicide risk?

A: 10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

Q: Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)?

A: First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

Q: DBT components?

A: Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

Q: Other therapies?

A: Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy (TFP)

Q: Pharmacotherapy?

A: No licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity

Answer Key with Explanations
