  4. D — Diagnostic criterion for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?
  5. A — Management of Borderline personality disorder (BPD) includes?
  6. B — Prognosis of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?
  7. C — Complication of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?
  8. A — Epidemiology of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?
  9. B — Risk factor for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?
  10. C — First-line treatment for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

SBAs

  2. Clinical assessment; investigation per specific criteria
  3. ~1-2% of population; 75% female
  4. ≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity
  5. Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs
  6. Chronic, fluctuating; treatment improves prognosis
  7. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content
  8. 10x general population; ~8% die by suicide
  9. Mental health team (psychiatrist, psychologist, community team) per severity
  10. Per clinical assessment; see Core Content

▸ 29.9.3 Cluster A Personality Disorders Paranoid Schizoid

Definition

Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) are characterised by odd, eccentric, or paranoid thinking and behaviour. They may share features with schizophrenia spectrum disorders but are typically egosyntonic and less severe.

Core Clinical Content

Cluster A

Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)

Paranoid PD

Pervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudges

Paranoid PD vs delusional disorder

PD: pervasive pattern (not fixed delusion); DD: non-bizarre delusion, fixed

Schizoid PD

Detachment from social relationships; restricted emotional expression; indifference to praise/criticism

Schizoid vs avoidant

Schizoid: indifferent to relationships; avoidant: wants but fears relationships

Schizotypal PD

Acute discomfort with close relationships; cognitive/perceptual distortions; eccentric behaviour

Schizotypal vs schizophrenia

Schizotypal: brief/transient psychotic symptoms, reality testing preserved, egosyntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis

Cluster A vs schizophrenia

Cluster A: subtle, chronic, ego-syntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis, deterioration

Treatment

Difficult to treat; low engagement; support; cautious use of antipsychotics for transient psychosis

Prognosis

Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Cluster AOdd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)
Paranoid PDPervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudge

Schizoid PDDetachment from social relationships; restricted emotional expression; indiffere
Schizoid vs avoidantSchizoid: indifferent to relationships; avoidant: wants but fears relationships
Schizotypal PDAcute discomfort with close relationships; cognitive/perceptual distortions; ecc
Schizotypal vs schizophreniaSchizotypal: brief/transient psychotic symptoms, reality testing preserved, egos
Cluster A vs schizophreniaCluster A: subtle, chronic, ego-syntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis, deteri

PrognosisStable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schiz

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) are characterised by odd, eccentric, or paranoid thinking and behaviour. They may share features with schizophrenia spectrum disorders but are typically egosyntonic and less severe.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Key features include: cluster a, paranoid pd, paranoid pd vs delusional disorder.

Q3. How is Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

davidson_chapter: Chapter 31: Medical psychiatry

heading: Personality Disorders

topic: Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)

modified: 2026-06-16

Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)

Definition

Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) are characterised by odd, eccentric, or paranoid thinking and behaviour. They may share features with schizophrenia spectrum disorders but are typically egosyntonic and less severe.

Core Clinical Content

Cluster A

Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)

Paranoid PD

Pervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudges

Paranoid PD vs delusional disorder

PD: pervasive pattern (not fixed delusion); DD: non-bizarre delusion, fixed

Schizoid PD

Detachment from social relationships; restricted emotional expression; indifference to praise/criticism

Schizoid vs avoidant

Schizoid: indifferent to relationships; avoidant: wants but fears relationships

Schizotypal PD

Acute discomfort with close relationships; cognitive/perceptual distortions; eccentric behaviour

Schizotypal vs schizophrenia

Schizotypal: brief/transient psychotic symptoms, reality testing preserved, egosyntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis

Cluster A vs schizophrenia

Cluster A: subtle, chronic, ego-syntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis, deterioration

Treatment

Difficult to treat; low engagement; support; cautious use of antipsychotics for transient psychosis

Prognosis

Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

Q1. What is the definition of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) are characterised by odd, eccentric, or paranoid thinking and behaviour. They may share features with schizophrenia spectrum disorders but are typically egosyntonic and less severe.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Key features include: cluster a, paranoid pd, paranoid pd vs delusional disorder.

Q3. How is Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

C) Difficult to treat; low engagement; support; cautious use of antipsychotics for transient

D) Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

Answer: C

  1. Key feature regarding Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

A) Cluster A: subtle, chronic, ego-syntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis, deterioration

B) Difficult to treat; low engagement; support; cautious use of antipsychotics for transient

C) Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

D) Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)

Answer: D

  1. Key feature regarding Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

A) Difficult to treat; low engagement; support; cautious use of antipsychotics for transient

B) Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

C) Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)

D) Pervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudges

Answer: A

  1. Key feature regarding Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal)?

A) Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

B) Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)

C) Pervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudges

D) PD: pervasive pattern (not fixed delusion); DD: non-bizarre delusion, fixed

Answer: B

SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Pervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudges
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | PD: pervasive pattern (not fixed delusion); DD: non-bizarre delusion, fixed
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Detachment from social relationships; restricted emotional expression; indifference to pra
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Schizoid: indifferent to relationships; avoidant: wants but fears relationships
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Acute discomfort with close relationships; cognitive/perceptual distortions; eccentric beh
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Schizotypal: brief/transient psychotic symptoms, reality testing preserved, egosyntonic; s
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Cluster A: subtle, chronic, ego-syntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis, deterioration
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Difficult to treat; low engagement; support; cautious use of antipsychotics for transient
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal). Which is most appropriate? | Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

Flashcards

Q: Cluster A?

A: Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)

Q: Paranoid PD?

A: Pervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudges

Q: Paranoid PD vs delusional disorder?

A: PD: pervasive pattern (not fixed delusion); DD: non-bizarre delusion, fixed

Q: Schizoid PD?

A: Detachment from social relationships; restricted emotional expression; indifference to praise/critic

Q: Schizoid vs avoidant?

A: Schizoid: indifferent to relationships; avoidant: wants but fears relationships

Q: Schizotypal PD?

A: Acute discomfort with close relationships; cognitive/perceptual distortions; eccentric behaviour

Q: Schizotypal vs schizophrenia?

A: Schizotypal: brief/transient psychotic symptoms, reality testing preserved, egosyntonic; schizophren

Q: Cluster A vs schizophrenia?

A: Cluster A: subtle, chronic, ego-syntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis, deterioration

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Cluster A
  2. B — Paranoid PD
  3. C — Paranoid PD vs delusional disorder
  4. D — Schizoid PD
  5. A — Schizoid vs avoidant
  6. B — Schizotypal PD
  7. C — Schizotypal vs schizophrenia
  8. D — Cluster A vs schizophrenia
  9. A — Treatment
  10. B — Prognosis

SBAs

  1. Cluster A
  2. Paranoid PD
  3. Paranoid PD vs delusional disorder
  4. Schizoid PD
  5. Schizoid vs avoidant
  6. Schizotypal PD

▸ 29.9.4 Cluster C Personality Disorders Avoidant Dependent

Definition

Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive) are characterised by anxiety, fear, and avoidance. They are the most common personality disorders and often present in general medical settings.

Core Clinical Content

Cluster C

Anxious/fearful; 'worried/wet'

Avoidant PD

Social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, hypersensitivity to criticism; wants but fears relationships

Avoidant vs schizoid

Avoidant: wants relationships; schizoid: doesn't want them

Avoidant vs social anxiety

PD: pervasive, ego-syntonic lifelong; SAD: episodic, ego-dystonic

Dependent PD

Pervasive need to be taken care of; submissive, clinging; fear of separation

Dependent PD risk

Higher risk of depression, abuse, somatic symptoms

Dependent vs dependent traits

PD: pervasive, rigid, maladaptive; traits: situational

OCPD

Preoccupation with order, perfectionism, control; ego-syntonic

OCPD vs OCD

OCPD: personality (egosyntonic); OCD: ego-dystonic obsessions/compulsions; often comorbid

Cluster C treatment

Psychotherapy (CBT, schema therapy, supportive); SSRIs for anxiety/depression; longer-term

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Cluster CAnxious/fearful; 'worried/wet'
Avoidant PDSocial inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, hypersensitivity to criticism; wants
Avoidant vs schizoidAvoidant: wants relationships; schizoid: doesn't want them
Avoidant vs social anxietyPD: pervasive, ego-syntonic lifelong; SAD: episodic, ego-dystonic
Dependent PDPervasive need to be taken care of; submissive, clinging; fear of separation
Dependent PD riskHigher risk of depression, abuse, somatic symptoms
Dependent vs dependent traitsPD: pervasive, rigid, maladaptive; traits: situational
OCPDPreoccupation with order, perfectionism, control; ego-syntonic
OCPD vs OCDOCPD: personality (egosyntonic); OCD: ego-dystonic obsessions/compulsions; often
Cluster C treatmentPsychotherapy (CBT, schema therapy, supportive); SSRIs for anxiety/depression; l

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive) are characterised by anxiety, fear, and avoidance. They are the most common personality disorders and often present in general medical settings.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Key features include: cluster c, avoidant pd, avoidant vs schizoid.

Q3. How is Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)

Definition

Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive) are characterised by anxiety, fear, and avoidance. They are the most common personality disorders and often present in general medical settings.

Core Clinical Content

Cluster C

Anxious/fearful; 'worried/wet'

Avoidant PD

Social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, hypersensitivity to criticism; wants but fears relationships

Avoidant vs schizoid

Avoidant: wants relationships; schizoid: doesn't want them

Avoidant vs social anxiety

PD: pervasive, ego-syntonic lifelong; SAD: episodic, ego-dystonic

Dependent PD

Pervasive need to be taken care of; submissive, clinging; fear of separation

Dependent PD risk

Higher risk of depression, abuse, somatic symptoms

Dependent vs dependent traits

PD: pervasive, rigid, maladaptive; traits: situational

OCPD

Preoccupation with order, perfectionism, control; ego-syntonic

OCPD vs OCD

OCPD: personality (egosyntonic); OCD: ego-dystonic obsessions/compulsions; often comorbid

Cluster C treatment

Psychotherapy (CBT, schema therapy, supportive); SSRIs for anxiety/depression; longer-term

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive) are characterised by anxiety, fear, and avoidance. They are the most common personality disorders and often present in general medical settings.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Key features include: cluster c, avoidant pd, avoidant vs schizoid.

Q3. How is Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • Mnemonic for Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD) — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components

  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD). Which is most appropriate? | PD: pervasive, rigid, maladaptive; traits: situational
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD). Which is most appropriate? | Preoccupation with order, perfectionism, control; ego-syntonic
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD). Which is most appropriate? | OCPD: personality (egosyntonic); OCD: ego-dystonic obsessions/compulsions; often comorbid
  1. Patient presents with features of Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD). Which is most appropriate? | Psychotherapy (CBT, schema therapy, supportive); SSRIs for anxiety/depression; longer-term

Flashcards

Q: Cluster C?

A: Anxious/fearful; 'worried/wet'

Q: Avoidant PD?

A: Social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, hypersensitivity to criticism; wants but fears relationsh

Q: Avoidant vs schizoid?

A: Avoidant: wants relationships; schizoid: doesn't want them

Q: Avoidant vs social anxiety?

A: PD: pervasive, ego-syntonic lifelong; SAD: episodic, ego-dystonic

Q: Dependent PD?

A: Pervasive need to be taken care of; submissive, clinging; fear of separation

Q: Dependent PD risk?

A: Higher risk of depression, abuse, somatic symptoms

Q: Dependent vs dependent traits?

A: PD: pervasive, rigid, maladaptive; traits: situational

Q: OCPD?

A: Preoccupation with order, perfectionism, control; ego-syntonic

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 29.9.5 Personality Disorders Overview Clusters A B C

Definition

Personality disorders are enduring, inflexible, maladaptive patterns of inner experience and behaviour that deviate from cultural expectations, causing distress or impairment. They are classified into three clusters: A (odd/eccentric), B (dramatic/erratic), and C (anxious/fearful).

Core Clinical Content

Cluster A — odd/eccentric

Paranoid, Schizoid, Schizotypal

Cluster B — dramatic/erratic

Antisocial, Borderline, Histrionic, Narcissistic

Cluster C — anxious/fearful

Avoidant, Dependent, Obsessive-compulsive

General criteria

Enduring pattern, deviates from cultural norms, pervasive, inflexibility, onset by adolescence/adulthood

Prevalence

10-13% of general population; cluster C most common

Comorbidity

High with mood, anxiety, substance use, eating disorders

Treatment

Long-term psychotherapy (DBT for BPD, mentalisation-based, schema therapy); medication for comorbidities

Prognosis

Tends to improve with age; some evidence of neuroplasticity

Personality change

Distinguish from personality disorder (temporary) vs PD (enduring)

Cluster A vs schizophrenia

PD: eccentric but reality testing preserved; schizophrenia: frank psychosis

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Cluster A — odd/eccentricParanoid, Schizoid, Schizotypal
Cluster B — dramatic/erraticAntisocial, Borderline, Histrionic, Narcissistic
Cluster C — anxious/fearfulAvoidant, Dependent, Obsessive-compulsive
General criteriaEnduring pattern, deviates from cultural norms, pervasive, inflexibility, onset
Prevalence10-13% of general population; cluster C most common
ComorbidityHigh with mood, anxiety, substance use, eating disorders
TreatmentLong-term psychotherapy (DBT for BPD, mentalisation-based, schema therapy); medi
PrognosisTends to improve with age; some evidence of neuroplasticity
Personality changeDistinguish from personality disorder (temporary) vs PD (enduring)
Cluster A vs schizophreniaPD: eccentric but reality testing preserved; schizophrenia: frank psychosis

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

Personality disorders are enduring, inflexible, maladaptive patterns of inner experience and behaviour that deviate from cultural expectations, causing distress or impairment. They are classified into three clusters: A (odd/eccentric), B (dramatic/erratic), and C (anxious/fearful).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

Key features include: cluster a — odd/eccentric, cluster b — dramatic/erratic, cluster c — anxious/fearful. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)
